chapters.description: He argues that without some sense of depth or transcendence, values become thin, and he links this loss of dimensionality to a poverty in ethics and in soulmaking.

chapters.title: Faith beyond outcomes
chapters.description: A long Hartmann passage supports his claim that one can devote oneself to an ideal even when it will not be fully realized in one’s lifetime.

chapters.title: Guilt, devotion, and healing
chapters.description: He closes by showing how guilt can be re-read as a sign of love and devotion, and how the gap between the ideal and the actual can become a source of wisdom rather than despair.

title: Sila and Soul (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What if virtue is not just a means to an end, but something beautiful in itself? The talk begins by setting Buddhist sila beside an expanded discussion of moral values and virtues, then moves into a contrast between traditions that see the good as serving liberation and a view, drawn from Hartmann, that the morally good stands on its own. From there it opens into questions of human nature, the beauty of a life shaped by virtue, and the tension between doing what is effective and doing what is right when the outcome is uncertain. Examples from Bonhoeffer, the Titanic, Plato, and climate activism keep the discussion grounded in real situations where efficacy and soul-level integrity diverge.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on sila, virtue, and moral beauty that contrasts Buddhist liberation language with the idea that the good is intrinsically valuable, not merely useful.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the talk’s focus on two ways imaginal practice can widen ethics, especially by bringing in ideas of values and virtues.

chapters.title: Values and virtues
chapters.description: He defines moral values, distinguishes them from virtues as dispositions, and begins to show why they matter beyond abstract theory.

chapters.title: Intention over result
chapters.description: The talk turns to the Buddhist emphasis on intention and disposition rather than outcome, with examples of harm, generosity, and practical wisdom.

chapters.title: Paramitas and release
chapters.description: Burbea compares virtues with the Buddhist perfections and argues that, in one traditional reading, the qualities serve liberation rather than existing as final ends.

chapters.title: Hartmann’s intrinsic good
chapters.description: He introduces Nikolai Hartmann’s claim that the morally good is good in itself and uses it to question a purely instrumental view of ethics.

chapters.title: Beauty, language, and soul
chapters.description: The discussion widens into etymology, the beauty of virtue, and the sense that virtues can be cultivated as part of a beautiful life and a fuller humanity.

chapters.title: Modern shifts in virtue
chapters.description: Using Paul Kugler, Jung, and MacIntyre, Burbea explores how language and modern moral thought have changed the way virtue is understood.

chapters.title: Virtue over efficacy
chapters.description: Bonhoeffer, the Titanic, and climate change illustrate cases where doing the right thing may matter even when results are limited or unclear.

chapters.title: Double intention
chapters.description: In the closing movement, Burbea questions Hartmann’s sharp divide between concern for the other and concern for one’s own moral being, suggesting a self-reflexive dimension in ethical action.

title: Sila and Soul (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What happens when a Dharma path that emphasizes suffering reduction, gentleness, and detachment meets climate collapse, social justice, and the pull of conscience? The talk moves through psychotherapy, Buddhism, and imaginal practice, showing how familiar frameworks can miss ethical force, grief, wrath, beauty, and the need to act. Along the way, the speaker uses stories of a second house, a monastery that would not recycle, and a Black Madonna image to ask what really shapes our choices and what our traditions leave out.
short_summary: An extended inquiry into how psychotherapy and Buddhadharma can narrow ethics, and how imaginal practice may open a wider response to climate, justice, and conscience.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Rob sets up the talk as an exploration of how ethics, soulmaking, and common development frameworks relate to each other.

chapters.title: Psychotherapy and Dharma
chapters.description: He compares psychotherapy and Buddhadharma at their best and at their limits, especially around self-preoccupation, ethics, and compassion.

chapters.title: Frameworks and blind spots
chapters.description: He emphasizes that every conceptual framework casts a shadow and can obscure important moral realities.

chapters.title: An imaginal misreading
chapters.description: A student’s therapy story about a monk image becomes an example of how imaginal material can be mistaken for denial instead of ethical calling.

chapters.title: Conscience and tradition
chapters.description: Rob asks what happens when conscience is absorbed into inner-critic language, and why living traditions need critique, dialogue, and care.

chapters.title: Ethics, time, and future
chapters.description: He turns to shadow, Kierkegaard, and the aesthetic-versus-ethical distinction to argue that present-moment spirituality can miss future commitment.

chapters.title: Dharma and moral courage
chapters.description: Rob questions apolitical Dharma, spiritual bypassing, and a dominant ethos of softness that may avoid necessary judgment or challenge.

chapters.title: Climate and responsibility
chapters.description: The talk deepens into climate ethics, fair share, and the difficulty of taking personal responsibility when harmful habits have been normalized.

chapters.title: Imagining the suffering
chapters.description: He uses imagined conversations with affected people and a critique of Four Noble Truths rhetoric to make ecological harm feel more real and morally immediate.

title: Sila and Soul (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: Ethics begins here as an open, unfinished question: how do sila, soulmaking Dharma, and modern life meet when morality is no longer held together by a single shared framework? The talk moves from the Buddha’s skillful/unskillful framing and the five precepts, through the West’s inheritance of guilt, individualism, and inner criticism, into a wider critique of modernity, postmodern relativism, law, democracy, globalization, and the loss of moral education. It then turns toward the possibility that imaginal practice, reverence, and a fuller sense of soul may deepen ethical life rather than stand apart from it, while also widening the range of what moral care can look like.
short_summary: A wide-ranging introduction to sila that traces ethics from Buddhist training into modern cultural confusion, then asks how soulmaking and imaginal practice might renew moral life.

chapters.title: Opening the subject
chapters.description: Burbea introduces ethics as an endless field of inquiry and says the talk will only be an incomplete beginning.

chapters.title: Buddhist and Western frames
chapters.description: He contrasts the Buddhist framing of skillful and unskillful action with Abrahamic good-and-evil morality and the effect of self-judgment.

chapters.title: Precepts and simplification
chapters.description: He explains how sila and the five precepts are meant to calm and simplify the mind rather than intensify moral worry.

chapters.title: Culture, guilt, and character
chapters.description: Using Thich Nhat Hanh and then wider cultural analysis, he traces the mix of inherited morality, individualism, inner critic, and Dharma archetypes.

chapters.title: MacIntyre and modernity
chapters.description: Drawing on Alasdair MacIntyre, he describes the narrowing and fragmentation of morality through modern philosophy, law, and postmodern relativism.

chapters.title: Rorty, democracy, and power
chapters.description: He critiques Richard Rorty’s pragmatist emphasis on conversation, then examines democracy, manipulation, and the instability of shared values.

chapters.title: Global ethics and moral education
chapters.description: Burbea turns to globalization, capitalism, climate, and the loss of moral education, arguing that ethics now has global consequences and needs rethinking.

chapters.title: Vinaya and meditation
chapters.description: He uses vinaya as an example of moral education in the Buddhist tradition and links ethics directly to the settling needed for jhana.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and moral life
chapters.description: He closes by showing how imaginal practice, values, and the polytheistic psyche can enrich ethical life while still leaving it complex and conflicted.

title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What if Dharma is not a single universal truth, but one framework among others, shaped by history, culture, and soul style? The talk moves from a critique of modern and postmodern ways of thinking into a careful questioning of Buddhist universals, then into the claim that Soulmaking Dharma can be understood either as inside Buddha Dharma or as a larger movement that includes it. Along the way, it weighs eclectic borrowing, feminism, colonialism, and the limits of conceptual frameworks, while returning again and again to the idea that teachings may need to stay open, elastic, and ready to break and be rebuilt.
short_summary: A wide-ranging inquiry into whether Dharma is universal or culturally shaped, and whether Soulmaking Dharma belongs within Buddhism or reframes it from a larger field.

chapters.title: Cultural context
chapters.description: Burbea begins by tracing the clash between modern and postmodern ways of thinking and how that tension colors contemporary Dharma.

chapters.title: What counts as universal
chapters.description: He asks whether Dharma claims, especially the Four Noble Truths, are being treated as universal in a way that should be examined more carefully.

chapters.title: Gender and structure
chapters.description: The talk turns to the Pali Canon, renunciation, patriarchy, and whether some Buddhist styles reflect a strongly male-shaped inheritance.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and plurality
chapters.description: Burbea describes Soulmaking Dharma as elastic and self-aware, then distinguishes it from superficial eclecticism and perennialist mixing.

chapters.title: Borrowing across traditions
chapters.description: He considers the use of the Old Testament, Jesus, Rumi, Kuan Yin, and even missionary stories to ask what is really being borrowed and what remains distinct.

chapters.title: Postmodern limits
chapters.description: The discussion widens to the limits of postmodernism, Richard Rorty, and the need for practice that can actually shift perception rather than merely critique ideas.

chapters.title: Dharma as art
chapters.description: He proposes Dharma, especially Soulmaking Dharma, as an art with poetic and imaginal truth, irreducible purpose, and a third kind of knowing.

chapters.title: Culture and spirit
chapters.description: Burbea reflects on how views arise from historical and cultural contexts, bringing in Hegel while rejecting any closed or final philosophy of spirit.

chapters.title: Two ways to place it
chapters.description: He sketches different ways to locate Soulmaking Dharma in relation to mainstream Buddhism, ending with the idea that the relationship may be seen from either side.

title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: How do culture, personality, and worldview quietly shape what we think Dharma is? The talk moves through several contrasting models of awakening and emptiness, showing how each one carries different assumptions about the world, the human being, and what practice is for. It also asks whether images, sacredness, and beauty are provisional stepping stones or essential parts of the path, and ends by returning to the need for honesty, autonomy, and careful inquiry in relation to authority.
short_summary: A wide-ranging inquiry into how cosmology, emptiness, and personal inclination shape different Dharmas and different ideas of awakening.

chapters.title: What Shapes Tradition
chapters.description: He recaps the earlier thread: what a healthy tradition needs, and how culture, error, conflict, and tension all participate in shaping it.

chapters.title: Personal Inclinations Matter
chapters.description: The talk turns to the human side of choice, including soul style, preference, fear, and how these silently steer which Dharma people notice and adopt.

chapters.title: Four Worldviews
chapters.description: Rob outlines four broad models—physicalist, transcendent, all-one, and soulmaking—and shows how each one frames suffering, awakening, and what counts as real.

chapters.title: Awakening Through Models
chapters.description: He keeps unpacking the implications of those cosmologies, especially for ethics, the meaning of practice, and the different kinds of human being they produce.

chapters.title: Images as Stepping Stones
chapters.description: Using a conversation about philosophers and Winnicott's transitional object, he examines the view that images are only provisional and contrasts it with a soulmaking reading of the imaginal.

chapters.title: Practice and Neuroscience
chapters.description: Rob considers a modern physicalist account of awakening as neurological change, and asks what that leaves out about emptiness, sacredness, and the range of human possibility.

chapters.title: Many Meanings of Emptiness
chapters.description: He surveys different Tibetan and modern construals of emptiness, from protecting conventional reality to using emptiness as an anti-spiritual or anti-sacred stance.

chapters.title: Deepening the View
chapters.description: Drawing on Nagarjuna, he argues that emptiness is deeper than impermanence or process, and that a deeper understanding can open both soul and imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Honesty, Authority, and Inquiry
chapters.description: The talk ends by returning to the need for honesty about what we really want, the role of beauty, and the balance between autonomy and authority in a living tradition.

title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What makes a tradition alive: shared texts, practices, values, and authority, or the tension between all of these and the freedom to differ? The talk moves through mindfulness, the Buddhist canon, and then wide-ranging comparisons with Judaism, Christianity, and philosophy to show how traditions are shaped by culture, interpretation, and even productive misunderstanding. It keeps returning to a harder question: when does difference enrich a living lineage, and when does it become a fracture or a counterfeit teaching?
short_summary: A close look at what sustains a living tradition, from canonical authority and interpretation to cultural influence, conflict, and creative mistake.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Rob frames the talk as an invitation to reflect on tradition with mind, heart, and soul rather than rush to conclusions.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and tradition
chapters.description: He uses Stephen Batchelor's Bodhi College example to contrast mindfulness as technique with a deeper relationship to history, teachings, and belonging.

chapters.title: What traditions share
chapters.description: The talk lays out the shared elements that may make a living tradition coherent: community, values, practices, stories, vocabulary, and beliefs.

chapters.title: Mystery and canonical depth
chapters.description: Rob turns to the Zohar, tantric texts, and John's Gospel to suggest that opacity, poetry, and unresolved depth can nourish eros and commentary.

chapters.title: Tradition as exchange
chapters.description: He broadens the frame to historical borrowing, showing how Judaism, Christianity, Islam, and modern Buddhism have all been shaped by outside influences.

chapters.title: Cultural conditioning
chapters.description: The talk examines how even core Buddhist schemes, such as the three fires, dependent origination, and the aggregates, may reflect the culture they emerged in.

chapters.title: Mindfulness revised
chapters.description: Rob returns to contemporary mindfulness and argues that popular presentations often truncate the Satipatthana Sutta and remove its active, discerning edge.

chapters.title: Origins and history
chapters.description: He questions the fantasy of origins, bringing in Nietzsche, Vattimo, and Howard Zinn to suggest that history is always shaped by perspective and selection.

chapters.title: Conflict as vitality
chapters.description: Drawing on MacIntyre and Whitehead, Rob argues that healthy traditions often contain internal argument, epistemological tension, and creative friction.

title: The Phoenix of Metaphysics (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What if the urge to know reality, rather than collapse into certainty or shrug into skepticism, opens into a wider middle way? The talk moves from critiques of classical science and positivism into emptiness, imaginal practice, and the claim that concepts can be fruitful even when they are unproven or perhaps untrue. Along the way it brings in physics, metaphysics, and figures like Rorty, Yeats, Corbin, Smolin, and Goethe to ask whether inquiry can stay rigorous without becoming rigid. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from proving final truth to letting eros, beauty, and conceptual frameworks support soulmaking.
short_summary: A long, reflective inquiry into epistemology, metaphysics, and soulmaking that argues for a middle way between scientistic certainty and empty shrugging.

chapters.title: Framing the problem
chapters.description: Burbea recaps the talk's central questions about knowledge, reality, and the scientific worldview, then introduces the tensions that will structure the discussion.

chapters.title: Shrugging and default views
chapters.description: Using Richard Rorty as an example, he describes the common move of refusing ontology altogether and falling back into inherited assumptions.

chapters.title: Imaginal middle way
chapters.description: He turns to Yeats and Corbin to outline an imaginal way of seeing that keeps sacredness, poetic meaning, and multiple levels of interpretation alive.

chapters.title: Sacredness in emptiness
chapters.description: Burbea deepens the discussion of emptiness, subject-object participation, and the way sacredness can reappear through a subtler understanding of fabrication and perception.

chapters.title: Higher truths and physics
chapters.description: He moves into examples from philosophy and physics, using other dimensions, mystical absolutes, and quantum theory to show how models can try to integrate paradox.

chapters.title: Metaphysics in science
chapters.description: The talk traces the decline of positivism and the continued role of beauty, intuition, and speculative models in scientific discovery.

chapters.title: Fruitful falsehoods
chapters.description: Burbea argues that untrue or unproven ideas can still be generative, especially when they function as scaffolding for practice, imagination, and soulmaking.

chapters.title: Beauty as cause
chapters.description: Through pseudo-Dionysus and later physics analogies, he develops the idea that beauty, correspondence, and duality can organize experience without collapsing it into a single fixed truth.

chapters.title: Inquiry as soulmaking
chapters.description: The talk closes with Goethe, Cohen, and a final return to epistemology: the point is not to solve everything, but to keep inquiry alive in ways that nourish the soul.

title: The Phoenix of Metaphysics (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What if the question of what is real is also a question of what a culture learns to respect? The talk moves from ontology and epistemology into the history of modern science, using Galileo, Descartes, Newton, quantum physics, and later philosophy of science to show how the idea of an observer-independent world both rose to dominance and then fractured under closer scrutiny. From there it turns toward the practical stakes for emptiness and sensing the soul: how a way of looking shapes not only knowledge, but ethics, imagination, and the kinds of life a person can trust. It ends by sketching an imaginal middle way, where rigor and openness are held together without collapsing into either literalism or vagueness.
short_summary: A wide-ranging inquiry into how scientific worldviews, philosophical critiques, and Buddhist emptiness unsettle assumptions about reality, knowledge, and the imaginal middle way.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: He frames ontology and epistemology as the twin questions behind metaphysics and explains why they matter for practice and culture.

chapters.title: Knowledge and social order
chapters.description: Using Moscovici and Foucault, he argues that what a culture counts as real is tied to respect, hierarchy, and power.

chapters.title: Classical worldview
chapters.description: He sketches the scientific revolution and the mechanistic, clockwork picture associated with Galileo, Descartes, Locke, and Newton.

chapters.title: Quantum fracture
chapters.description: Wave-particle duality, superposition, and the observer’s role begin to break the old model of fixed, independently existing things.

chapters.title: What science reveals
chapters.description: With Victor Mansfield and related physics, he explores how observation, measurement, and the past itself become less fixed than common sense assumes.

chapters.title: Paradigms and truth
chapters.description: He turns to Kuhn, Marshall Walker, Laudan, and Lakatos to question whether science is moving toward truth or just solving problems within paradigms.

chapters.title: Truth, models, and realism
chapters.description: The talk develops distinctions between correspondence and coherence, then shows how anti-realist claims often smuggle realism back in.

chapters.title: Deconstruction and assumptions
chapters.description: He follows deconstruction, Descartes, Peirce, and Newton to show how hidden assumptions and motivations shape even claims to objectivity.

chapters.title: Epistemology as practice
chapters.description: He brings the discussion back to Buddhist epistemology, Yeats, Corbin, and the imaginal middle way, asking how soulmaking perceptions can be trusted and lived.

title: Soulmaking Dyad Practice
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: How do you keep a relational practice from slipping into confusion, craving, or collapse? The talk moves from basic dyad exercises—balance of attention, metta, energy body awareness, and the sense of a temenos—to the more delicate terrain of image, eros, and the ways a shared field can either open or shut down. Along the way, it keeps returning to small but decisive questions: what is happening in me, what is happening between us, and what level of practice is actually appropriate right now?
short_summary: A detailed guide to soulmaking dyad practice, moving from foundational exercises and boundaries into the subtleties of image, eros, and the shared relational field.

chapters.title: Opening the practice
chapters.description: Rob introduces the talk as practical guidance for working in soulmaking dyads and names it as a large subject that needs careful handling.

chapters.title: Early exercises and scales
chapters.description: He revisits earlier dyad and temenos exercises, especially balance of attention and the three-part check-in with energy body, emotion, and image.

chapters.title: Why dyads are sensitive
chapters.description: The talk turns to the potency of relational practice, the ease with which it can spark beauty or contraction, and the need for real skill.

chapters.title: Prerequisites for practice
chapters.description: Rob lists the capacities needed for this work, including emotional awareness, mindfulness, energy body skill, and clarity of intention.

chapters.title: Not rushing ahead
chapters.description: He encourages listeners to pitch the practice appropriately, and to value basic attention training and simple relational presence before more advanced imaginal work.

chapters.title: Attention, gaze, and metta
chapters.description: He describes specific ways to work with attention, including noticing habitual patterns, relaxing the gaze, and widening the field through metta.

chapters.title: Temenos as container
chapters.description: Rob explains temenos as the sacred, protective space of the practice, shaped by posture, formality, metta, intention, and careful reception.

chapters.title: After the encounter
chapters.description: He extends the idea into the "temenos tail," where the effects of a dyad continue after the formal session and need thoughtful care.

chapters.title: Shared framework and stages
chapters.description: The talk returns to earlier exercises and emphasizes that soulmaking dyads need shared ontological commitments and a common conceptual frame.

title: Logoi and Leanings
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: How do you steer when practice opens a fork: move toward the calm of samadhi, or stay with the imaginal depth and its soulmaking pull? The talk moves through navigation, pacing, and the subtle cues of the energy body, then widens into the role of logoi, conceptual frameworks, and how they can either limit or release what practice can become. Near the end, the speaker turns to symbolic time, causality, and a reading of experience that is less flat and more open to multiple dimensions.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of how to navigate between samadhi, imaginal practice, and the conceptual frameworks that shape what those practices reveal.

chapters.title: Opening the navigation
chapters.description: Rob introduces the talk as a set of pointers about steering in imaginal practice and between practices.

chapters.title: Subtle shifts in practice
chapters.description: He describes the art of moving between practices, comparing it to leaning a hawk, bike, or boat rather than making abrupt moves.

chapters.title: The samadhi fork
chapters.description: He contrasts wide awareness with microscopic attention, then develops the major fork between following pleasant feelings into samadhi or staying with the image and its wider soulmaking possibilities.

chapters.title: Pacing and balance
chapters.description: He explains that eros and practice have rhythms, sometimes needing slowing, stability, or counterbalance so experience can be digested rather than rushed.

chapters.title: Logos and causality
chapters.description: He widens the discussion to logos, showing how different causal stories and perceptions of time shape what is possible in practice.

chapters.title: Energy body and ideas
chapters.description: He returns to the fountain image and argues that both bodily resonance and the underlying ideas of practice are necessary for soulmaking to open fully.

chapters.title: Present and future causes
chapters.description: He contrasts past-cause explanations with present-moment dependent origination and a teleological sense of being called by the future or the angel out ahead.

chapters.title: Water, time, and scripture
chapters.description: Using Corbin and the Moses story, he reads water as time and argues against flattening sacred stories into only literal history.

chapters.title: Concepts as support
chapters.description: He insists that imaginal and emptiness experiences need a supporting conceptual framework, and illustrates how a missing or limiting framework can make practice stagnate.

title: The Invitation of Otherness (Autonomy, Eros, and Intentionality)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What does it mean for an image to be other, and yet intimate, autonomous, and responsive? The talk moves from two-ness and erotic tension into the autonomy of the self and the image, then into a careful distinction between narrowing practice around getting something and opening to what the image itself wants. Along the way, it turns to desire as part of self-knowledge, and to the way a fuller intention can let eros, healing, and soul-making unfold together rather than cancel each other out.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of imaginal relationship, autonomy, and eros, with concrete teaching stories showing how desire can be met without narrowing the practice.

chapters.title: Imaginal relationship
chapters.description: He begins by situating the talk in earlier teaching on relationship with images and explains that the imaginal is less a thing than a relationship.

chapters.title: Two-ness and otherness
chapters.description: He develops the role of two-ness, showing how eros depends on difference and how otherness can deepen without becoming alienation.

chapters.title: Autonomy of the image
chapters.description: He turns to autonomy, explaining that the imaginal figure can feel both mine and not mine, with its own intelligence and will.

chapters.title: Essence and anchoring
chapters.description: Using Aslan and other examples, he shows how an image can open into essence, dimensionality, and a stable sense of being rooted without being frozen.

chapters.title: Autonomy of self
chapters.description: He clarifies that imaginal practice preserves the self’s freedom to choose, pause, refuse, or re-enter contact, even when the image feels powerful or demanding.

chapters.title: Eros and wisdom
chapters.description: Through examples of goddess, sexual energy, and divine nourishment, he shows how eros can be held skillfully without being reduced to possession or urgency.

chapters.title: Being loved back
chapters.description: He expands autonomy into reciprocity, drawing on anteros to show that the imaginal other may also return eros and that being received can be healing.

chapters.title: Desire and self-knowledge
chapters.description: He widens the discussion to desire itself, distinguishing culturally conditioned wishes from authentic soul desire and showing why knowing oneself includes knowing desire.

chapters.title: Fullness of intention
chapters.description: He closes by contrasting a narrow healing agenda with a fuller intention for soul-making, then uses a teaching story to show how eros can soften, become divine, and deepen into ensouling.

title: Dimensionality, Humility, Duty, and Love
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What changes when an image is not just something seen, but a presence that can be noticed, ignited, and answered? The talk moves from the practical point of the imaginal elements—how they help discern what is genuinely imaginal and how they can be worked with in practice—into revisions on twoness, particularity, and the danger of reducing an image to one meaning. From there it widens into dimensionality, humility, duty, prayer, and love, including a long reflection on how imaginal love differs from metta and how it can reveal the ideal or deepest dimension of a person.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of the imaginal elements, moving from discernment and practice into dimensionality, humility, duty, prayer, and a love that sees through the empirical surface to the ideal.

chapters.title: Purpose of the lattice
chapters.description: He explains that the imaginal elements are meant both for discernment and for active practice, where noticing or tweaking an element can ignite others.

chapters.title: Two revisions
chapters.description: He offers two corrections to the list: images should not be reduced to one meaning, and twoness means retaining particularity rather than dissolving into oneness.

chapters.title: Dimensionality and depth
chapters.description: He develops dimensionality as depth and other-level being, using Corbin to suggest that a person can be a refraction or emanation of an angel.

chapters.title: Anchors in depth
chapters.description: He argues that imaginal depth matters because it gives the soul a different kind of anchor than ordinary solidity, and can include cosmic love and awareness.

chapters.title: Humility and self
chapters.description: He redefines humility as a relation to mystery, love, and divinity, not self-denigration, and ties it to a lighter, less fabricated sense of self.

chapters.title: Prayer and desire
chapters.description: He shows how prayer can increase separation if desire hardens the self, then suggests a subtler understanding in which personal desire participates in divine desire.

chapters.title: Duty and rightness
chapters.description: He explores how the rightness of an image can carry a felt duty, sometimes through difficult or stark figures, and how necessity may be woven into imaginal life.

chapters.title: Particular love
chapters.description: He contrasts metta with the particular, demanding love of imaginal figures, then uses Hartmann to describe love as seeing the ideal or highest possibility in another.

chapters.title: Love as knowing
chapters.description: He closes by emphasizing that imaginal love is also a mode of knowing: it sees through the empirical surface, recognizes the ideal, and can guide and anchor a life.

title: Daimon, Refracted
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What if deep emptiness does not flatten the self, but opens a wider range of selves that can be played with, inhabited, and seen as empty? The talk moves from Nagarjuna and the Buddha’s middle way into a long exploration of imaginal practice: a fountain of soul flowing into self, other, and world; a wounded blackbird that becomes an image; a queen in a blood-trailing robe; and a naked goddess in a tree. From there it turns to the inner critic, desire, imprinting, Corbin’s angel out ahead, the sefirot, and a short guided meditation on metta and the voice as a way of letting the self become more dimensional and soul-suffused.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on emptiness and Soulmaking that explores how the self can become imaginal, balanced, and more alive through image, eros, and embodied practice.

chapters.title: Emptiness and self-views
chapters.description: Burbea begins by showing how a radical understanding of emptiness changes the way selfhood can be understood.

chapters.title: Middle way, not nihilism
chapters.description: He brings in Nagarjuna and the Buddha’s middle way to clarify that emptiness does not mean blankness or the disappearance of all self-conceptions.

chapters.title: Soul’s balance and spread
chapters.description: Using the fountain analogy, he explains how soulmaking needs a balance across self, other, and world rather than an over-focused enchantment in one direction.

chapters.title: Desire as entry point
chapters.description: He turns to desire and eros, suggesting that what looks like dukkha or self-contraction can become a doorway into imaginal practice.

chapters.title: The wounded blackbird
chapters.description: A retreat story shows how care, compassion, and loss can settle into an image that becomes more vivid, less proliferating, and more healing.

chapters.title: Images as shared qualities
chapters.description: He develops several examples, including the blood-trailing queen and the naked goddess, to show how images can reveal qualities, mirror the self, and preserve a light but real two-ness.

chapters.title: Imprint, genius, and soul
chapters.description: A concern about wanting to make an imprint leads into a broader argument that soul wants to touch and be touched, and that creativity may be more than ego-display.

chapters.title: Corbin, Souriau, and angels
chapters.description: Burbea uses Oscar Wilde, Stravinsky, Corbin, Souriau, and Hillman to widen the framework into imaginal persons, the angel of the work, and a more polytheistic psyche.

chapters.title: The inner critic
chapters.description: He gives several ways to work with the inner critic, including feeling its charge, imagining its destruction, and finding the eros underneath its tightening.

title: "Until All is Liquid" (Emptiness and Sensing with Soul)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: How does loosening perception without losing form make imaginal practice possible? The talk begins with an alchemical maxim—wait until all is liquid—and uses it to trace a middle territory where things are less fabricated but not yet vanished. From there it moves through emptiness as a support for soulmaking, the danger of turning practice into a “should,” and the shift from striving for more fading to curiosity, ease, and insight into dependent arising. Later it turns to examples from illness, lineage devotion, and prayer, showing how even modest emptiness can deepen image, self-sense, and a sense of sacredness without flattening them.
short_summary: An exploration of how emptiness, fabrication, and ways of looking can loosen perception enough to support imaginal and soulmaking practice.

chapters.title: Until All Is Liquid
chapters.description: Rob opens with the alchemical maxim and uses it to describe the loosening of perception needed for imaginal practice.

chapters.title: When Emptiness Helps
chapters.description: He explains why some practitioners need emptiness as a basis for soulmaking, while others may not, and why eros matters more than obligation.

chapters.title: Curiosity Over Shoulding
chapters.description: The talk turns to emptiness as a journey, with emphasis on curiosity, ease, and the shift away from striving for more fading.

chapters.title: Partial Fading, Real Insight
chapters.description: Rob argues that even incomplete fading can reveal clinging, dependent origination, and the constructed nature of perception.

chapters.title: Ways of Looking
chapters.description: He develops the central idea that perception is shaped by ways of looking, and that these ways can become imaginal rather than merely philosophical.

chapters.title: Empty Yet Soulful
chapters.description: A detailed example from illness shows how a simple 'just appearances' practice can support stamina, and how appearance can be transparent rather than dismissive.

chapters.title: Parallels Across Traditions
chapters.description: Rob briefly brings in Corbin, tantric practice, Schneur Zalman, and tummo to show that emptiness, eros, and image are woven together in other lineages too.

chapters.title: Emptiness in Daily Practice
chapters.description: He returns to the practical scale of the work, showing how even small degrees of emptiness can open prayerfulness, love, and imaginal depth.

chapters.title: Middle Ways and Flexibility
chapters.description: The closing section distinguishes the imaginal middle way from the emptiness middle way and emphasizes that the right degree of reification varies with person and situation.

title: Heat and the Material
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: How do emotions become a crucible rather than something to get rid of? The talk traces a careful path between ordinary mindfulness, which can quiet and deconstruct feeling, and soulmaking practice, where emotion is held, enlarged, and sometimes transformed into image, eros, and a more vivid sense of self. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to caveats: not every feeling has one cause, not every person needs more heat, and sometimes the way forward begins by noticing what has been shut down, narrowed, or taken for granted.
short_summary: A close exploration of how emotion, image, and soulmaking interact, with practical cautions about when to soften, when to open, and when feeling itself can become imaginal.

chapters.title: Opening cautions
chapters.description: He begins by framing the talk around emotions in soulmaking practice and warning against simple prescriptions about healthy psychology.

chapters.title: Cultural assumptions
chapters.description: He questions contemporary and historical assumptions about emotion, using the idea of passionate love to show how easily people are pathologized or misunderstood.

chapters.title: Emotional style and skill
chapters.description: He distinguishes different temperaments and points out that some people need help riding the force of their emotions rather than suppressing them.

chapters.title: The crucible of emotion
chapters.description: He develops the alchemical image of a vessel that can hold heat, describing how emotions may need to be felt and respected so they can ferment into images.

chapters.title: From mindfulness to image
chapters.description: He contrasts standard mindfulness, which tends toward pacification and less fabrication, with the imaginal mode where emotion becomes transformed rather than simply reduced.

chapters.title: Stretch and mutual dependence
chapters.description: He argues that soulmaking and stretch arise together: opening, breaking, and difficulty can bring forth images, and images can in turn create more stretch.

chapters.title: Images and emotion
chapters.description: He sets out a tripartite contrast between fixated imagery, mindfulness deconstruction, and imaginal practice, then explains how the emotional field changes in each case.

chapters.title: Wide fields of cause
chapters.description: He invites listeners to broaden the field around emotion, including narrative, values, trust, love, beauty, and other possible causes beyond family history.

chapters.title: Practice examples
chapters.description: He offers a detailed guided-style example involving a mule image and shows how attention to the body, power, integrity, and self-sense can unlock a more living imaginal response.

title: Opening the Sense of the Energy Body
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What happens if the energy body is not a fixed thing but a way of sensing that can be opened, widened, and even re-imagined? The talk moves from a practical definition of energy body awareness—felt sense, image, and idea—into a series of adjustments: experimenting rather than fixating, bringing the whole body into contact with the object or other, and noticing how the energy field can become an organ of perception with a more synesthetic quality. It then connects this opening to breath, generosity, devotion, samadhi, and dyadic practice, including the possibility that a shift in one person’s perception can change the whole field.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of energy body awareness, showing how felt sense, image, and idea can open into imaginal perception, relationship, and practice.

chapters.title: Framing the inquiry
chapters.description: Rob situates the talk within a larger series on sensing the soul of practice and introduces the energy body as this session's focus.

chapters.title: Three aspects of energy
chapters.description: He defines the energy body through felt sense, image, and idea, emphasizing that they overlap but that felt sense is experientially primary.

chapters.title: Experimenting with form
chapters.description: Using the story of a student over-literalizing a visualization, he points to the need to test instructions by their effects rather than by visual detail alone.

chapters.title: A nose for essentials
chapters.description: He broadens the point into a general Dharma skill: developing discernment for what is crucial, what is secondary, and what actually bears fruit.

chapters.title: Whole-body contact
chapters.description: Rob explains how to bring the whole energy body into contact with an object or other person, including the idea that the body itself can become an organ of perception.

chapters.title: Opening the field
chapters.description: He describes the range of subtle-body experience, the limits imposed by cultural assumptions, and the way relationship can become a shared energetic field.

chapters.title: Breath, generosity, and opening
chapters.description: He turns to the Anapanasati Sutta and then to generosity, arguing that long breath, openness, and radical giving can all open the energy body and support samadhi and freedom.

chapters.title: Capacity and path
chapters.description: The talk shifts to trainable habits, soul styles, devotion, and the possibility that different Dharma paths fit different capacities and openings.

chapters.title: Energy as imaginal
chapters.description: Rob concludes by showing how the energy body can be sensed as imaginal or poetic, with examples from dyad practice, eros, and the subtle interdependence of experience between two people.

title: The Crucible
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What kind of vessel can hold an image long enough for it to ferment into something soul-making? The talk moves through that question by way of emotion, vulnerability, and the difference between forcing an image and creating the conditions for it to arrive. It returns again and again to the practical art of receptivity: letting the body lead, staying with what feels truly soulful, and allowing a memory, idea, or encounter to deepen without being flattened or rushed.
short_summary: A quiet, detailed exploration of how images, emotions, and attitudes become a vessel for soul-making practice.

chapters.title: The crucible
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the alchemy metaphor and says he wants to clarify the conditions that allow imaginal practice to work.

chapters.title: Emotions and sustainment
chapters.description: He explains why emotions matter as part of the vessel, and why neither bypassing nor being overwhelmed helps the imaginal process.

chapters.title: Vulnerability in practice
chapters.description: He explores vulnerability, including fear of possession, merging, withdrawal, craving, and the wish for an image or duty to be seen.

chapters.title: Mary and Jacob
chapters.description: Using Van Gogh, the Annunciation, Zoharic teaching, and Jacob wrestling the angel, he shows wound, blessing, and divine dependence as part of soulmaking.

chapters.title: Images from life and art
chapters.description: He turns to how memories and artworks become imaginal, including the hospital kiss and the movie image that echoed his illness and purpose.

chapters.title: How images are received
chapters.description: Burbea emphasizes opportunism, research, foundations, deliberate ideas, and humility when images are not yet arising or are hard to sustain.

chapters.title: Art and grace
chapters.description: He stresses that images cannot be mastered, only invited, and gives examples of letting the body lead, being loved, and seeing life from beyond death.

chapters.title: Finding the eros
chapters.description: He discusses tuning to what is soul-making in an image, using examples from Schiele and Bach to show that eros is broader than sexuality.

chapters.title: Series, patience, and expression
chapters.description: He describes images arriving in sequences, the role of pacing, and cautions about writing, painting, or moving an image too soon.

title: The Way of Image
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. As well as addressing and inquiring into common Dharma themes such as emptiness, ethics, Awakening, and tradition, they attempt to clarify or explore further various aspects and implications of some of the Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, including their bearing on some of those common Dharma themes. PLEASE NOTE: Although not all of it, much of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended when there is already some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of Soulmaking Dharma, in addition to a good working familiarity with Insight Meditation.
podcast_description: What counts as an image, and when does it become genuinely imaginal? The talk starts by laying out the foundations of Soulmaking Dharma: how ordinary Dharma capacities like mindfulness, metta, samatha, and emotional skill can become roots in their own right, and how clear definitions matter without freezing the practice into something rigid. From there it moves into distinctions that protect the imaginal from being confused with shamanism, energy healing, or ESP, then opens out into many examples of non-visual imagery: compassion sensed through chant, music as a god with a claim on life, a dream of dying soon, a chest opening like a Torah ark, and angelic ministration at the threshold of death.
short_summary: An introduction to imaginal practice that traces how ordinary Dharma becomes ensouled, distinguishes the imaginal from adjacent traditions, and illustrates it through vivid non-visual examples.

chapters.title: Opening frame
chapters.description: Rob sets out the aims of the series and explains that this talk will introduce the terrain of imaginal practice, its roots, and its relation to broader Dharma.

chapters.title: Foundations become roots
chapters.description: He shows how ordinary Dharma capacities like mindfulness, metta, samatha, and emotional skill are not only prerequisites but can be drawn into soulmaking themselves.

chapters.title: What soul means
chapters.description: Rob offers a working definition of soul as the part that senses in soul-making ways and wants soul-making, while holding the definition lightly enough for it to grow.

chapters.title: Dyad and boundaries
chapters.description: The talk expands from individual practice to the idea of a 'soul of the dyad,' then carefully distinguishes imaginal practice from shamanism, channeling, energy healing, and related fields.

chapters.title: Imaginal middle way
chapters.description: He clarifies that image and imaginal are not just private inner pictures, and introduces the middle way between literal belief and dismissal.

chapters.title: Three kinds of image
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes fixated images, mindful observation of images, and images that become genuinely imaginal, then gives examples showing that image is not necessarily visual.

chapters.title: Music as a god
chapters.description: A long personal example shows grief about leaving music deepen into reverence, surrender, and a sense of music as an autonomous, soul-level presence.

chapters.title: Death, sacredness, land
chapters.description: He turns to dream fragments, an imaginal sense of dying, and a car ride in which the chest, the Torah ark, life and death, and the land itself take on sacred resonance.

chapters.title: Soft edges and refinement
chapters.description: The closing section returns to non-visual compassion and angels, then broadens into teaching about spectra, dimmer switches, trust, refining view, and the need for lightness around labels.

title: Closing Comments From Rob - Soulmaking and Sangha
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: The talks, instructions, and guided meditations in this set are from a retreat, led by Catherine McGee and Rob Burbea, for practitioners already familiar with Soulmaking Dharma. The teachers strongly recommend that you also have an understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice, before listening. Without this background in practice it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you leave a retreat where the heart has been opened, without spilling what feels precious? The talk moves from simple thanks and practical guidance for the days after retreat into a careful warning about sensitivity, enthusiasm, and the need to check the moment and the listener before sharing what has been touched. From there it turns toward the deeper question of how Soulmaking Dharma is meant to be taken up: as something to digest fully, develop, and only then weave into one’s own practice, in solitude and in Sangha.
short_summary: A closing talk on leaving retreat carefully, assimilating Soulmaking Dharma deeply, and treating practice and Sangha as conditions that need ongoing care.

chapters.title: Opening Thanks
chapters.description: Rob begins with gratitude and a brief sense of not yet knowing exactly what to say.

chapters.title: Leaving Retreat Carefully
chapters.description: He offers practical counsel for transitioning out of retreat, especially the heart’s sensitivity and the need for rest, space, or solitude.

chapters.title: Sharing What Is Precious
chapters.description: Rob warns that soul-touching experience cannot always be shared immediately and encourages checking the moment, the listener, and the conditions.

chapters.title: The Gift of Dana
chapters.description: He reflects on the moving dana ceremony and on how imaginal and soulmaking practice once felt difficult to speak about publicly.

chapters.title: Imaginal Lineage
chapters.description: Rob describes turning to a timeless imaginal lineage for support when teaching or when illness left him depleted.

chapters.title: Assimilation and Development
chapters.description: He encourages each person to take up the teachings according to their calling, while developing a solid base and noticing blind spots.

chapters.title: Sangha and Friendship
chapters.description: Rob closes by emphasizing study, mutual support, and the hard work of deep friendship within Sangha.

chapters.title: Final Thanks
chapters.description: He ends with repeated thanks and a brief, unfinished exchange as the talk comes to a close.

title: Image, Ethics, and Awakening (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: The talks, instructions, and guided meditations in this set are from a retreat, led by Catherine McGee and Rob Burbea, for practitioners already familiar with Soulmaking Dharma. The teachers strongly recommend that you also have an understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice, before listening. Without this background in practice it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What happens when ethics, eros, ritual, and imaginal practice are all held in the same room? In this Q&A, the talk moves from precepts as the ground of practice to a broader claim that ethics must keep growing as Soulmaking Dharma deepens, with repeated concern for how images shape action, speech, and responsibility. It also turns practical and personal: ritual, sexual eros, the risks of misunderstanding, the place of service, and a closing question about what kind of life is actually worth living. The tone stays reflective but direct, with several vivid examples and a clear resistance to easy answers.
short_summary: A wide-ranging Q&A on how ethics, eros, ritual, and imaginal practice change as Soulmaking Dharma deepens, ending by asking what one truly wants from life.

chapters.title: Opening Questions
chapters.description: Rob begins by taking up retreat questions and setting the tone for a practical, wide-ranging discussion.

chapters.title: Precepts as Basis
chapters.description: He reflects on his first Dharma talk and explains why the precepts are not a side issue but the ground of practice.

chapters.title: Ethics Deepens
chapters.description: He argues that ethics becomes more organic and more demanding as Soulmaking Dharma unfolds, rather than less important.

chapters.title: Ritual and Sacredness
chapters.description: Rob turns to ritual, describing how it can sanctify ordinary things and widen the sense of what can be sensed with soul.

chapters.title: Imaginal Attention
chapters.description: He explains that imaginal practice is a way of looking, balancing skill, receptivity, and grace rather than relying on the object itself.

chapters.title: Eros and Boundaries
chapters.description: The discussion moves through sexual eros, consent, and the care needed when imaginal practice enters relationships and community life.

chapters.title: Dark Night Reframed
chapters.description: Rob rejects fear-based ideas about the unconscious and the dark night, and instead links difficult practice effects to dependent origination and the style of practice.

chapters.title: The Sister Image
chapters.description: A detailed imaginal reading of an image of his sister becomes a living example of grief, beauty, eros, and compassion in practice.

chapters.title: Service and the Path
chapters.description: He closes by reflecting on the bodhisattva ideal, rebirth, awakening, and the need to ask what one truly wants from a life of practice and service.

title: Eros and Desire (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: The talks, instructions, and guided meditations in this set are from a retreat, led by Catherine McGee and Rob Burbea, for practitioners already familiar with Soulmaking Dharma. The teachers strongly recommend that you also have an understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice, before listening. Without this background in practice it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How does desire stay alive without collapsing into union, craving, or confusion? The talk moves through that question by clarifying twoness, showing why eros depends on retained tension and particularity, and then working back and forth between imaginal practice, emptiness, and the self so the listener can see how each may open into the other. Along the way, the speaker answers worries about narcissism, taboo objects, and overwhelm, and keeps returning to a practical point: the issue is less the object than the quality of the relationship around it.
short_summary: A Q&A on eros, desire, and imaginal practice that traces twoness, emptiness, and the difference between craving and a soulmaking relationship.

chapters.title: Twoness and relationship
chapters.description: Burbea explains why twoness matters in relationship and why eros depends on a retained polarity rather than full merger.

chapters.title: Eros needs polarity
chapters.description: He contrasts eros with oneness, emphasizing tension, particularity, and the difference between attraction and dissolution.

chapters.title: Eros for the self
chapters.description: In response to a question about narcissism, he argues that the issue is the quality of eros, not whether the self can be its object.

chapters.title: Imaginal practice and cosmos
chapters.description: He describes imaginal practice as a way of sanctifying the self's world, then paraphrases a vivid scene of self, friend, and lover on the grass.

chapters.title: Emptiness opens into image
chapters.description: A question about emptiness leads into his distinction between skillful fabrication and skillful unfabricating, and how emptiness can itself become imaginal.

chapters.title: Eros and craving
chapters.description: Burbea develops the idea that eros and craving may share a root, then shows how balancing self, object, and world keeps the field open and alive.

chapters.title: Body as beloved
chapters.description: He addresses the body as an object of eros, noting that charge and pain often help imaginal practice become vivid and meaningful.

chapters.title: Feminine figures of desire
chapters.description: The final exchange turns to Aphrodite, Tara, Kuan Yin, and Ragarati, with Jung used to clarify archetype as a forming principle rather than a fixed image.

title: On Practising with Images (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: The talks, instructions, and guided meditations in this set are from a retreat, led by Catherine McGee and Rob Burbea, for practitioners already familiar with Soulmaking Dharma. The teachers strongly recommend that you also have an understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice, before listening. Without this background in practice it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What happens when an image starts to include the self, and the self-sense itself begins to feel imaginal? The talk moves through practical questions about how to work with images that are vivid, flat, shifting, or surprisingly erotic, and keeps returning to the need to attune to what feels soulmaking rather than getting lost in story or trying to force a result. It also opens out into how art, beauty, grief, and relationship can all carry this imaginal quality, changing the way one sees oneself and others.
short_summary: A Q&A on how to practice with imaginal images, especially when the self becomes part of the image, with guidance on trust, pacing, resonance, and the shift from narrative to soulmaking.

chapters.title: A self seen as image
chapters.description: A participant describes an image of meeting another version of herself, and Rob begins to unpack what it means when the self becomes part of the imaginal field.

chapters.title: How image enters practice
chapters.description: He explains several ways imaginal quality can arise indirectly or directly, including how attention, resonance, and the self-sense can be drawn into the image.

chapters.title: Divinity, beauty, and culture
chapters.description: Rob expands on the sense of being image or angel, and reflects on how culture limits the way people understand self-worth, beauty, and divinity.

chapters.title: Repeated encounters matter
chapters.description: He emphasizes that this shift often needs repetition, post-meditative carryover, and patient exposure rather than a single striking experience.

chapters.title: Trusting the process
chapters.description: Rob responds to questions about bird-like selves and other imaginal forms, stressing trust, experimentation, and the fact that practice evolves through use.

chapters.title: Art, beauty, and violence
chapters.description: The conversation turns to beauty in art, including references to the Seven Stories and Rilke, and to the way art can open care, action, grief, and a wider field of feeling.

chapters.title: Boundaries and texture
chapters.description: He addresses boundary, dissolution, and the balance between vivid detail and what is soulmaking, trying to distinguish mere visual richness from living resonance.

chapters.title: Narrative and nectar
chapters.description: Using an image of two men on a porch, Rob distinguishes timeless iconic images from narrative drift and returns to the importance of lingering with what feels alive.

chapters.title: Relationship and desire
chapters.description: Questions about real people, shame, erotic charge, and multiple relational tones lead him to say that deep human connection already includes an imaginal dimension.

title: Dukkha and Emotions in Soulmaking Practice
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: The talks, instructions, and guided meditations in this set are from a retreat, led by Catherine McGee and Rob Burbea, for practitioners already familiar with Soulmaking Dharma. The teachers strongly recommend that you also have an understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice, before listening. Without this background in practice it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you work with dukkha when there is no formula, and when what seems like ordinary suffering may need either basic emotional repair or a more imaginal response? The talk moves through a patient, improvised account of anger, depression, energy, and the need to rebuild neglected foundations before anything can become soulmaking. From there it widens into climate grief, sacred responsibility, and the possibility that some inner tensions are not to be harmonized but lived as part of a larger, polytheistic psyche.
short_summary: A careful, non-linear talk on meeting dukkha through improvisation, emotional honesty, and the shifting border between healing and soulmaking.

chapters.title: No formula for dukkha
chapters.description: He opens by saying the dukkha-soulmaking nexus is too complex for fixed methods and must be approached improvisationally.

chapters.title: Anger and hidden pain
chapters.description: A case study shows how disallowed anger can turn inward into depression, suggesting that basic permission may be needed before deeper work.

chapters.title: Foundations first
chapters.description: He develops the idea of rebuilding emotional and psychological foundations so blocked parts of the psyche can come alive again.

chapters.title: Images after repair
chapters.description: An example shows how, once the basics settle, imaginal material can emerge and liberate what was stuck.

chapters.title: Lattice and leverage
chapters.description: He points to the non-linear lattice of elements and says any one of them may become the opening that changes everything.

chapters.title: Crucible of emotion
chapters.description: Using Catherine McGee’s framing, he describes how a proper stance and enough energetic mindfulness can make emotion into a crucible rather than a meltdown or a mere letting-go practice.

chapters.title: Dharma and soul
chapters.description: He contrasts ordinary Dharma moves like reducing self-story with Soulmaking moves that allow self, narrative, and object to become imaginal together.

chapters.title: Ecological grief and roles
chapters.description: Questions about climate crisis lead him to discuss sacred-world views, imaginal roles such as warrior or hospice worker, and the relative freedom and cost of such callings.

chapters.title: Polytheistic psyche
chapters.description: He ends with the tension between conflicting inner gods and images, suggesting that soulmaking may bring both freedom and sacrifice rather than simple harmony.

title: A Rite of Angels
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: The talks, instructions, and guided meditations in this set are from a retreat, led by Catherine McGee and Rob Burbea, for practitioners already familiar with Soulmaking Dharma. The teachers strongly recommend that you also have an understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice, before listening. Without this background in practice it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you practice when the mind keeps measuring what’s missing? The talk begins with a quiet return to gratitude: Buddha, Dharma, Sangha, the lineage behind practice, and the many supports that have carried a person to the cushion. It then turns into a more elaborate retreat ritual of walking, watching, chanting, and blessing, where others are met first with welcoming and metta, then increasingly as unique beings, and finally as angel or Buddha nature. The movement is from self-criticism toward a more expansive way of seeing: body, voice, soul, limitation, and potential held together in one field of practice.
short_summary: A talk that moves from gratitude for the supports of practice into a ritual of walking, chanting, and seeing others as angel or Buddha nature.

chapters.title: Opening the ritual
chapters.description: He introduces the Triple Gem, lights candles, and frames the evening as a practice of time and the timeless.

chapters.title: Resting on support
chapters.description: The talk turns into a reflective practice on how practice rests on lineage, teachers, and one’s own long effort.

chapters.title: Setting up the circle
chapters.description: He explains the walking-circle exercise and the basic intentions of welcoming and metta.

chapters.title: Seeing the walker
chapters.description: The practice expands into sensing another person’s dukkha, uniqueness, and possible angelic or Buddha-nature presence.
