The talk continues its close look at realism in imaginal practice, showing how images can be over-literalized into self-judgment, prediction, or duty, and how that rigidity can produce swings between grandiosity and self-abasement, even religious or secular dogmatism. From there, the speaker turns to personal examples of creative loneliness, changing archetypal figures over time, and the way imaginal work can open an image without trying to fix it by force. The later movement widens into harder questions: what healing and liberation mean, how many callings can coexist, and whether eros points to reality, creates it, or participates in it.
short_summary: A careful, questioning talk on realism, eros, and imaginal practice, moving from personal archetypal images to broader questions of healing, liberation, and the ontology of sensing with soul.

chapters.title: Realism and dukkha
chapters.description: He begins by naming realism as the main way imaginal practice gets mishandled, especially when images are taken too literally or made into fixed claims about the self or the future.

chapters.title: Polarized swings
chapters.description: He shows how believed-in realism often produces pendulum swings between grandiosity and self-abasement, including clinical language around mania and depression.

chapters.title: Creative loneliness
chapters.description: He turns to a personal imaginal history shaped by solitude, marginality, and struggle, using it to show how an image can be both deeply meaningful and painful.

chapters.title: Images evolve
chapters.description: He traces how solitary warrior and wanderer images gradually widened into figures of rest, healing, and nourishment, suggesting that imaginal transformation often happens organically.

chapters.title: Images ask for images
chapters.description: He argues that conventional frameworks did not change these images; rather, repeated imaginal engagement opened them, and he stresses the importance of not trying to improve them too quickly.

chapters.title: Healing and liberation
chapters.description: He widens the discussion with Jung, Hillman, and soulmaking language, asking what healing or liberation would even mean for an image, a self, or a soul.

chapters.title: Impossibility and duty
chapters.description: He develops Hillman’s challenge to simple liberation talk, then describes the dukkha built into imaginal life: the impossibility of fully manifesting every image, calling, or duty at once.

chapters.title: Full intention, less craving
chapters.description: He returns to eros and intention, explaining how dukkha drops when desire stays spacious, includes logos, and remains oriented to the whole of soulmaking rather than a single object.

chapters.title: Auden and ontology
chapters.description: He reads Auden on eros as a religious vision, then sketches a spectrum of ontological views and ends by proposing a participatory way of understanding sensing the soul.

title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What does it take for soulmaking practice to deepen instead of simply generating more movement, more intensity, or more dukkha? The talk keeps returning to pacing: when to open, when to stay with one image or desire, and how to balance imaginal work with steadier practices like samadhi, metta, mindfulness, and emptiness. It also addresses common worries about danger, fantasy, and losing touch with reality, including a discussion of Jungian sub-personalities, the Kabbalistic idea of the breaking of the vessels, and why questioning can be part of growth rather than a threat to it.
short_summary: A careful talk about pacing soulmaking practice, balancing openness with consolidation, and working through fears about eros, imagination, and reality.

chapters.title: Opening the path
chapters.description: He begins by naming the basic concern of making soulmaking practices sustainable rather than dukkha-producing.

chapters.title: Pacing and steadiness
chapters.description: Rob explains why the path needs both opening and staying with what has been revealed long enough for it to deepen.

chapters.title: Retreat balance
chapters.description: He turns to how retreat life can mix imaginal practice with other forms of practice instead of doing only one thing all day.

chapters.title: Two practice currents
chapters.description: He sketches the balance between practices that build equanimity and practices of eros, and how they can support one another.

chapters.title: Working with fear
chapters.description: Rob addresses ambivalence, concern about danger, and the importance of not forcing oneself into practices one cannot yet hold.

chapters.title: Imaginal figures and autonomy
chapters.description: He distinguishes his sense of the imaginal from a safer Jungian sub-personality frame and stresses the autonomy of imaginal figures.

chapters.title: Breaking and rebuilding
chapters.description: Using the Kabbalistic idea of breaking vessels, he describes how practice can fracture old structures and later require integration.

chapters.title: Questioning and sexual imaginal
chapters.description: He says inquiry can be risky but is often more dangerous not to do, then turns to sexual imaginal practice and its icon-like, non-escalating quality.

chapters.title: Darker images and realism
chapters.description: The talk closes by encouraging trust in darker images, expanding sacredness, and returning to the deeper question of realism and the imaginal middle way.

title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What makes a path sustainable when imaginal practice, desire, and suffering all start to touch each other? The talk moves from the scope of dukkha and soulmaking into a careful map of what this work asks: emotional awareness, body and energy-body sensitivity, the ability to discern eros from craving, and enough insight to know when to let go and when to stay with what is present. It then turns to the emotional life in detail, including different ways of relating to self, the need for flexibility around psychological frameworks, and how the earlier seven instructions can reveal the dependent arising of emotion. In the later part, the emphasis widens to relationship, vulnerability, and temenos: the kind of sacred, careful space needed for imaginal sharing to remain alive rather than collapse.
short_summary: A detailed talk on how soulmaking practice relates to dukkha, emotion, self, relationship, and the care needed to make the path sustainable.

chapters.title: Framing the subject
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the scope of the series and sets out the large questions around dukkha, imaginal practice, eros, and the Four Noble Truths.

chapters.title: Why care is needed
chapters.description: He emphasizes that soulmaking work can also intensify suffering if it lacks the right awareness, prerequisites, and sensitivity.

chapters.title: Foundational skills
chapters.description: The talk turns to the practical capacities needed for this path, including body, heart, mind, humility, reverence, and distinctions between eros and craving.

chapters.title: Sustainable practice
chapters.description: Using the Buddha and Tibetan Buddhist preliminaries as examples, he argues that meaningful paths need pacing, preparation, and foundations rather than just intensity.

chapters.title: Working with emotion
chapters.description: He unfolds a sequence of ways to relate to emotions, from bare sensation to compassionate self-inclusion, showing how attention, kindness, and flexibility change the experience.

chapters.title: Self, story, and view
chapters.description: The discussion widens to self-fabrication, imaginal selfhood, and the dangers of getting locked into one psychological framework or one style of mindfulness.

chapters.title: Seven instructions revisited
chapters.description: Burbea returns to the earlier seven practices, treating them as focused exercises that can reveal subtle emotion, support samadhi, and make dependent origination visible.

chapters.title: Relationship and vulnerability
chapters.description: He argues that soulmaking often becomes alive in relationship, where desire, image, shame, fear of rejection, and the need to be seen all become active.

chapters.title: Temenos and sacred space
chapters.description: The talk closes by developing temenos as a protective, reverent field for sharing imaginal experience, where timing, privacy, attunement, and care help soulmaking hold its ground.

title: Sensing with Soul (Part 7)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What counts as knowing, and why do we trust some kinds of experience while shrinking back from others? The talk moves through that question by comparing ordinary facts, values like beauty and goodness, inner images, and the harder case of sensing soul in the world around us. From a tree’s love, to the Smurfs and Beethoven, to a black cat in a dark basement, the speaker keeps testing where contemporary culture draws the line between imagination, perception, and reality. The talk ends by suggesting that a middle way, and eventually the idea of participation, may open a less cramped way of understanding truth and soul-making.
short_summary: A close, philosophical exploration of how we know values, images, and soul, and why a middle-way stance may matter more than fixed certainty.

chapters.title: Opening the epistemic question
chapters.description: He returns to the talk’s opening concern: what counts as knowing when the subject is soul-sensing and value.

chapters.title: Values and modern skepticism
chapters.description: He contrasts material facts with beauty, goodness, and love, arguing that modern culture often grants values a lesser truth status.

chapters.title: Inner images, outer knowing
chapters.description: He distinguishes intra-psychic images, which many people now validate, from the harder question of extra-psychic soul-sensing.

chapters.title: Love, beauty, and judgment
chapters.description: Using the tree example, a mother-son story, and the Smurfs versus Beethoven, he asks how we know love and beauty at all.

chapters.title: ESP and imaginal contrast
chapters.description: He separates matter-of-fact ESP-like information from imaginal perceptions that carry depth, beauty, and meaningfulness.

chapters.title: Middle way in practice
chapters.description: He describes the imaginal middle way as a stance that preserves freedom, wonder, and soulmaking, while literalizing tends to diminish the experience.

chapters.title: Black cat metaphor
chapters.description: A basement-and-black-cat image illustrates how experience can make what once seemed speculative feel worthy of conceptual framing.

chapters.title: Culture and hidden assumptions
chapters.description: He broadens the inquiry into how cultures rank forms of knowing, using autism, color-blindness, and Richard Rorty to question default materialism.

chapters.title: Participation and soulmaking
chapters.description: He closes with participation as a possible way beyond subject/object dualism, where truth, reality, and soul are mutually discovered and made.

title: Sensing with Soul (Part 6)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What if sensing with soul is also a way of knowing values like beauty, love, goodness, and duty, when modern culture tends to treat those as merely subjective? The talk traces how ideas from Galileo through Descartes, Bacon, Locke, and later scientism helped install a hierarchy of what counts as real, and how that hierarchy can quietly limit imagination, practice, and even the way the body is perceived. It also warns that any method of knowing can become a prison when it is treated as absolute, whether in science, religion, or Dharma. The speaker argues that imaginal perception includes a real sensing of values, and shows how modern and religious ideas shape what we think we can know.
short_summary: A close look at how modern ideas about reality and knowledge can obscure the value-sensing dimension of imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Opening the Questions
chapters.description: Rob introduces the talk as an opening into ontology and epistemology in relation to soulmaking and imaginal perception.

chapters.title: Values in Imaginal Perception
chapters.description: He develops the claim that sensing with soul includes sensing values such as beauty, love, nobility, and humility.

chapters.title: Galileo to Descartes
chapters.description: Using Galileo, Locke, and Descartes, he sketches the rise of primary qualities, secondary qualities, and the modern split between objective fact and subjective value.

chapters.title: How Cultures Rank Knowing
chapters.description: He broadens the argument to show how cultures and subcultures create hierarchies of reality, respect, and legitimate knowledge.

chapters.title: Modes of Being
chapters.description: He distinguishes different modes of being, such as science, art, politics, and soulmaking, each with its own value-order and way of knowing.

chapters.title: Jung and the Prison of View
chapters.description: He uses Jung to describe how even liberating ideas can harden into a prison when treated as absolute.

chapters.title: Literalism and Interpretation
chapters.description: He shifts to biblical literalism as an example of interpretive rigidity and the way a narrow reading can shape religion and culture.

chapters.title: Body, Fantasy, and Perception
chapters.description: He closes with a retreat story and a Theravada-influenced body view to show how conceptual frameworks and imaginal backgrounds color perception.

title: Sensing with Soul (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: How do you tell the difference between a busy mind and an imaginal opening? The talk keeps returning to that question through concrete examples: a creative project softened by another person's perception, a painful memory reframed in a shared field, and ordinary tenderness turning into an iconic sense of beauty and blessing. From there it widens into the claim that sensing with soul is not a technique to control, but a way of noticing what we value, what we receive from others, and how the energy body, humility, and attention help a life situation become imaginal rather than merely problematic.
short_summary: A practical review of how imaginal perception emerges through relationship, value, and the energy body, with examples of anxiety, grief, art, and desire opening into soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening the review
chapters.description: Rob begins by reviewing the overall intention of the talk and encourages experimentation, careful noticing, and patience rather than pressure to get it right.

chapters.title: Conditions for sensing
chapters.description: He names the main supports for sensing with soul, including humility, heart-softening, reduced fabrication, sensitivity, energy-body awareness, and different entry points into imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Grace and receptivity
chapters.description: The review turns to the limits of technique, the role of grace, and the idea that imaginal knowing often involves receiving something from beyond oneself.

chapters.title: A creative project opens
chapters.description: Rob tells a personal story about how Catherine’s imaginal perception helped loosen his anxiety and reopen a creative project in a more imaginal way.

chapters.title: Pain re-seen
chapters.description: He gives an example from retreat where a painful memory is reframed through an imaginal image, showing how shame can shift without erasing the original suffering.

chapters.title: Nakedness and blessing
chapters.description: A shared sense of tenderness with a friend becomes iconic and luminous, and Rob describes how attention, blessing, and touch can participate in the imaginal field.

chapters.title: What people transmit
chapters.description: Using Catherine’s niece and grandmother, he reflects on how people carry and communicate a particular refraction of the sacred, not just comfort or advice.

chapters.title: When fantasy becomes imaginal
chapters.description: Rob reframes a woman’s mixed feelings about art, literature, and professional pressure as a nexus of meaningful fantasies that can be worked with meditatively instead of dismissed as mere papanca.

chapters.title: Values and next steps
chapters.description: He closes by linking imaginal perception to values, suggesting that what we deeply value is always involved, and noting several themes that could be explored further in future talks.

title: Sensing with Soul (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What changes when experience becomes less fixed, and can be sensed as already alive with image, value, and sacredness? The talk moves from a broad map of “sensing with soul” to the conditions that support it: intimacy with what is present, sensitivity in the whole energy body, loosening clinging, and then a readiness for whatever imaginal quality is most helpful. It then turns to examples — a student’s emptiness practice turning vedana holy, a ritual with tangerines and a gold bowl, and an eating meditation that opens into interdependence, compassion, and devotion. Near the end, the talk asks how intention itself can help or hinder this kind of sensing, especially when the aim is not just to reduce suffering but to let perception deepen and reconfigure it.
short_summary: A careful exploration of how emptiness, intimacy, ritual, and intention can open ordinary perception into sensing with soul.

chapters.title: Opening the Theme
chapters.description: Rob resumes the thread of sensing with soul and offers a broad view of how imaginal practice matures.

chapters.title: Four Conditions
chapters.description: He begins outlining the conditions that tend to support sensing things with soul rather than only as fixed objects.

chapters.title: Intimacy and Energy Body
chapters.description: The talk turns to openness, sensitivity in the whole energy body, loosening clinging, and receptivity to imaginal elements.

chapters.title: How the Process Works
chapters.description: Rob explains the mutual dependence among these factors and uses the framework to prepare for concrete examples.

chapters.title: Vedana Becomes Holy
chapters.description: A student’s report shows emptiness practice and imaginal perception meeting, with feeling-tone becoming sacred rather than merely noted.

chapters.title: Tangerines and Gold
chapters.description: He recounts a retreat ritual with a gold bowl, chanting, and tangerines to show how shared ritual can sanctify ordinary food and create a soul field.

chapters.title: A Meal of Interdependence
chapters.description: Another student’s eating meditation illustrates how interdependence, gratitude, desire, and embodied imagination can deepen perception.

chapters.title: Two Perceptions, One World
chapters.description: He closes by describing how ordinary perception can coexist with imaginal perception, and how a medical intention to reduce suffering may sometimes narrow the fuller opening of soul-making.

title: Sensing with Soul (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: How does perception shift from ordinary seeing into sensing the world with soul, and what actually helps that change happen? The talk moves through that question by treating soulmaking as a dynamic process: sometimes sudden, sometimes gradual, sometimes consolidating before it shifts again. From there, it turns practical, asking where attention needs to be weighted—toward Self, Other, World, the energy body, or desire—so the movement does not get stuck in habit or flatten into a formula.
short_summary: An exploration of how perception becomes imaginal, with practical guidance on balance, desire, the energy body, and noticing when habit or reification is blocking soulmaking.

chapters.title: Framing the inquiry
chapters.description: He introduces the talk as a close look at how perception becomes soul-sensing, while encouraging an attitude of play rather than rigidity.

chapters.title: Soulmaking as dynamism
chapters.description: He describes soulmaking as a living process with different tempos, from sudden shifts to gradual consolidation, and uses the hawk gliding on thermal currents as an image of responsive practice.

chapters.title: Different tempos of change
chapters.description: He develops the idea that conceptual frameworks, like Logos, often change more slowly than other elements, and that change can unfold in stages rather than always dramatically.

chapters.title: Self, Other, World
chapters.description: He turns to the Self-Other-World trinity, showing how practice may need to lean toward one pole to counter habit and restore balance.

chapters.title: The energy body
chapters.description: He suggests that some people need to spend more time with the energy body, moving between gross and subtle registers and noticing where attention has become one-sided.

chapters.title: Desire and eros
chapters.description: He discusses blocked desire, encouraging listeners to notice it, allow it, and eventually recognize it as beautiful or even divine.

chapters.title: Habit or soul's call
chapters.description: He asks what is merely habitual and what is genuinely given by soul, applying that question to emotion, energy, and the broader shape of practice.

chapters.title: Reification and looseness
chapters.description: He returns to reification as a root problem in stuck or distorted soulmaking, but ends by emphasizing a light, art-like way of teaching and practicing that allows for exceptions.

title: Sensing with Soul (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What is actually being made when experience becomes soulful? The talk returns to that question by unpacking sensing with soul as a movement where Eros, image, and concept interweave: desire reaches toward more, imagery holds that reaching, and understanding gives it shape and durability. It also explores how this dynamic can stretch our ideas, deepen discernment, and open a larger sense of life, while noting that the whole subject resists any tight definition.
short_summary: A close look at sensing with soul as an Eros-Psyche-Logos dynamic that deepens experience, stretches concepts, and sharpens discernment.

chapters.title: What sensing with soul means
chapters.description: Rob reintroduces the term as a way of framing imaginal practice and explains why it matters for how we meet life, nature, and each other.

chapters.title: Making and receiving
chapters.description: He describes soulmaking as both something that happens to us and something we participate in through choice, desire, and responsiveness.

chapters.title: What soul names
chapters.description: Soul is unpacked as a set of qualities and then as an organ or capacity for ways of looking that open soulfulness.

chapters.title: Eros, Psyche, Logos
chapters.description: The talk lays out the three strands of the dynamic and shows how they interweave in imaginal perception and soulmaking.

chapters.title: Beyond and transgression
chapters.description: Rob traces the way Eros creates a beyond, including the possibility of transgression, while warning against reducing this to taboo or craving.

chapters.title: Why frameworks matter
chapters.description: He argues that understanding the conceptual structure is not secondary, but helps the whole practice become more robust, fertile, and usable.

chapters.title: Agile thinking and Feynman
chapters.description: The talk turns to the flexibility of Logos, with a brief Feynman anecdote illustrating why some subjects can only be explained simply after familiarity grows.

chapters.title: Undefinable but fertile
chapters.description: Rob reflects on why soul must remain partly undefinable so it can keep opening, and ends by showing how soulmaking sharpens discernment and distinction.

title: Sensing with Soul (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What if the point of practice is not just to notice experience, but to sense the world, the self, and even perception itself with soul? The talk traces how imaginal practice can spill beyond an inner image into a wider cosmopoetic way of seeing, then distinguishes this from oneness perceptions and more reductionist forms of mindfulness. It moves toward a careful, practical map of sensing with soul: how it can include the senses, thought, reverence, discernment, play, and a deepening equanimity even in the face of illness and death.
short_summary: A sustained inquiry into sensing with soul, where imaginal practice, mindfulness, and oneness are sorted into different modes of perception and practice.

chapters.title: From Image to World
chapters.description: Burbea recalls earlier imaginal teaching and explains how an inward image can spill outward into the world as cosmopoiesis.

chapters.title: Cosmopoiesis and Oneness
chapters.description: He distinguishes imaginal cosmopoiesis from perceptions of universal oneness and shows how both can shape experience without being reducible to illusion.

chapters.title: Introducing Sensing with Soul
chapters.description: He names sensing with soul as the larger frame that includes imaginal perception, oneness-perceptions, and the movement between them.

chapters.title: Why It Matters Most
chapters.description: Burbea argues that the real point is not fascinating imagery, but the opening, healing, and re-enchantment of existence itself.

chapters.title: Philosophy and Examples
chapters.description: He quotes J. N. Findlay on mysticism and beauty, then uses examples from music and lovers to show that sensing with soul can be embodied and ordinary.

chapters.title: Soul in All Senses
chapters.description: Using B.B. King, touch, thinking, and the six or seven senses, he shows how the whole field of experience can be sensed imaginally.

chapters.title: Four Modes of Contact
chapters.description: He outlines everyday, dharma-lensed, oneness-based, and imaginal modes of sense contact, clarifying what changes in each one.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and Dependence
chapters.description: Burbea links sensing with soul to mindfulness, appropriate attention, and dependent origination, emphasizing responsiveness rather than passivity.

chapters.title: Reverence and Play
chapters.description: He closes by showing that reverence, discernment, and play are part of sensing with soul and of practice itself.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 6)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: How do imaginal images become more fully imaginal: by being noticed, lightly questioned, deliberately nudged, and held with enough delicacy that one quality can brighten the whole field. The talk moves through a practical list of aspects—meaningfulness, timelessness, the imaginal middle way, participation, and concept—showing how each can be tuned in practice rather than treated as an abstract definition. Along the way, the speaker returns to examples like body-as-light or body-as-jewels, and keeps insisting that impermanence is not denied but held together with a subtler sense of sacredness and poetic truth.
short_summary: A detailed walkthrough of how specific qualities can support imaginal practice, from meaningfulness and timelessness to participation and the role of concepts.

chapters.title: Framing the Teaching
chapters.description: He restates the purpose of the series and the practical reason for naming the qualities of the imaginal.

chapters.title: Amplifying the Image
chapters.description: He explains three ways to work with an imaginal node: notice it, question it, or deliberately turn it on.

chapters.title: Subtlety and Intention
chapters.description: He emphasizes delicacy in practice and gives examples of humility, love, and fullness of intention as catalysts.

chapters.title: Bodies of Light and Jewel
chapters.description: He offers deliberate imaginal images of the body, including body as light, mantra as jewels, and body as earth.

chapters.title: Meaningfulness and Devotion
chapters.description: He describes meaningfulness as larger than any single meaning, with a sense of pregnant significance and orientation.

chapters.title: Timelessness and Sacred Time
chapters.description: He develops the sense of eternality, contrast it with narrative time, and shows how it can open the imaginal world.

chapters.title: Holding Impermanence
chapters.description: He guards against misunderstanding timelessness as denial of impermanence and insists both dimensions can be held together.

chapters.title: Imaginal Middle Way
chapters.description: He distinguishes the imaginal middle way from ordinary realist categories and connects it to the theatre-like quality of imaginal experience.

chapters.title: Way of Looking
chapters.description: He explains that the imaginal depends on the perceiver's way of looking and that this dependence does not make it unreal.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: How can imaginal practice be fuller than self-improvement, and what changes when the image is not there for my growth but for soul-making itself? The talk works through that question by distinguishing intention, autonomy, two-ness, and the way meaning emerges without collapsing an image into a single interpretation. It moves from a reflection on eros, logos, and divinity into concrete examples—a rapturous Dartmoor image, a warrior god figure, and a flying dragon—to show how images can open into deeper relationship, not just symbolism.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of fullness of intention in imaginal practice, moving from soul-making and autonomy to soft boundaries, meaningfulness, and the refusal to reduce images to one fixed meaning.

chapters.title: Fullness of Intention
chapters.description: Burbea defines the 12th element as an intention that moves beyond self-growth toward soul-making, divinity, and Buddha-nature.

chapters.title: Autonomy and Dependent Arising
chapters.description: He introduces the imaginal other as autonomous while keeping dependent arising as the underlying Buddhist frame.

chapters.title: Dartmoor Tentacles
chapters.description: A walk on Dartmoor becomes an example of an image that is rapturous without meaning union or dissolution.

chapters.title: Two-ness and Free Choice
chapters.description: He extends the discussion to self and other, then brings in freedom, necessity, and the role of intention in soulmaking.

chapters.title: Ethics and Freedom
chapters.description: Viktor Frankl’s testimony is used to show how ethical choice can remain present even under extreme constraint.

chapters.title: Soft Edges of Being
chapters.description: Burbea develops the 14th element: boundaries in imaginal perception are indefinite, soft, and elastic rather than fixed.

chapters.title: Echoing and Resonance
chapters.description: He links soft boundaries to the sense of infinite echoing, then illustrates how an image can mirror a person’s life and practice.

chapters.title: Meaning and Values
chapters.description: The final stretch clarifies that meaningfulness includes, but is not limited to, specific meanings, and it ends by tying imaginal life to values and their open-endedness.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What if an imaginal perception is not a single thing, but a whole constellation of elements that can be entered from different doors? The talk moves through that field by tracing how attention, receptivity, eros, beauty, divinity, grace, reverence, humility, trust, and duty can each ignite the others, sometimes all at once and sometimes gradually, like dimmers turning up rather than a switch flipping on. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to the practical question of how to sense these qualities without reducing them: how to stay open, how to tell the difference between contraction and opening, and how to let an image or insight actually reshape the life around it.
short_summary: An exploration of the imaginal as a living constellation of qualities—grace, humility, trust, beauty, and duty—that can be gradually or suddenly ignited through careful, receptive attention.

chapters.title: Imaginal constellation
chapters.description: Burbea frames imaginal perception as a lattice of interrelated elements that can be studied, tuned, and gently stirred.

chapters.title: Ignition and grace
chapters.description: He describes how one node can light up the whole field, and how receptivity matters as much as technique.

chapters.title: Switches and dials
chapters.description: The talk develops the sense that imaginal practice can shift suddenly or gradually, with attention acting like a set of adjustable dials.

chapters.title: Elements of the field
chapters.description: He names several key nodes in the constellation, including energy body, soulfulness, love, eros, and beauty.

chapters.title: Dimensionality and divinity
chapters.description: Burbea explores how imaginal depth shades into divinity, while warning against easy ontological claims and insisting on an epistemological middle way.

chapters.title: Grace, reverence, humility
chapters.description: He describes the sacred as gift, then turns to reverence, bowing, and humility as bodily and ethical orientations rather than self-effacing ideals.

chapters.title: Humility as image
chapters.description: Humility itself becomes an imaginal object that can expand, deepen, and be transformed through soulmaking rather than remaining a fixed virtue.

chapters.title: Trust in the image
chapters.description: A grain of trust can loosen reification, let puzzling images move, and reveal a deeper intelligence at work in the field.

chapters.title: Duty to consolidate
chapters.description: The talk closes with duty as the soul’s felt responsibility to bring new insight, oneness, or practice-state into ordinary life and not let it be forgotten.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What makes something imaginal: the image itself, or the relationship that brings it to life? The talk moves by loosening fixed definitions and then testing them against practice, arguing that imaginal qualities arise when attention, energy body awareness, meaningfulness, eros, and love begin to light one another up. A lattice of connected nodes becomes the guiding image here, with the emphasis on how noticing, jiggling, or gently empowering one element can open the whole field without reducing it to technique alone. Grace, mystery, and the limits of control stay present throughout.
short_summary: A careful exploration of the imaginal as a relational field, with energy body awareness, soul-making, and love as key nodes that can open practice.

chapters.title: Recap of the framework
chapters.description: He reviews the loose, non-exhaustive list of imaginal aspects and emphasizes that the teaching is meant to sharpen discernment without becoming rigid.

chapters.title: Relational, not inherent
chapters.description: Using James Hillman as a comparison, he argues that nothing is inherently imaginal; it becomes so through a particular relationship and what that relationship opens.

chapters.title: The imaginal lattice
chapters.description: He introduces the image of a lattice of nodes, explaining that when enough elements are alive together, the whole perception can become imaginal.

chapters.title: Wiggling a node
chapters.description: He gives practical suggestions for nudging practice along the spectrum, especially by noticing and enlivening the energy body as one node in the constellation.

chapters.title: What energy body means
chapters.description: He defines energy body as a way of looking, contrasts it with materialist conceptions, and shows how body, concept, image, and felt sense overlap.

chapters.title: Soul-making and jhana
chapters.description: He turns to soul-making, saying it involves eros, psyche, and logos, and illustrates it with jhana practice and the deepening sense of connection to the tradition.

chapters.title: New territory and play
chapters.description: He explores how newness, body awareness, heart practice, and playfulness can themselves become soul-making when they open fresh range and meaning.

chapters.title: Love as a key node
chapters.description: He ends by focusing on loving and being loved as a decisive node in the imaginal lattice, including the different flavors love can take and the value of affirming it.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: What makes an image imaginal, rather than just vivid or useful? The talk reworks that question by moving from a recap of imagination as a spectrum, through the roles of duty, trust, and selfless intention, into practical ways of opening to imaginal perception through samadhi, emptiness, dream figures, living people, emotions, and even the body itself. A long personal example shows how an apparently ordinary image can become meaningful through patient attention, while a more dramatic one can still miss the imaginal if trust, love, and depth are absent.
short_summary: A practical exploration of how imaginal perception emerges through a way of looking, not just through striking inner images.

chapters.title: Recapping the terrain
chapters.description: He reviews the spectrum of imagination and re-sets the basic distinction between useful imagination and the imaginal.

chapters.title: Duty and trust
chapters.description: He adds duty, trust, and non-self-centered intention to the constellation of factors that support imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Opening the gates
chapters.description: He briefly points listeners to earlier instructions and then turns to how one might begin working with images in practice.

chapters.title: Ways into image
chapters.description: He offers a wide set of entry points: samadhi, emptiness, dreams, prior figures, deities, people, places, emotion, and the body.

chapters.title: The black coat story
chapters.description: A plain image of suit lapels unfolds into a fuller imaginal sense of a man, his devotion, and the meaning carried by the scene.

chapters.title: Vivid but not imaginal
chapters.description: He contrasts the previous example with a dramatic snake image that lacked trust, love, and dimensionality at first.

chapters.title: Patient transformation
chapters.description: He shows how introducing trust and related qualities can change an image over time, and reminds listeners that imaginal images are not always visual.

chapters.title: Technique and patience
chapters.description: He speaks candidly about not being naturally image-rich, and encourages experimentation, patience, and alternating imaginal work with other practices.

chapters.title: Life already imaginal
chapters.description: He closes by pointing to the imaginal already present in ordinary life, where self, body, conversation, inquiry, and world can all become image through the right mode of being.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: How do you tell the difference between ordinary imagination and something more fully imaginal? The talk opens by situating this as a continuation of earlier soulmaking teachings, then slows down to clarify the word “imaginal,” introduce “sensing with soul,” and map the kinds of practitioners who may need different entry points. From there it moves into a careful distinction between papañca, mindful imagination, and imaginal perception, before broadening into the philosophical questions of what counts as real and how we know it, and finally into a spectrum of experience that invites more depth, reverence, and mutual enrichment rather than rigid definitions.
short_summary: A careful first talk on imaginal practice that clarifies terms, distinguishes imagination from papanca, and sketches the move toward sensing with soul.

chapters.title: Course framing
chapters.description: Rob frames the series as a supplement to earlier soulmaking talks and emphasizes pacing, digestion, and clarification over adding new material.

chapters.title: What imaginal means
chapters.description: He begins to define the imaginal and introduces "sensing with soul" as nearly interchangeable language that may make the teaching easier to hear.

chapters.title: Different practitioner entry points
chapters.description: Rob sketches three practitioner situations: those who do not get images, those unsure whether their images are imaginal, and those who sense the sacred in the world but not inner images.

chapters.title: Ontology and knowing
chapters.description: He turns briefly to what is real and how we know, arguing that imaginal practice questions ordinary assumptions about ontology and epistemology.

chapters.title: Imagination in practice
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes papañca from mindful, embodied imagination and gives examples from metta and healing imagery, including figures such as Tara, the Buddha, Jesus, or a grandmother.

chapters.title: Naming the tradition
chapters.description: He pauses to say that his use of imaginal is specific, noting overlap and difference with Henri Corbin, James Hillman, Tom Cheetham, Philip Pullman, and Mary Watkins, then begins listing core qualities.

chapters.title: Core qualities of the imaginal
chapters.description: Rob names a set of qualities such as beyondness, autonomy, two-ness, mutual mirroring, meaningfulness, theater, conceptual structure, timelessness, humility, reverence, grace, and soulfulness.

chapters.title: Why lists matter
chapters.description: He explains why the list is useful despite his ambivalence, using the Buddha as a model for enumerated teaching and saying these elements can act as ignition keys for practice.

chapters.title: A dynamic spectrum
chapters.description: The talk closes by stressing that imaginal experience is dynamic rather than fixed, and that the question is how to move toward the fuller, more fertile end of the spectrum.

title: Shaping Vessels
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do practice and soulmaking keep growing after retreat ends? The talk moves from that central question into a search for the kinds of vessels that can support intimacy, shared inquiry, and imaginal life in ordinary settings. Along the way, it considers why people who burn for the path can still feel lonely, and how careful structure, mutuality, and right speech may help what is most alive become more shareable and more fully seen.
short_summary: A talk about what can sustain practice off-retreat, especially through shared vessels, vulnerable conversation, imaginal sharing, and right speech.

chapters.title: Opening Thanks
chapters.description: He begins by thanking the group for their practice and the quality of their participation in the retreat.

chapters.title: Practice Beyond Retreat
chapters.description: He asks what supports practice, growth, and soulmaking after retreat ends, framing the central question of the talk.

chapters.title: Loneliness In Dharma
chapters.description: He reflects on how dedicated practitioners can still feel isolated, especially when what matters most is hard to share.

chapters.title: Vulnerability And Simplicity
chapters.description: A story about a dog opening her belly becomes a contrast to human guardedness and complexity.

chapters.title: Kinds Of Vessel
chapters.description: He names different containers for practice, including retreat, study, discussion, and the sharing of images.

chapters.title: Temenos For Images
chapters.description: He introduces the idea of temenos, or sacred holding, and shows how structured sharing can deepen imaginal perception.

chapters.title: Right Speech Reconsidered
chapters.description: He turns to right speech as something that shapes consciousness and perception, not just conduct.

chapters.title: Closing Question
chapters.description: He ends by returning to the practical question of what will support the life of practice going forward.

title: Beauty and the Buddha
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if beauty is not a property of things at all, but something that arises in the relation between subject and object, and can be deepened by the way we look? The talk moves from the Pali Canon’s rather stark treatment of sense pleasure, through critiques of reductionist or tragic views of aesthetics, into an account of beauty as eros, resonance, imaginal perception, and eventually divinity and buddhanature. It ends by asking whether the aim of practice is really to reach a final truth, or to support an ever-expanding movement of soulmaking.
short_summary: A wide-ranging inquiry into beauty, emptiness, eros, and soulmaking that argues for a more open, relational, and mystical understanding of experience.

chapters.title: Opening the Question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces beauty as the talk’s theme and says the material will unfold nonlinearly, tied to earlier retreat teachings.

chapters.title: Beauty in the Canon
chapters.description: He looks at the Pali Canon’s unusual use of beauty, especially its association with the mind and the fourth jhana rather than ordinary sense objects.

chapters.title: Sense Pleasures and Limits
chapters.description: The talk turns to the canon’s warnings about sense pleasure and the tension between worldly senses and the unfabricated.

chapters.title: Radiance and Vividness
chapters.description: He considers a friend’s claim that beauty arises from mindful, craving-free perception, then reframes radiance as vividness, alertness, and presence.

chapters.title: Tragedy or More
chapters.description: Keats’s negative capability and a tragic reading of art are used as a contrast, setting up Burbea’s critique of a singular, impermanence-centered aesthetic.

chapters.title: Eros and Resonance
chapters.description: Burbea develops beauty as eros, resonance, and participation between subject and object, linking it to imaginal experience and the widening of perception.

chapters.title: Emptiness and Dimensions
chapters.description: He argues that beauty is relational and empty of fixed essence, then critiques practices or teachings that flatten experience into one dimension or one taste.

chapters.title: Divinity and Buddha Nature
chapters.description: Beauty becomes an analogy for divinity and buddhanature, presented as open-ended, mysterious, and available through shifts in ways of looking.

chapters.title: Soulmaking Practice
chapters.description: He closes by describing how soulmaking, emptiness, and an expanded sense of the senses can dissolve dualism and orient practice toward limitless creation and discovery.

title: Modes of Soulmaking (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if emptiness is not a deflationary view, but a way of opening into mystery, divinity, and a wider range of mystical experience? In this Q&A, the talk moves from the relation between samadhi and emptiness as less-fabricated states, into how imaginal practice can spill into the world as cosmopoiesis, changing how perception itself makes reality feel. It then turns to eros, the pull of image and otherness, and how the path is less about forcing results than trusting what feels most alive as it unfolds in different ways for different people.
short_summary: A Q&A on emptiness, samadhi, eros, and imaginal practice that frames the path as a gradual opening rather than a fixed method.

chapters.title: Emptiness and divinity
chapters.description: He contrasts a deflationary reading of emptiness with an understanding of emptiness as a doorway into mystery, divinity, and multiple kinds of mystical experience.

chapters.title: Samadhi and less fabrication
chapters.description: Rob explains that samadhi and emptiness are related by progressively less fabrication of self, other, and world, and places the classical lists of practice inside that movement.

chapters.title: Cosmopoiesis explained
chapters.description: He defines cosmopoiesis as the way perception helps create the cosmos in a beautiful way, and shows how an imaginal image can spill over into the world.

chapters.title: The tree example
chapters.description: Using an image of a tree, he shows how imaginal qualities can spread in subtle, partial, and gradual ways rather than appearing as a single dramatic shift.

chapters.title: Not making it happen
chapters.description: Rob encourages trusting what is already beautiful and noticing the imaginal rather than forcing results, while also describing the fourfold interplay of self, other, world, and eros.

chapters.title: Trusting eros
chapters.description: In response to questions about directionality and gendered language, he says eros should be followed where it is most alive and allowed to unfold in its own order.

chapters.title: Otherness and merging
chapters.description: He clarifies that eros tends to generate more otherness and multiplicity rather than simple fusion, even when the experience feels like merging with an image.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and Tantra
chapters.description: Rob compares soulmaking with Tantra, noting similarities but also important differences in openness, prescription, and the sense that the tantric field may be effectively infinite.

chapters.title: Hillman and influence
chapters.description: He briefly names James Hillman as an important influence in developing the conceptual language around soulmaking.

title: Longing, Vulnerability, Anteros
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What happens when longing is not treated as a problem to overcome, but as a doorway into relation? The talk moves from a difficult book-writing experience, through imaginal practice and the figure of Anteros, into a wider exploration of how devotion can answer us back, especially when vulnerability, grief, and not-knowing are included. It closes by asking how ordinary things, even tangerines, can become holy through the way we look, bless, and participate in their meaning.
short_summary: A talk on longing, devotion, and vulnerability that moves from personal struggle into Anteros, imaginal practice, and the possibility that sacredness is co-created in relation.

chapters.title: Opening the theme
chapters.description: He introduces longing, devotion, and vulnerability as a shared field where humanity and divinity meet.

chapters.title: Book-writing as doorway
chapters.description: A difficult book project becomes a case study in meeting burden through imaginal practice instead of bypassing it.

chapters.title: Anteros and reciprocity
chapters.description: He names Anteros as reciprocal eros and emphasizes that we are loved by what we love.

chapters.title: Anxiety into image
chapters.description: A more recent project, its anxiety, and the single-mother image show how staying with loss can reveal the soul of the work.

chapters.title: Deity and vulnerability
chapters.description: He widens the teaching through devotional relation to Jesus, the Buddha, Kuan Yin, and Avalokiteshvara.

chapters.title: Alchemy of desire
chapters.description: Longing, devotion, and eros are described as becoming transubstantiated in a mutual relation between self and beloved.

chapters.title: Devotion and image
chapters.description: Devotion is reframed as oriented to image, soul, and an imaginal dimension rather than flat material reality alone.

chapters.title: Sacredness made visible
chapters.description: He closes by saying eros needs twoness and by using tangerines as an example of ordinary things becoming holy.

title: Awakening and Soulmaking (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Can awakening still be possible when long sits, frequent retreats, or a fixed practice style are not available? The talk moves from that practical worry into a widening view of meditation as flexible ways of looking, where insight can arise in walking, in nature, in relationship, and even outside formal practice. From there it turns to jhana, imaginal practice, eros, and devotion, asking how these threads can open both samadhi and a more expansive soulmaking path without reducing any of them to a single formula.
short_summary: A Q&A on practice beyond rigid forms, moving from physical limits and jhana into imaginal work, eros, devotion, and soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: A listener describes the limits of her current practice and asks whether awakening is still possible without long sitting or retreat.

chapters.title: Beyond the cushion
chapters.description: Rob widens the meaning of meditation and argues that realization is not confined to one posture, one retreat format, or one body condition.

chapters.title: Jhana without dogma
chapters.description: He challenges the idea that jhana is a required ladder to insight and speaks cautiously about teacher authority and fixed path models.

chapters.title: The unfabricated nearby
chapters.description: Rob says the perfume of the unfabricated can appear in ordinary life and may bring both faith and liberation in small ways.

chapters.title: Image into samadhi
chapters.description: A question about a recurring imaginal image leads into a discussion of how image, beauty, and energy-body resonance can tip into jhana or samadhi.

chapters.title: Body and image
chapters.description: Another listener asks whether the body can be primary, and Rob treats body, image, and experience as different but fertile starting points.

chapters.title: No single framework
chapters.description: He uses emptiness to argue that science, body-based inquiry, or any other framework can be fruitful without being ultimate.

chapters.title: Eros and devotion
chapters.description: Rob develops eros as a method of inquiry, links it with devotion and bhakti, and describes soulmaking as a process that expands and sacralizes experience.

chapters.title: Closing exchange
chapters.description: The talk ends lightly with a listener's question about bare attention, followed by a brief, quiet close.

title: On Blessed Ground: Fantasies of the Self on the Path
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if practice is not mainly a project of reducing suffering, but a field shaped by fantasy, eros, and the images that give it life? The talk starts by questioning the inherited medical model of Buddhism, then opens into other possible ways of understanding the self on the path: as researcher, artist, adventurer, or lover. Rather than trying to strip fantasy away, it asks whether the fantasies already at work are soulmaking—whether they deepen meaning, aliveness, and the sense of discovery.
short_summary: The talk questions the usual medical frame for practice and explores how fantasy may be the ground of the path, with the real test being whether it is soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Rob introduces the talk as a way to open up another possibility in how practice is understood, rather than adding more content.

chapters.title: Medical model of practice
chapters.description: He describes the common Buddhist frame as a healing project shaped by the Four Noble Truths and the reduction of suffering.

chapters.title: Religion and soul-making
chapters.description: He contrasts a backward-looking religious fantasy with the more open question of what the practitioner actually wants, naming soul-making as a different aim.

chapters.title: Researcher and artist
chapters.description: He offers the self as researcher or artist, where practice becomes discovery and the poetry of perception rather than only cure.

chapters.title: Adventure and lover
chapters.description: He widens the field further with the fantasies of adventurer and lover, each carrying a different sense of time, purpose, and intimacy.

chapters.title: Fantasy as ground
chapters.description: Through a close example, he shows how practice may be animated by several fantasies at once, often without being fully recognized.

chapters.title: Reconstruction and freedom
chapters.description: He considers the fear that seeing fantasy will collapse the path, and argues instead that reconstruction is inevitable and the question is whether it is realist or poetic.

chapters.title: What enlivens practice
chapters.description: He closes by asking what really makes one feel alive in practice, whether the fantasy is soulmaking, and how it imagines completion or ending.

title: Visions of the Beyond
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if the first question about practice is not whether your view is correct, but whether it is soulmaking? The talk begins with simple questions about what you love and want from the path, then moves into a wide-ranging comparison of path-conceptions: classical transcendence, one-dimensional craving, a realism of coping, and tantric or soulmaking ways of opening. Along the way, it keeps returning to eros, fantasy, the senses, and the idea that the path may either narrow life or make it more dimensional and alive.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on how different images of the path shape desire, suffering, and whether practice becomes soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening Questions
chapters.description: He begins with reflective questions about what one loves in practice, what one wants from it, and whether the answers feel complete.

chapters.title: Culture and Self
chapters.description: Burbea turns to how our sense of self and freedom are conditioned by culture, and asks whether our view of awakening is merely inherited or genuinely alive for us.

chapters.title: Soulmaking Test
chapters.description: He reframes authenticity as soulmaking, contrasts this with the Pali canon’s unfabricated goal, and discusses the traditional link between lessening fabrication and ending suffering.

chapters.title: Eros and Beyond
chapters.description: The talk develops the idea that a sense of a beyond can constellate eros or craving, and that fear, self-measurement, and anti-libidinal defenses can block desire.

chapters.title: Horizontal Craving
chapters.description: Burbea explores what happens when eros cannot open into depth, showing how it becomes endless acquisition in a one-dimensional world of more and more.

chapters.title: Four Path Imaginations
chapters.description: He contrasts the realist, existential path with other images of the path, including the medical, religious, scientific, and artistic fantasies.

chapters.title: Tantra and Imaginal
chapters.description: Burbea presents a tantric view of awakening in which emptiness, appearances, wisdom, means, and divine appearances are held together in an open-ended way.

chapters.title: Open-Ended Awakening
chapters.description: He shows how soulmaking and tantra keep awakening open-ended, with many possible directions rather than a single finish line.

chapters.title: Questions to Carry
chapters.description: The talk closes by asking listeners to notice their dominant fantasy of practice, what drives their devotion, and whether the soul loves soulmaking.

title: Energy Body and Image (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you work with experience when the body feels split between something wide, pleasant, and subtle, and something else contracted, jerky, or hard to breathe into? The talk moves from clarifying what is meant by the energy body to showing how breath, attention, and imagination can soften contraction, open movement, and shift what feels pleasant or difficult. It then turns toward imaginal practice: how images arise, how they can be received without grasping, and how relationship itself shapes what unfolds.
short_summary: A question-and-answer talk on the energy body, breath, and imaginal practice, centered on how attention and relationship shape experience.

chapters.title: Opening Question
chapters.description: A listener asks about the energy body, breath body, astral body, and the felt difference between subtle pleasantness and a more contracted physical experience.

chapters.title: Energy Body as Relation
chapters.description: Rob reframes the energy body as a way of looking, sensing, and relating, rather than a separate object or realm.

chapters.title: Maps and Dependent Arising
chapters.description: He uses different chakra systems and body maps to show how concepts fabricate experience and remain useful only when they serve exploration.

chapters.title: Working with Restlessness
chapters.description: The talk turns practical: if something feels stuck or restless, one can use attention, imagination, or breath to let energy move more freely.

chapters.title: Meeting Drowning Sensations
chapters.description: Rob addresses fear and shallow breathing with care, emphasizing gentle attention, kindness, and soothing the person who feels overwhelmed.

chapters.title: How Imaginal Perception Arises
chapters.description: Using sunlight, metta, and poetic language, he describes how ordinary perception can become imaginal when warmth, meaning, and receptivity come together.

chapters.title: Loosening Conceptual Grip
chapters.description: He expands on the conditions for imaginal life: loosened concepts, humility, reverence, and a more total involvement of the energy body.

chapters.title: Samadhi and Fabrication
chapters.description: In the final major section, Rob contrasts samadhi as less fabrication with imaginal practice as a subtler kind of fabrication that can still deepen and open.

chapters.title: Pīti and Relationship
chapters.description: He closes by discussing pīti, resistance, and the way relationship shapes body, world, emotion, mind, image, and energy.

title: Intention and Devotion (Guided Meditation)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if meditation is not just about settling down, but about sensing where life wants to open into sacredness? The talk moves from the contrast between simplifying practices and the more complex, generative movement of imaginal practice, then turns into a guided exercise of standing, walking, sensing the body, and letting intention become a quiet devotion. It closes by emphasizing that intention is impermanent and must be returned to gently, like tending a flame, while care for the heart and body is kept alive in movement.
short_summary: A guided meditation on intention and devotion that invites standing, walking, and sensing the body as a way of opening to more sacredness, then ends with a gentle reminder to keep tending the flame of practice.

chapters.title: Renunciation and desire
chapters.description: Burbea begins by contrasting simplifying practices with the more generative movement of opening to desire and imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Imaginal complexity
chapters.description: He describes imaginal practice as erotic, proliferating, and complexifying, with images generating deeper dimensions and sacredness.

chapters.title: The body becomes sacred
chapters.description: The talk turns to how the body can be re-imagined as object, subject, and locus of knowing, not just something functional or culturally defined.

chapters.title: Movement and stillness
chapters.description: Burbea opens meditation beyond strict stillness, allowing walking, gentle motion, or inward movement in stillness if it feels soulful.

chapters.title: Intention toward sacredness
chapters.description: He names intention as the wish to discover more sacredness in practice and in life, and invites the group into a shared exercise.

chapters.title: Standing exercise
chapters.description: Participants are guided to feel rootedness, heart, and energy body, then to sense longing and devotion as an orientation toward sacredness.

chapters.title: Walking with holy fire
chapters.description: The exercise expands into open-sense walking, with the intention to let all that is perceived be seen as sacred, including others and the room itself.

chapters.title: Returning to the flame
chapters.description: After a period of silence, he speaks about intention as impermanent and the need to return gently, maintaining care for body, heart, and soul.

chapters.title: Bowing and closing
chapters.description: The talk ends with bowing to the sacredness one wants to know, and a final reminder not to lose that care while rearranging the room.

title: Opening to Desire and the Imaginal (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you work with desire when it seems to open into something spacious, then tighten around an image, or disappear altogether? The talk moves through a series of questions about the energy body, imaginal practice, depression, fear of being flooded by images, and the way eros can show up either as strong feeling or as something very subtle. The speaker keeps returning to a practical thread: notice the fork in the road, learn when to stay with an image, when to rest in the body, and when to loosen back into a wider field. By the end, the emphasis is less on fixing a problem than on sensing how different conditions bring the whole field back to life.
short_summary: A Q&A on opening to desire, where the talk follows how eros, image, and the energy body interact, and how practice shifts between them.

chapters.title: Contraction Around Image
chapters.description: A question about spacious energy-body practice leads into the problem of contraction when images arise.

chapters.title: Holding Both Experiences
