chapters.description: Rob links the difficulty to Catherine McGee’s two-ness exercise and suggests learning to hold spaciousness and contraction together.

chapters.title: Images and Soulmaking
chapters.description: He explains that images can become alive imaginally and that the apparent problem may not lie where it first seems.

chapters.title: Desire, Depression, Reification
chapters.description: The talk turns to lack of desire, inner critic, depression, and how blocked or reified experience can interrupt eros and imaginal unfolding.

chapters.title: Fear Of Flooding
chapters.description: A question about opening awareness widely brings discussion of whether receptivity risks being overwhelmed by painful images.

chapters.title: Pain and Compassion
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes the imaginal realm from the realities of world pain and says the wider work requires emotional skill and compassion practice.

chapters.title: Opening To Desire
chapters.description: He returns to the day’s instruction, describing how opening to desire can lead into an imaginal realm of happiness rather than a single image.

chapters.title: Finding The Deeper Wanting
chapters.description: A long exchange about bodily pain and perfectionism explores how desire may point to ease, meaning, or a more beautiful aspect of a pattern.

chapters.title: Eros, Conditions, Subtlety
chapters.description: Rob closes by relating eros to the imaginal, noting that practice forks under different conditions and that mature practice becomes more subtle and sensitive.

title: Instructions: Opening to the Current of Desire
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: A desire is traced back from its fixed object to the deeper current underneath it. The talk lays out a three-step practice: notice dukkha or desire, ask what is really wanted, and then open the body to the energetic movement of that desire rather than clinging to the image. Through several examples, the speaker shows how this can lead either into imaginal experience or into a more spacious sense of being, sometimes without any outer change at all.
short_summary: A practical teaching on moving from fixation on desire’s object to opening into the deeper current underneath it.

chapters.title: Practice threads
chapters.description: Rob sets the retreat context by naming the main threads of practice and where desire fits among them.

chapters.title: Introducing desire practice
chapters.description: He distinguishes imaginal eros from the new practice of opening to the current of desire and emphasizes the role of trust.

chapters.title: Three-step method
chapters.description: Rob gives the basic sequence: notice dukkha or desire, ask what is really wanted, and trust that desire may hold something worthwhile.

chapters.title: Opening the body
chapters.description: He explains how to open bodily and energetically to desire rather than merely observing it mindfully.

chapters.title: Freedom and joining
chapters.description: Two examples show desire opening into felt freedom from constraint and then into a sense of joining that does not depend on the object.

chapters.title: Craving and hype
chapters.description: A new-romance example leads into a more standard Dharma analysis of craving, papancha, and the mind’s exaggeration of here and there.

chapters.title: Deeper wants
chapters.description: Rob works through a case where the surface object gives way to wider wants such as being heard, heart-opening, connection, and love.

chapters.title: Lack and fabrication
chapters.description: He warns that desire is often entangled with lack, grief, and frustration, and that attention can help fabricate the whole pattern.

chapters.title: Trusting the current
chapters.description: He closes by restating the practice and noting that opening to desire may lead into imaginal manifestation or into a fuller internal fulfillment, with action sometimes relevant and sometimes not.

title: Clinging, Craving, Eros (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if clinging is not something to eliminate, but a range of push-pull movements that can become either contracted and suffering or open and creative? In this question-and-answer session, the talk moves from defining craving, clinging, and eros to practical ways of working with them: bringing in the energy body, loosening the sense of reality, and letting images become more imaginal. Later, it widens into emptiness, soulmaking, and the possibility of relating to anger, old patterns, and even one’s own history as part of a larger transformation rather than a problem to be fixed.
short_summary: A Q&A on clinging, craving, and eros that moves from definition into practice, imagination, and the transformation of difficult energies.

chapters.title: Clinging and eros
chapters.description: Rob defines clinging broadly and distinguishes contractive craving from the opening quality of eros.

chapters.title: Fabrication and suffering
chapters.description: He places clinging in dependent origination, discusses papancha, and asks whether clinging is always unwholesome.

chapters.title: Transforming craving
chapters.description: Rob explains two main levers for shifting craving: involving the whole energy body and loosening the reality of the object through emptiness and imaginal perception.

chapters.title: Emptiness without forcing
chapters.description: He widens the frame to include non-fabrication, samadhi, and metta, and encourages a flexible, non-obsessive relationship to dissolution and old patterns.

chapters.title: Soul and self images
chapters.description: Rob suggests that self-images can become imaginal over time and that practice can move gently rather than by force.

chapters.title: Anger as alchemy
chapters.description: A question about anger opens into tantric transformation: letting the image carry the emotion, trusting it, and finding what is alive and potent in it.

chapters.title: How to recognize imaginal
chapters.description: Rob responds to examples of light, story, and recurring figures, and clarifies that imaginal practice is recognized by its soulmaking qualities, not by the image alone.

chapters.title: Relational images
chapters.description: He finishes by noting that some images are brief while others become long-term relationships, and that the imaginal can spread into life and the wider world.

title: Instructions: Letting Go
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Letting go is presented here not as a single move, but as one thread among several: nourishment, emotional care, imaginal practice, and renunciation all have to be woven together. The talk then lays out four ways of working with desire and craving: return to what nourishes, look beneath desire to the feeling underneath, give craving space until it peaks and passes, and reflect on whether the object will really satisfy. In the later part, the speaker turns to impermanence and then to a practical exercise with mealtimes and the lunch queue, using food to notice craving, fear of scarcity, and the difference between raw sensation and the mind’s story.
short_summary: A practical talk on letting go that moves from nourishment and emotional care to craving, impermanence, and a close investigation of desire during meals.

chapters.title: Expanding the path
chapters.description: He introduces the retreat’s larger frame: practice can include samadhi, emotional care, and imaginal work, and the art is learning how to move among these threads.

chapters.title: Renunciation as a thread
chapters.description: He adds renunciation to the picture and distinguishes between trusting desire at times and deliberately cooling things down by letting them go.

chapters.title: Return to nourishment
chapters.description: The first way of working with desire is to come back to what nourishes, such as loving-kindness and samadhi, especially when grasping comes from a low reservoir.

chapters.title: Care for hidden pain
chapters.description: He suggests that desire often covers loneliness, grief, boredom, or heartache, and that turning toward those emotions with care can soften the pull.

chapters.title: Give craving space
chapters.description: The third possibility is to notice craving as dukkha, feel its pressure in the body, and create enough space for it to rise and subside without being acted on.

chapters.title: Reflect on desire
chapters.description: He offers reflective practices: asking whether the object is really wanted, remembering how often satisfaction has been partial, and seeing how impermanence changes the picture.

chapters.title: Impermanence and dukkha
chapters.description: He broadens the view with impermanence and a temporary dukkha-based way of looking, using them as contemplative gears that support letting go.

chapters.title: Eating as practice
chapters.description: He ends with a practical exercise for lunch: watch craving in the queue, notice fear and taking, and pay close attention to taste, smell, and the gap between sensation and story.

title: Eros and Equanimity in the Movements of Soul
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How does desire deepen practice without collapsing into grasping, realism, or imbalance? The talk develops a framework in which eros, imaginal perception, and concepts mutually fertilize one another, widening experience when they are not blocked by rigid views, aversion, or a refusal of image. It then turns to the tension between eros and equanimity, asking how the movement of desire can remain balanced, spacious, and sustainable. Near the end, the speaker returns to the key middle way of seeing image as image, and shows how that stance can open both potency and trust in practice.
short_summary: A careful exploration of eros, imaginal perception, and equanimity, centered on how a middle way of seeing can keep desire vivid without becoming rigid or out of balance.

chapters.title: Opening the Terms
chapters.description: Burbea begins by explaining why soul and soul-making resist fixed definition and why that resistance may be appropriate.

chapters.title: What Soul-Making Means
chapters.description: He offers a working definition of soul-making as a dynamic relationship with experience that expands sensing, conceiving, and imaginal ways of knowing.

chapters.title: The Shape of Eros
chapters.description: He describes eros as desire for more intimacy and connection, with qualities like juiciness, arousal, beauty, and an attraction that carries longing for more.

chapters.title: Eros-Psyche-Logos
chapters.description: Burbea lays out the three-part dynamic of eros, imaginal perception, and concepts, showing how they can deepen and enrich one another.

chapters.title: Otherness and Beyondness
chapters.description: He distinguishes eros from metta, then argues that eros depends on twoness and creates more otherness without dismissing present appearance.

chapters.title: Self, World, and Eros
chapters.description: The imaginal field widens to include the self and the world, and he shows how attention can either support or block that expansion.

chapters.title: Staying Balanced
chapters.description: Using a clinical example, Burbea distinguishes intensity from potency and shows how opening attention can keep eros sustainable and less constricted.

chapters.title: Eros and Equanimity
chapters.description: He compares eros with equanimity, noting that equanimity quiets fabrication while eros can support skillful fabrication grounded in emptiness.

chapters.title: Seeing Image as Image
chapters.description: Burbea closes with the middle way between real and unreal, including Mother Teresa as a tentative example, and ends by stressing trust in the imaginal without reifying it.

title: Faith in Soulmaking (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you stay faithful to one vivid image without closing down the wider imaginal field? The talk moves through that tension in a question-and-answer format, showing how a single image can deepen, how multiple images can appear without “unfaithfulness,” and how soulmaking tends to make things more manifold rather than more fixed. It then turns to the path itself, suggesting that devotion, meaning, and calling can be related to as erotic and imaginal even when they are not visual or easily defined, before ending with practical questions about analysis, heart, and how to keep a practice alive without boxing it in.
short_summary: A Q&A on staying with images, understanding eros and soulmaking, and using analysis without flattening the imaginal process.

chapters.title: One image, many ways
chapters.description: A question about staying with a single image opens into guidance on fidelity, openness, and the possibility that the relationship itself may change without changing the image.

chapters.title: Manifold imaginal life
chapters.description: Burbea explains that having many images is not unfaithfulness and uses religious figures like Buddha, Jesus, Mary, and saints to show how soulmaking multiplies and complicates images.

chapters.title: Eros in the path
chapters.description: He connects erotic imagination to devotion, meaningfulness, and the way the path itself can become a living, charged relationship rather than an abstract aim.

chapters.title: Working with vague images
chapters.description: The discussion turns to images that are not visually clear, and Burbea describes how one can relate to a pull, let the being open, and allow the image to form without forcing it.

chapters.title: Intellect and image
chapters.description: A question about interpretation leads to a careful defense of analysis as potentially fertile, so long as it does not box the image into a finished meaning.

chapters.title: Desire, autonomy, heart
chapters.description: Burbea reflects on desire having an object, the autonomy of images, the meaning of 'heart,' and the possibility that soul is a way of relating rather than a thing.

chapters.title: Analysis as practice
chapters.description: He compares practice to surfing or cycling, emphasizing timing, sensitivity, and the lack of any single formula for when analysis helps or hinders.

chapters.title: From craving to eros
chapters.description: The closing section returns to how energy body, humility, and reverence can shift a contracted craving toward a more expansive erotic relation, followed by a quiet ending before tea.

title: Eros in the Imaginal (Guided Meditation)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you work with an image that is more than visual, more than personal, and more than a tool for metta? The talk first carefully sets out what counts as imaginal: something sensed in relationship, with resonance, mystery, autonomy, beauty, and even a quality of sacredness, rather than a merely imagined picture. It then moves into a long guided meditation that begins with the energy body, invites an image to arise or be chosen, and explores different kinds of contact—gazing, identifying, witnessing, and the possibility of erotic or other forms of communion—before ending with practical ways to enter imaginal work from dreams, emotion, the body, or the room itself.
short_summary: A guided exploration of imaginal practice that starts with the energy body and then opens into relationship with images, including subtle and erotic forms of contact.

chapters.title: What Imaginal Means
chapters.description: Burbea defines imaginal practice as more than visualization, more than something purely internal, and more than something only visual.

chapters.title: Settling Into Practice
chapters.description: He invites the group into posture and stillness before opening attention to the body and the energy field.

chapters.title: Energy Body and Samadhi
chapters.description: The meditation begins with sensing the energy body, tending well-being, and using a light, caring mode of attention.

chapters.title: Letting An Image Form
chapters.description: An image is invited to arise, either from looseness, difficulty, or deliberate choice, and to be met in relationship.

chapters.title: Ways Of Relating
chapters.description: He explores several modes of contact with the image: as a dyad, by identifying with it, by witnessing it, or by noticing mutual gaze and exchange.

chapters.title: Eros And Transformation
chapters.description: The practice opens into being with, moving with, and potentially sexual contact, while staying rooted in sensitivity and non-hierarchy.

chapters.title: Primary Sense And Resonance
chapters.description: Attention turns to the image's dominant sense modality, its element, solidity, and the emotional and bodily resonance it evokes.

chapters.title: Choosing And Receiving Images
chapters.description: He closes by explaining that images may arrive or be chosen, and that practice can begin from dream images, people, the room, emotions, emptiness, or the energy body.

title: The Problem of Desire
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if desire is not one thing, but three different movements with very different effects? The talk starts by questioning the simple idea that desire is the problem, then turns to the Buddha’s distinction between wholesome and unwholesome desire, before widening into a critique of reducing awakening to mere relief from unpleasantness. From there it moves into a careful reworking of craving, clinging, and eros, with meditation and ways of looking used to show how clinging fabricates experience and how eros can open into intimacy, beauty, and soulmaking.
short_summary: A close examination of desire that distinguishes craving, clinging, and eros, and connects eros to soulmaking, emptiness, and the deeper possibilities of practice.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces desire and soulmaking as central, inexhaustible themes and frames the talk as an entry into a complex topic.

chapters.title: Culture and confusion
chapters.description: He looks at modern culture, consumerism, and the self, then turns to the mixed messages practitioners often inherit about desire.

chapters.title: Wholesome and unwholesome
chapters.description: Using the Buddha's distinction between skillful and unskillful desire, he shows how teachings can be oversimplified or turned back to front.

chapters.title: The path is not thin
chapters.description: He questions the idea that awakening is simply having no desires, especially around romance, beauty, senses, and the wish to live with integrity.

chapters.title: Beyond relief
chapters.description: Burbea challenges a reduced view of awakening as mere relief from unpleasantness and contrasts it with truth, beauty, mystery, and the transcendent.

chapters.title: Three kinds of desire
chapters.description: He proposes a working distinction between craving, clinging, and eros, then begins to show how each unfolds differently in practice.

chapters.title: Clinging and emptiness
chapters.description: This section develops clinging as subtle push-pull, traces it through ways of looking, and connects letting go with dependent origination and emptiness rather than with a fantasy of no clinging.

chapters.title: Defining eros
chapters.description: He offers a provisional definition of eros as wanting more contact, intimacy, and opening, distinguishes it from pleasure-seeking and other theories, and places it in the territory of the imaginal.

chapters.title: Eros, path, and sacredness
chapters.description: The talk closes by linking eros to soulmaking, sexuality, sacredness, and the way our concepts themselves shape what practice becomes for us.

title: From Mindfulness to Divinity: Towards the Tracing of a Phenomenology of Soul (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What happens when mindfulness keeps widening until it touches eros, imagination, and the sense that anything perceived is already fabricated? The talk traces a path from dependent origination and emptiness into states of less fabrication, then into the way divinity, soulmaking, and the imaginal can arise as lived experience rather than abstract belief. From there it turns to the self, the beloved, and the world as participating in a double movement: toward transcendence and toward the rich particularity of manifestation.
short_summary: A phenomenological exploration of how emptiness, eros, and soulmaking open into shifting experiences of divinity, the imaginal, and the self.

chapters.title: Phenomenology of practice
chapters.description: Burbea frames meditation as an inquiry into how ways of looking fabricate experience and how practice can gradually lessen fabrication.

chapters.title: Stages of divinity
chapters.description: He describes different kinds of oneness and divinity, from universal love and consciousness to the unfabricated and emptiness.

chapters.title: Opening the imaginal
chapters.description: Seeing the emptiness of all things loosens the real/unreal split and creates room to explore the imaginal without crude reduction.

chapters.title: Eros and soulmaking
chapters.description: He turns to eros, fantasy, and soulmaking as active forces in ordinary life and as a practical guide for working with blocked or limited practice.

chapters.title: The self as image
chapters.description: Using Corbin, Panagiotis Nellas, and Meister Eckhart, he develops the image of the self as a divine image or theophany rather than a flat identity.

chapters.title: Beauty and divine mind
chapters.description: Aquinas and the phrase 'my soul doth magnify the Lord' are used to show how beauty, soul, and divinity arise together in perception.

chapters.title: Two movements of eros
chapters.description: Burbea contrasts eros toward the unfabricated with eros toward soulmaking fabrication, and names the distortions or near enemies of each movement.

chapters.title: Dependent origination and matter
chapters.description: A philosophical question about matter leads into dependent origination, subject-object-time, and the claim that these teachings are not temporal explanations.

chapters.title: Eternity and time
chapters.description: He returns to the idea that phenomenology can arrive at what sounds like metaphysics: eternity, divine mind, and the productions of time participating in one another.

title: From Mindfulness to Divinity: Towards the Tracing of a Phenomenology of Soul (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What happens when mindfulness is widened enough to notice that eros, fantasy, and soul-making are not distractions but part of the texture of being human? The talk moves from that opening claim into a close reading of image, showing how the imaginal can feel theatrical, mysterious, timeless, and relational, and how it can reshape the sense of self, other, and world. From there, it turns to the tension between reducing suffering and allowing soul-making, then widens again into a phenomenological account of divinity, the unfabricated, and the ways practice can open both transcendent and immanent dimensions of experience.
short_summary: An extended exploration of eros, fantasy, soul-making, and divinity, moving from the imaginal life of practice to the question of how experience, suffering, and the unfabricated relate.

chapters.title: Eros and soul-making
chapters.description: He begins by saying that meaningfulness, devotion, eros, fantasy, and soul-making are not optional extras but recurring features of human life.

chapters.title: What image reveals
chapters.description: He unpacks the imaginal as theatrical, timeless, mysterious, and relational, with love, eros, and sacredness implicit in it.

chapters.title: Subtle fantasies in practice
chapters.description: He shows how life and fantasy interpenetrate, how ordinary moods and identities can hide imaginal structures, and how practice can call forth rather than impose images.

chapters.title: Life as image
chapters.description: The talk widens the frame so that meditation, relationship, work, and culture can all be sensed as part of a single imaginal field.

chapters.title: The Buddha as fantasy
chapters.description: He turns to the ways practitioners construct a Buddha, arguing that this imaginative relationship can be more fruitful than a flat historical one.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: He asks how we choose between perspectives, contrasting the usual Buddhist aim of reducing suffering with the added question of what brings soul-making.

chapters.title: Pathos and suffering
chapters.description: He explores how soul-making can involve tension, pathos, and even pain, drawing on James Hillman and Ajahn Chah to keep the issue unsettled.

chapters.title: Suffering and interpretation
chapters.description: He examines how retreat culture, discipline, and ideas of purification or awakening can wrap suffering into fantasy, and how the meanings of dukkha vary.

chapters.title: What divinity means
chapters.description: He distinguishes divinity as something inexhaustible, relational, and beyond full capture, with experiences and concepts both unfolding over time.

title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if desire is not mainly a problem to be suppressed, but a way of inquiry that can open into soulmaking? The talk moves from questioning assumptions about craving and selfing, to distinguishing eros from papañca, to showing how an unavailable beloved or a teacher-fantasy can become an imaginal opening rather than just something to let go of. It then widens into a detailed argument that desire can arise from abundance, already be present in the imaginal, and even be transformed by how it is seen, held, and understood.
short_summary: A wide-ranging exploration of desire that distinguishes eros from craving and shows how inquiry, imaginal practice, and careful ways of looking can turn longing into soulmaking.

chapters.title: Inquiry as invitation
chapters.description: He begins by emphasizing permission to question, experiment, and uncover assumptions for oneself.

chapters.title: Reframing desire
chapters.description: He revisits the earlier practice of opening to a deeper current of desire and how it unsettles common views of selfing and defilement.

chapters.title: Eros and the imaginal
chapters.description: He distinguishes eros from craving and shows how imaginal perception can expand what feels stuck or impossible.

chapters.title: Peace without flattening
chapters.description: He argues that allowing eros can bring equanimity and spaciousness without losing soul or depth.

chapters.title: Teacher fantasy and soulmaking
chapters.description: He turns to teacher-student longing, keeps the boundary clear, and uses a student’s letter to show how attraction can reveal a whole world.

chapters.title: Psyche loves itself
chapters.description: He develops the idea that psyche is drawn to its own treasures and that boundaries can form a temenos or alchemical vessel.

chapters.title: Desire beyond lack
chapters.description: He questions the idea that desire comes from deficiency and explores longing, trust, and the possibility that desire is already flowing in the imaginal.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: He reviews several practices that transform desire through imaginal perception, including divine influx and hierophantic time.

chapters.title: Alchemy of desire
chapters.description: He closes by distinguishing many forms of desire, then gathers them into an alchemy in which craving can become eros and transubstantiation.

title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: When desire shows up as dukkha, is it possible to ask what it really wants rather than only trying to manage the craving? The talk unfolds through a personal story of political upset, then two concrete examples—romance and intellectual hunger—to show how deeper wanting can be felt in the body, opened to, and sometimes revealed as freedom, connection, inquiry, or love. It also pauses to distinguish this from simple acceptance: the speaker keeps returning to preconceptions, lack, posture, and the need for compassion when opening becomes frightening.
short_summary: A talk on working with desire by asking what it really wants, opening to its deeper current, and distinguishing that from both craving and generic acceptance.

chapters.title: Introducing the inquiry
chapters.description: He opens by situating the talk as a further exploration of desire and a different approach from what has come before.

chapters.title: Origin story
chapters.description: He расскаts the Gaia House episode where asking what he really wanted revealed a desire for freedom from constraint.

chapters.title: Three-step practice
chapters.description: He sets out the core method: unhook desire from its obvious object, clear preconceptions, and feel the deeper current in the whole being.

chapters.title: Examples of craving
chapters.description: He uses a romance interview and related reflections to show how craving amplifies difference and how deeper desire can be uncovered.

chapters.title: Desire becomes inquiry
chapters.description: A second autobiographical example shows how pressure and impatience around study can open into joy in ongoing inquiry.

chapters.title: Desire and lack
chapters.description: He pauses to note that desire is often mixed with lack and that sometimes distress or grief needs to be met first.

chapters.title: Experiment, don't flatten
chapters.description: He stresses flexibility, warns against reducing the teaching to simple acceptance, and encourages repeated experimentation until the method becomes a usable tool.

chapters.title: Relation to imaginal practice
chapters.description: He closes by contrasting this practice with imaginal work, then widens back out to clinging, dependent origination, and emptiness.

title: Eros and the Brahmaviharas
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What happens when loving-kindness is no longer treated as the whole field of love, and eros is allowed to meet it instead? The talk carefully separates eros from metta, compassion, joy, and equanimity, then shows where they overlap and where they move in different directions: one complexifies and personalizes, the other simplifies and loosens fabrication. From there it becomes a practical inquiry into how erotic imaginal work can support compassion, steady the heart before suffering, and open new possibilities for practice without replacing the Brahmaviharas.
short_summary: The talk distinguishes eros from the Brahmaviharas and explores how erotic imaginal practice can be joined with metta, compassion, and equanimity in a way that deepens both.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea explains why he wants to spell out the topic more fully and sets up the question of eros in relation to the Brahmaviharas.

chapters.title: Eros and imaginal love
chapters.description: He defines eros in the imaginal realm as a form of love with attraction, beauty, and contact, and distinguishes it from simple imaginative support in mettā practice.

chapters.title: Shared ground with the Brahmaviharas
chapters.description: The talk covers important overlaps, including care, equanimity, and love directed toward phenomena as well as beings.

chapters.title: How eros differs
chapters.description: He clarifies that eros includes desire, delight, beauty, and a love for uniqueness, while mettā remains unconditional and not based on attraction.

chapters.title: Particularity and the modern self
chapters.description: Burbea explores personal love for particulars, the modern sense of self, and why uniqueness may need to be loved rather than erased.

chapters.title: Receiving love
chapters.description: He describes the difficulty of receiving love, ambivalence about being seen, and the role of trust, pacing, and imaginal figures in opening to a loving gaze.

chapters.title: Less and more fabrication
chapters.description: The discussion turns to how the Brahmaviharas simplify self and other, while eros complicates and enriches them, often bringing cosmic ease and expanded compassion.

chapters.title: Combining practice
chapters.description: Burbea suggests that eros, including sexual energy, can be brought into mettā and compassion practice in creative ways that add energy and range rather than distraction.

chapters.title: Eros as fuel
chapters.description: He closes by showing how erotic imaginal practice can support compassion, bliss, samādhi, and equanimity, while widening the sense of sacredness that practice can disclose.

title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What happens when the impulse toward simplicity becomes a shadow that closes off self, sexuality, and body? The talk begins by questioning the habit of treating insight as if it only illuminates, then follows how every view also casts blind spots—using the Four Noble Truths, no-self, and renunciation as examples of teachings that are liberating but double-edged. From there it turns toward recent openness around sexual orientation and gender, imaginal practice, and the way eros can widen perception of the body into something more multifaceted, sensual, and alive. Near the end, the speaker contrasts a narrow, reductionist picture of the body with a view of it as endlessly revealing, a field of possible ways of knowing.
short_summary: The talk examines the shadow side of simplification in Dharma and moves into an expansive account of how soulmaking, eros, and imaginal practice can open body, sexuality, and identity into greater complexity.

chapters.title: Shadow of Insight
chapters.description: Burbea defines shadow as the blind spot that any insight or perspective inevitably casts.

chapters.title: Sexuality and Complexity
chapters.description: He returns to the earlier discussion of sexuality as a historical and psychic deepening of selfhood, drawing on Foucault.

chapters.title: Against Simplism
chapters.description: He criticizes a lazy form of Dharma that uses no-self and renunciation to avoid engagement with complexity.

chapters.title: Gender and Opening
chapters.description: He suggests that cultural shifts around gender and sexual orientation can be understood as soulmaking movements.

chapters.title: Body as Expressive Field
chapters.description: The talk turns toward the body, energy body, movement, and the ways imaginal practice can widen perception.

chapters.title: Erotic Perception
chapters.description: Burbea explores how eros can alter the way the body appears, using examples from imagination, beauty, and lived context.

chapters.title: Freud and Polymorphous Eros
chapters.description: He contrasts soulmaking with Freud’s account of infant sexuality and the contraction of erotic possibility through training.

chapters.title: Infinite Modes of Knowing
chapters.description: The body is described as endlessly revealing, with more possible ways of sensing, knowing, and conceiving than modern reductionism allows.

chapters.title: Body, Soul, and View
chapters.description: He ends by treating the body as a soulscape and a locus of epistemic potential, asking what soulmaking wants to open next.

title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How does sexuality change when it is no longer treated as a fixed fact, but as something shaped by view, culture, and imagination? The talk moves from reductionist ways of talking about sex and love, through stories of Dharma communities and inherited moral judgments, into a sustained reading of the Inanna and Dumuzi myth as a contrasting image of sacred, fertile lovemaking. From there it asks whether sex can be soulmaking rather than merely distracting, and whether a more open, less contracted way of perceiving can reveal holiness, dimensionality, and participation in the cosmic. The final movement turns to modern identity and Foucault, asking how sexuality became so tightly bound up with selfhood, and whether kindness and questioning can open a different relationship to it.
short_summary: The talk traces sexuality from reductionist and moralizing views toward a soulmaking, imaginal understanding shaped by openness, myth, and the way of looking.

chapters.title: Sex as a view
chapters.description: The talk begins by framing sexuality as empty and dependently arisen, not a fixed thing independent of how it is seen.

chapters.title: Reduction and reaction
chapters.description: Rob gives examples of reductionist and dismissive views of love and sex, including scientific, moral, and Dharma contexts.

chapters.title: Inherited moral frames
chapters.description: He reflects on Christian and Buddhist moral inheritances and how they shape what people judge, hide, or condemn around sexuality.

chapters.title: Questioning the binaries
chapters.description: He challenges the idea that sex is simply a distraction or that one must choose between sensuality and the unfabricated.

chapters.title: Inanna and Dumuzi
chapters.description: A long mythic passage presents lovemaking as sacred, fertile, and participatory in something larger than the private self.

chapters.title: Cosmic sexuality
chapters.description: He interprets the Inanna material as a vision of sexuality tied to divinity, abundance, and the fertility of the world.

chapters.title: Sex and soulmaking
chapters.description: The talk turns to intentions, ego, pleasure, stress release, and the possibility that sex can become soulmaking through imaginal openness.

chapters.title: Foucault and identity
chapters.description: Rob uses Foucault to trace how modern culture made sexuality more central to identity, diagnosis, and self-scrutiny.

chapters.title: Kindness in inquiry
chapters.description: He ends by returning to the practical test of whether a way of relating is soulmaking and emphasizes kindness over rigidity.

title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the problem is not too much desire, but how quickly desire is pathologized and flattened into ideas of balance, health, or bare attention? The talk moves from intense spiritual longing, through examples like Thomas Merton and the Buddha, into a critique of modern Dharma assumptions about sensuality, practice, and the senses. It then opens into a wider argument about beauty, eros, soulmaking, and why a richer view of fabrication may matter for art, nature, and the ecological imagination.
short_summary: A detailed argument that eros, beauty, and the senses belong on the path, and that narrow views of practice can flatten both spiritual longing and lived experience.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: He introduces the talk as a way of widening questions about body, sensuality, and sexuality rather than settling them.

chapters.title: Intensity and pathologizing
chapters.description: He argues that strong eros, whether spiritual, artistic, or sexual, is often misread as extreme or unhealthy.

chapters.title: Merton and total commitment
chapters.description: Thomas Merton's longing to give himself to God is used to show how devotion can look excessive from the outside.

chapters.title: Moderation and its limits
chapters.description: He questions the cultural elevation of balance and psychological health when it becomes a way of shrinking soulmaking.

chapters.title: Eros and the senses
chapters.description: He begins to connect eros with body, sensuality, and sexuality as fields of potentially endless opening.

chapters.title: Three views of practice
chapters.description: He lays out three ways of relating to sense experience: bare attention, transcendence beyond the senses, and a soulmaking or imaginal approach.

chapters.title: Beauty, depth, and belonging
chapters.description: He develops beauty as more than pleasantness, tying it to depth, intimacy, and the feeling of belonging to something larger.

chapters.title: Practice styles and flatland
chapters.description: He contrasts Mahasi-style insight and non-doing approaches with a view that preserves mystery, otherness, and erotic possibility.

chapters.title: Fabrication and sacredness
chapters.description: He closes by linking beauty to fabrication, poetry, nature, and ecology, arguing that recognizing illusion need not diminish the sacred.

title: The World and More: Immanence, Tantra, and Transcendence (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if eros is not a distraction from awakening but part of its movement, reshaping how a tradition understands the path, the body, and the world? The talk moves through Buddhism, Tantra, Christianity, Kabbalah, and a few intimate examples to test three possibilities at once: that erotic language is symbolic, that it is a vehicle, and that it may also be a real re-sanctification of sexuality itself. Again and again, the speaker returns to the same pressure point: whether practice should strip the world down to literal meaning, or open it into a layered, imaginal, and cosmically participatory field.
short_summary: A wide-ranging exploration of eros, tantra, and Jewish and Christian mysticism that asks whether sexuality and practice can be read as symbols, vehicles, or acts of re-sanctification.

chapters.title: Eros at the Center
chapters.description: He opens by naming eros as central to tantric symbolism, re-enchantment, and the relationship with sense experience and awakening.

chapters.title: Origins or Telos
chapters.description: Using Nietzsche and historical examples, he contrasts the myth of origins with a teleological way of reading religious development.

chapters.title: Tradition as Growth
chapters.description: Michelangelo and Buddhist history are used to suggest that traditions may be moving toward something fuller rather than decaying from an original purity.

chapters.title: Sophia and Imaginal Prayer
chapters.description: Gichtel and Thomas Merton illustrate a Christian line in which feminine divine presence permeates the world and devotion becomes imaginal participation.

chapters.title: Ambiguous Sacred Sex
chapters.description: Gnostic and tantric materials are read as deliberately multivalent, raising the question of whether sexual imagery is symbolic, literal, or a doorway into practice.

chapters.title: Three Tantric Readings
chapters.description: He lays out symbolic, instrumental, and re-sanctifying readings of tantra, then argues that all three are active in a soulmaking approach.

chapters.title: Sun, Moon, and Cosmos
chapters.description: An imaginal episode with a woman, a heart-sun, and later a moon image becomes a way of describing erotic polarity as cosmic harmony rather than personal psychology.

chapters.title: Sex as Divine Mystery
chapters.description: With the Hermetic Asclepius and related material, he argues that sexual union can reveal and participate in divine and cosmic dimensions when seen reverently.

chapters.title: Ritual, World, and Thirst
chapters.description: Kabbalah and the Zohar are used to show ritual as world-making, and the talk ends by holding together the sacred world and the pull beyond it into the transcendent.

title: The World and More: Immanence, Tantra, and Transcendence (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if passion is not the problem, but the force that helps the world arise? This talk moves from a critique of how modern insight practice has narrowed its inheritance into a wider weaving of emptiness, tantra, eros, and imaginal practice, using a tantric couplet, Jung, Nietzsche, and Kabbalah as points of comparison. It then deepens into a careful distinction between craving and a holy, inexhaustible eros, with a long imaginal example that shows how erotic and sacred perception can transform how the senses, self, and world are known. The movement of the talk is toward a double perspective: loosening fabrication through emptiness while also allowing divine appearance, devotion, and participation in the world.
short_summary: A wide-ranging exploration of how emptiness and imaginal eros can be held together, from Buddhist and tantric sources to Kabbalah, Nietzsche, and lived imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Weaving the threads
chapters.description: He opens by framing the talk as a continued weaving of themes already introduced in the retreat.

chapters.title: Insight as an evolving tradition
chapters.description: He describes the insight meditation tradition as selective, modern, and still developing, with influences from Zen, Dzogchen, and Mahamudra.

chapters.title: Tantric verse and Vajrasattva
chapters.description: He introduces a tantric couplet about passion and reads it as a bridge between world-arising, emptiness, and the diamond being.

chapters.title: Desire and world-making
chapters.description: Using Jung and the Buddha's language of bhava-tanha, he distinguishes craving from eros and tracks how desire fabricates worlds at different levels.

chapters.title: World rejection and transcendence
chapters.description: He places Buddhism within a broader religious pattern of world rejection, then contrasts that with a more tantric or imaginal affirmation of the world.

chapters.title: A living imaginal sequence
chapters.description: He shares a detailed personal sequence of a burnt man and a healing goddess that develops into a highly erotic, devotional image of sacred commitment.

chapters.title: Inexhaustible eros
chapters.description: He contrasts tanha with the holiness of eros, then develops the idea that erotic energy can widen divinity, re-enchant the world, and work across many kinds of objects.

chapters.title: Nietzsche and the senses
chapters.description: He reads Nietzsche on loving the world and the senses, using the passage to support a transfiguring, non-puritanical spirituality.

chapters.title: Buddha mind and Kabbalah
chapters.description: He compares Buddhist, tantric, and Kabbalistic accounts of transcendence and immanence, including the erotic union of wisdom and appearance.

title: In Love with the Way: Images of Path and of Self
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the path is not mainly a cure to reach, but a field of images that shapes how practice feels, what it promises, and who we imagine ourselves to be on it? The talk begins by unpacking the familiar medical model of practice, then turns to four path-fantasies — medical patient, religious, researcher, and artist — to show how each can open or narrow the way we relate to awakening, tradition, and self. From there it becomes a set of practical inquiries: where fantasy is alive, where it has collapsed into ego-measurement, and how different practices like emptiness, imaginal work, samadhi, metta, and ethics each invite different ways of imagining the path.
short_summary: The talk examines four fantasies of the path and asks how they shape our experience of practice, tradition, and awakening.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea explains why he wants to slow down and revisit a point about Eros for the path rather than rush past it.

chapters.title: Eros and path fantasy
chapters.description: He introduces the question of being in love with practice and suggests that the path is always carried by images, desire, and a sense of more to discover.

chapters.title: Medical model of practice
chapters.description: The Four Noble Truths are read through a medical diagnosis-and-cure model, showing how Dharma can be imagined as treatment for suffering.

chapters.title: Bones need flesh
chapters.description: Burbea argues that the Four Noble Truths are only a skeleton unless they are fleshed out by psyche, logos, image, and fantasy.

chapters.title: Researcher fantasy
chapters.description: Using science and figures like Galileo, Newton, Einstein, and Planck, he presents a path-image grounded in revision, openness, and new discoveries.

chapters.title: Artist fantasy
chapters.description: He turns to the path as art, beginning with creativity and then widening into a sense of participation in divine or Buddha-nature creativity.

chapters.title: Permission and scope
chapters.description: The talk explores how these fantasies overlap, how much permission we give them, and how they can expand or contract depending on the situation.

chapters.title: Five areas of practice
chapters.description: Burbea applies the inquiry to emptiness, imaginal practice, samadhi, metta, and ethics, asking what each area most truly calls forth.

chapters.title: Open-ended path
chapters.description: He closes by inviting listeners to test these fantasies, notice what is authentic or fertile, and consider whether the path itself may be open-ended.

title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if practice is not mainly a way to reduce suffering, but a living relationship with eros, image, and soul? The talk contrasts several ways of understanding the path: as medicine, as scientific inquiry, as religious replication of the past, and as an open-ended artistic unfolding. It then asks whether the images of awakening, the self on the path, and even the Buddha and Dharma need to be seen as images so they can remain beautiful, fertile, and not harden into idols.
short_summary: A close examination of how different fantasies of practice shape eros, soulmaking, and the sense of what awakening can be.

chapters.title: Eros and the path
chapters.description: The talk opens by linking eros, awakening, and the images that shape how practice is lived.

chapters.title: The medical model
chapters.description: He frames the Four Noble Truths as a reduction-of-suffering model and names its strengths and limits.

chapters.title: Tradition and replication
chapters.description: He examines a religious fantasy of practice that tries to reproduce the Buddha and place authority in the past.

chapters.title: The artistic possibility
chapters.description: An artistic model is introduced as more open-ended, allowing originality and even a kind of healthy obsession.

chapters.title: Open-ended practice
chapters.description: He contrasts attainment-oriented ideas with a practice understood as exploratory, authentic, and not confined to measurement.

chapters.title: What the soul wants
chapters.description: The talk shifts toward beauty, desire, and the question of whether practice is truly fertile for the soul.

chapters.title: Outgrowing fixed images
chapters.description: Through examples, he shows how even authentic visions of practice can become too small and need to expand.

chapters.title: Immanence and transcendence
chapters.description: He asks what allows practice to become soulmaking, and how transcendence can return to the world as depth and holiness.

chapters.title: Icons, not idols
chapters.description: The closing movement argues for seeing path-images as icons—open, inexhaustible, and capable of keeping eros alive without collapsing into craving.

title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the path needs more than restraint, more than bare attention, more than a quiet wish to reduce suffering? In this talk, the speaker explores how eros, imagination, and logos shape what the path can even mean, moving from a critique of papanca and deflationary emptiness into Vajrayana imagery, pragmatism, and the question of how much otherness awakening can hold. The talk keeps returning to the same practical issue: whether practice is narrowing desire away from the world, or opening a richer, more dimensional relationship to life, the senses, and the path itself.
short_summary: A sustained inquiry into how eros, imagination, and views of awakening shape whether the path becomes deflationary or opens into greater dimensionality.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: He frames the talk around eros, the path, and how they already include questions of awakening and the world.

chapters.title: Pleasure and reduction
chapters.description: He contrasts common teachings about sense pleasure and papancha with more nuanced ways of understanding what the mind is doing.

chapters.title: Papancha reconsidered
chapters.description: He revisits papancha as objectification and fabrication, then distinguishes different levels at which experience can be simplified or emptied.

chapters.title: Cooking and devotion
chapters.description: Using the Gaia House kitchen, he shows how ordinary activities can carry fantasy, beauty, and soulmaking beyond simple care.

chapters.title: Vajrayana and emptiness
chapters.description: He turns to Vajrayana mandala symbolism and the Heart Sutra to show different ways appearance, wisdom, and emptiness can be related.

chapters.title: Eros in the path
chapters.description: He develops the idea that the degree and kind of eros shape the kind of path a person can inhabit, with examples from lovers, food, and embodied practice.

chapters.title: History and deflation
chapters.description: He draws on Richard Tarnas, Nietzsche, William James, and Richard Rorty to show how sobriety, pragmatism, and deflation can narrow the available imagination.

chapters.title: Fear and limitation
chapters.description: With Douglas Fairbairn, he explores how fear of disappointment can create an anti-libidinal stance that dampens eros and soulmaking.

chapters.title: A larger path
chapters.description: He closes by arguing that more eros requires a larger vision of awakening, one that can keep opening into depth, divinity, and multidimensionality.

title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the path is not mainly about settling into this life, but about a longing for something beyond it? This talk opens by tracing the Buddha’s repeated language of the unfabricated, the end of the world, and the ceasing of the senses, then asks how eros and soul-making get directed when the path is understood as a movement beyond fabrication. From there it moves through jhana, the holy life, the Bodhisattva ideal, and a broader history of world-rejection to ask what the Buddha’s orientation means for practice and for our cultural assumptions about transcendence.
short_summary: A close reading of early Buddhist passages on the unfabricated becomes a wider inquiry into eros, jhana, the holy life, and the place of transcendence in practice.

chapters.title: Eros and the path
chapters.description: Burbea opens by asking where eros is directed in practice and whether the path itself can be understood as a form of soul-making.

chapters.title: Beyond the senses
chapters.description: He reads passages from the Samyutta Nikaya that describe liberation as a dimension where the sense bases cease and dukkha ends.

chapters.title: Beyond ordinary perception
chapters.description: A Majjhima Nikaya passage is used to show that even refined jhana states are still not the final goal, which lies beyond the whole field of perception.

chapters.title: The unfabricated
chapters.description: He turns to the Udana and a strong instruction to 'smash' the aggregates, interpreting it as meditative unfabrication rather than literal destruction.

chapters.title: Consciousness without attribute
chapters.description: Burbea gathers more Pali passages that describe an objectless, attribute-free mode beyond the six senses, beyond the immaterial realms, and beyond concentration alone.

chapters.title: No coming, no going
chapters.description: Another cluster of verses points to a dimension without arising or passing away, which he presents as utterly beyond time, process, and objecthood.

chapters.title: Via negativa and longing
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion into apophatic language and then brings in Gregory of Nyssa to suggest that human longing may point beyond the limited world.

chapters.title: The soul's more
chapters.description: Returning to eros and soul-making, he distinguishes ordinary accumulation from a deeper 'more' that seeks the transcendent and maps onto jhana and nibbana.

chapters.title: The holy life
chapters.description: Burbea explains how the phrase 'the holy life' functions in the Pali Canon, then contrasts the early Buddhist renunciate aim with the Mahayana Bodhisattva ideal.

title: Doubt and Discernment (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: When does desire open into eros, and when does it contract into craving? The talk moves by careful distinctions: first between eros and craving, then between ordinary comfort and a genuinely imaginal relationship, and then into how emptiness and dependent origination change the way doubt itself can be held. Along the way, the speaker tests common assumptions about images, rest, jhana, and longing, asking not just what is real, but what is potent, soulmaking, and worth caring for.
short_summary: A nuanced inquiry into how to distinguish eros from craving, and how emptiness, images, rest, and longing can be discerned as part of soulmaking practice.

chapters.title: Practice as preparation
chapters.description: He opens by naming the skills that make this investigation possible: mindfulness, energy body work, emotion, and insight into emptiness.

chapters.title: Eros or craving
chapters.description: He develops the central distinction between eros and craving, emphasizing how eros opens the energy body and craving contracts it.

chapters.title: Wise effort
chapters.description: Using the Buddha’s four bases of success and examples from practice, he shows how desire and effort can be handled without either strain or passivity.

chapters.title: Questioning and experiment
chapters.description: He encourages daring, careful experimentation, and notes that eros and craving can become tangled without that meaning the whole practice should be discarded.

chapters.title: Imaginal autonomy
chapters.description: He turns to Piaget, then offers a story of an imaginal figure acting with its own intelligence, to show why the imaginal is not just wish fulfillment.

chapters.title: Not realism, but relation
chapters.description: With James Hillman and many examples, he argues that the main danger is identification, realism, and objectification, not the mere presence of strong images.

chapters.title: Emptiness as basis
chapters.description: He places emptiness at the foundation of the path and reframes the issue as one of efficacy and potency rather than reality claims.

chapters.title: Rest and jhana
chapters.description: He distinguishes shutting down from resting in living relationship, then compares imaginal aliveness with states of less fabrication and jhana.

chapters.title: Longing and devotion
chapters.description: He closes by asking where the imaginal is alive in our lives, and by treating longing as a clue to both personal desire and what we are called to serve.

title: Doubt and Discernment (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How do you tell the difference between eros, craving, and the imagination’s pull into fantasy? The talk starts with the common doubts that arise around erotic imaginal practice — whether it is escapism, wish fulfillment, or a closing down of life — and keeps turning those questions back into inquiry rather than belief. From there it moves into a careful discernment of what images ask of us, how they shape engagement, and why the path may involve risk, subtlety, and a different sense of reality than flat, materialist assumptions allow.
