It closes by contrasting a path of oneness and non-engagement with the open-ended, infinitely creative possibilities of the erotic imaginal.
short_summary: A sustained inquiry into doubt, eros, and imaginal practice, with careful distinctions between craving, fantasy, and the kind of engagement that deepens life.

chapters.title: Opening the doubt
chapters.description: He names the normal doubt that arises around erotic imaginal practice and proposes turning uncertainty into inquiry rather than paralysis.

chapters.title: Image asks for translation
chapters.description: He returns to earlier work on the imaginal and explains how images often carry a sense of duty and ask to be translated into life.

chapters.title: Non-engagement or depth
chapters.description: He questions the image of renunciation and non-engagement, asking whether imaginal practice really withdraws from life or deepens contact with it.

chapters.title: Fleeing reality revisited
chapters.description: He reframes the accusation of fleeing reality by asking what is meant by reality and whether dismissing the imaginal may itself be a kind of avoidance.

chapters.title: Eros versus craving
chapters.description: He introduces a sharper discernment between eros and craving, showing how desire can either narrow the heart or open and energize the whole being.

chapters.title: Fire, risk, and unbinding
chapters.description: He explores how eros is like fire: useful and dangerous, linked to Buddhist fire imagery, the Buddha’s reversal of it, and the meaning of Nibbana as unbinding.

chapters.title: Doubt, suffering, and path
chapters.description: He places the work in a wider mythic and practical frame, using Dionysus, Vajrayana, Mahasi-style retreat hardship, and Ajahn Chah’s tears to ask what kinds of risk and suffering a path can hold.

chapters.title: Personal movement in practice
chapters.description: He shares his own gradual movement from caution toward trust in erotic imaginal work, including dreams, ambivalence, and the slow opening of relationship with image and nature.

chapters.title: Living relationship and infinity
chapters.description: He ends by distinguishing imaginal practice from ordinary fantasy through sensitivity, logos, intentionality, and the theater of image, then contrasts a finite path of oneness with the infinite creative possibilities of eros and imaginal relating.

title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the question is not whether an experience is real, but whether it is important, soul-making, and worth refining? The talk uses music, nature, other people, and the cosmos as a way to trace how perception can open into nuance, image, and participation, while also showing how culture pushes us toward a flattened, materialist way of seeing. From there it turns to conceptual frameworks, dependent origination, and the non-separation of chitta and reality, asking what kind of ideas actually support exploration rather than shut it down. Near the end, it widens into developmental psychology and the thought that any theory must eventually crack open, letting more light in.
short_summary: Using music as a starting point, the talk argues for a soul-making way of knowing that is broader than realism, and ends by showing how any conceptual framework must remain open, breakable, and fertile.

chapters.title: Music and limited hearing
chapters.description: Burbea opens with a musical anecdote to show how a very limited mode of perception can miss most of what is actually there.

chapters.title: Real or soul-making
chapters.description: He develops the music analogy into a larger question of whether an experience is real, important, or soul-making, and extends it to nature, other people, and the cosmos.

chapters.title: Culture and realism
chapters.description: The talk turns to how dominant cultural views gain authority, how clashes of realism arise, and why scientific method should not be confused with a complete account of reality.

chapters.title: Frameworks that support
chapters.description: Burbea argues for a stable conceptual framework that can withstand cultural pressure while opening experience, rather than shutting it down through inherited assumptions.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and participation
chapters.description: He begins to articulate soulmaking more directly, including the role of Eros, the non-separation of citta and reality, and the idea of participation as a fertile way of understanding perception and imagination.

chapters.title: Valid because valuable
chapters.description: The argument deepens around participation: an idea can matter because it enlarges experience, and its value is tied to its capacity to stimulate soulmaking rather than to a claim of final metaphysical proof.

chapters.title: Eros and development
chapters.description: Burbea re-reads developmental psychology through the lens of soulmaking, contrasting his view with Freud, Fairbairn, and Kohut, and suggesting that the soul wants expansion across self, other, world, body, and mind.

chapters.title: Self, image, and pathology
chapters.description: He questions culturally inherited ideas of a fixed self and explores how blocked or one-sided imaginal development may relate to pathology, while noting that the psyche may naturally learn to see image as image.

chapters.title: Cracks in theory
chapters.description: The closing movement returns to the limits of any framework, the inevitability of cracks and expansion, and the idea that theory is a kind of theatre serving soulmaking rather than final truth.

title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: If a conceptual framework shapes what you can notice, feel, and value, then what should a Dharma framework be able to hold without shrinking experience? The talk works through that question by arguing that a viable logos must support soulmaking, imaginal perception, eros, and the widening of ways of knowing, rather than settling into a narrow or stagnant view. From there it turns to the Pali Canon, scholarship, and modern readings of Buddhism, questioning any claim to neutral authority and instead asking how tradition is always interpreted through an agenda. It ends by pressing the same issue into epistemology itself: what counts as knowledge, who gets to decide, and whether meditation can train us into more flexible ways of looking.
short_summary: A close examination of how Dharma views shape experience, with a critique of narrow interpretations, epistemic certainty, and the claim that meditation trains flexible ways of looking.

chapters.title: What a logos must hold
chapters.description: He returns to the problem of how to build a conceptual framework that can make room for soulmaking, dependent origination, emptiness, and multiple ways of knowing.

chapters.title: Eros and interpretation
chapters.description: He shows how ideas about desire and eros directly change experience, and why some views support soulmaking while others constrain it.

chapters.title: When Dharma narrows
chapters.description: He warns that a narrow Dharma can become entrenched dogma, quietly limiting freedom, creativity, beauty, and the range of experience.

chapters.title: Whose Pali Canon?
chapters.description: He asks who is really being heard when people appeal to the Pali Canon, and argues that every reading comes with assumptions, agendas, and a fantasy of the Buddha.

chapters.title: Scholarship and tradition
chapters.description: He questions the claim that traditional language guarantees authenticity, and suggests that scholarship and interpretation are always creative and historically situated.

chapters.title: Origins and dialogue
chapters.description: He looks at how Mahayana and Vajrayana traditions claim continuity with the Buddha, then reframes the relationship to tradition as dialogue rather than fixed authority.

chapters.title: Epistemicide in Dharma
chapters.description: He introduces epistemicide to name the erasure of embodied, imaginal, and culturally diverse ways of knowing, including within some secular Dharma settings.

chapters.title: Mindfulness is not enough
chapters.description: He argues that stripped-down secular Dharma can function like a missionary worldview unless it also opens deeper insight, dependent origination, and liberating ways of looking.

chapters.title: Training ways of looking
chapters.description: He closes by using contemporary philosophy to show that epistemology is always lived, then returns to meditation as the training of flexible states of mind and ways of looking.

title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if practice needs a logos — a living conceptual framework — to hold it together, guide its navigation, and keep it responsive as experience changes? The talk moves from that basic claim into a series of analogies, then widens the frame to include fabrication, dependent origination, emptiness, and the many ways of looking that can shape what is seen and lived. From there it opens into eros, soulmaking, body, relationships, death, earth, beauty, and divinity, asking what kind of framework can include all of that without reducing it.
short_summary: A meditation on logos as a living framework for practice that expands into fabrication, emptiness, and a broad soulmaking vision of life.

chapters.title: Logos as support
chapters.description: He argues that every path needs a conceptual framework to hold practices together, guide navigation, and provide stability.

chapters.title: Trellis and tree
chapters.description: Using the images of a trellis and then a fruit tree, he shows how logos can be organic, integrated, responsive, and breakable.

chapters.title: Concept beyond thought
chapters.description: He clarifies that conceptuality is not just discursive thinking and is still operating even in refined meditative absorption.

chapters.title: Fabrication and emptiness
chapters.description: The talk turns to the spectrum of fabrication, dependent origination, emptiness, and the legitimacy of multiple ways of looking.

chapters.title: What the frame must hold
chapters.description: He broadens the needed framework to include imaginal life, eros, body, relationships, death, the earth, beauty, psychology, sacredness, and divinity.

chapters.title: History and conditioning
chapters.description: Concept and experience are described as mutually conditioning, and the framework is placed within history and society rather than treated as ahistorical truth.

chapters.title: Modern self and soulmaking
chapters.description: A retreat story, the construction of the modernist self, and the claim that fabrication can still be genuinely soulmaking are brought together.

chapters.title: Holy bodies and Christ
chapters.description: He uses the image of holding a loved one's feet, then turns to Christ and Paul to show how devotion can generate theology and a larger cosmology.

chapters.title: Plural knowing
chapters.description: The final movement gathers the need for a fertile, non-dogmatic framework that avoids epistemicide, includes dependent origination and emptiness, and leaves room for a broad range of embodied ways of knowing.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How do you keep imaginal practice from tipping into one-sided intensity? The talk works through balance as a practical art: noticing where attention gets captured, redressing the emphasis, and finding a steadier field in which heart, energy body, image, and desire can support one another. It moves from a concrete example of overexcited heart-energy into a broader map of sexual energy, erotic tension, participation, and the different ways practice can be steered toward less fabrication or toward skillful imaginal making. In the end, the conceptual framework itself is treated as part of the vessel that can hold and guide the work.
short_summary: A practical exploration of balance in imaginal practice, from intensity and sexual energy to the role of conceptual frameworks in holding the work.

chapters.title: Balance in Practice
chapters.description: He frames the talk around balance, sustainability, and fertility in imaginal practice, using attention as the main lever.

chapters.title: Heart and Image
chapters.description: A case example shows how attention can overconcentrate in the heart and energy body, crowding out the imaginal dimension.

chapters.title: Intensity and Potency
chapters.description: He names the difference between something feeling intense and something actually being transformative or soulmaking.

chapters.title: Sexual Energy
chapters.description: The talk turns to sexual arousal, suggesting a wider, whole-body attention that can let energy spread and transform into bliss.

chapters.title: Union or Participation
chapters.description: He describes two possible directions in erotic imaginal practice: dissolving into oneness, or sustaining two-ness and participation.

chapters.title: Shifting the Emphasis
chapters.description: He generalizes the balancing move across desire, image, energy body, emotional tone, and the three centers of belly, heart, and head.

chapters.title: Degrees of Fabrication
chapters.description: He revisits the distinction between bare attention, a middle mode, and the deliberate fabrication of imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Framework as Vessel
chapters.description: The closing section emphasizes conceptual framework as a container that offers orientation, trust, stability, and guidance.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the problem is not desire itself, but the way it gets flattened into something only personal, only literal, or only to be let go? The talk moves through cases of unavailable love, shame, grief, and devotional longing to show how opening to the imaginal can enlarge self, other, and world instead of contracting them. It then turns to balance: not suppressing eros, but learning how to include the self in the field, let the image become more dimensional, and sometimes perceive desire as already happening in a sacred time.
short_summary: A talk on working with eros and imaginal practice as a way to deepen desire, balance self and other, and loosen reified perception.

chapters.title: Eros and balance
chapters.description: Burbea frames the talk as a question of how to work with eros so it becomes fertile and soulmaking rather than merely alternating between fire and steadiness.

chapters.title: The unreachable beloved
chapters.description: He offers a relational example in which painful attraction can be entered imaginally, enlarging the other beyond a flat, concrete reality.

chapters.title: Beyond 'let go'
chapters.description: He questions the simplicity of the usual advice to let go and suggests that, in complex situations, opening to the image may be more fruitful.

chapters.title: A transforming story
chapters.description: A long student example shows frustration, inferiority, and longing opening into vivid animal-erotic imagery, connection, and holiness.

chapters.title: Include the self
chapters.description: He argues that balance in the soul-making dynamic requires seeing not only the beloved other but also the beauty and eros of the self.

chapters.title: Forest and benefactors
chapters.description: A retreatant’s account moves from grief with trees and cold air into specific imaginal love from several benefactors, showing pain opening into care.

chapters.title: Love in the imaginal
chapters.description: Burbea draws out the lesson that imaginal perception already contains love and that love can flow both from the image and back toward the practitioner.

chapters.title: A balanced constellation
chapters.description: He broadens the teaching to include object, self, world, and eros, warning against reification and showing how multidimensionality loosens fixation.

chapters.title: Desire, divinity, and time
chapters.description: Using Blake and Henri Corbin, he explores desire as divine influx, desire as already happening in sacred time, and then contrasts this with an anatta reading that makes desire fade.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How do you work with desire without flattening it, obeying it, or becoming rigid? The talk begins with mastery as a fluid balance: learning to navigate, in the heat of practice, between letting go, staying with feeling tone and craving, meeting the underlying emotion with kindness, or deliberately turning toward nourishment and spaciousness. It then moves into imaginal practice, where the speaker explores how to stay with erotic image, notice what makes it hard to bear, and make subtle shifts of emphasis without leaving the field of the practice.
short_summary: A close, practical map of how to meet desire, craving, and erotic imaginal practice with flexible response rather than one fixed method.

chapters.title: Mastery as navigation
chapters.description: He frames mastery as fluid balance: moment-to-moment responding, not rigidly repeating one method.

chapters.title: Why range matters
chapters.description: He contrasts habitual one-sided practice with the wider range needed for freedom and insight.

chapters.title: Letting desire pass
chapters.description: He explores noticing pleasure, seeing where it goes, and using impermanence to support letting go.

chapters.title: Vedanā and craving
chapters.description: He shifts to pleasant and unpleasant feeling tones, tracing craving and the pressure that can be tolerated and allowed.

chapters.title: Kindness for emotion
chapters.description: He introduces a gentler response: caring for the self and holding the underlying emotion with mindfulness and metta.

chapters.title: Subtle skill in practice
chapters.description: He emphasizes delicate shifts of attention and the artistry of micro-adjustments in the heat of practice.

chapters.title: Skillful redirection
chapters.description: He presents conscious turning away from desire toward nourishment, samadhi, metta, spaciousness, and less fabrication.

chapters.title: Opening desire's depth
chapters.description: Drawing on an earlier talk, he outlines a three-step inquiry into what is really wanted and how to open to desire's energy.

chapters.title: Working with eros
chapters.description: He focuses on staying with imaginal images, meeting what is hard to bear, trusting dissolution, and distinguishing eros from equanimity.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How do you keep eros from becoming either a narrow fixation or an ungrounded rush? The talk argues that balance is not a fixed middle, but a dynamic capacity built through samadhi, equanimity, rootedness, inquiry, and the kind of nourishment that makes one less dependent on sense pleasures or ego boosts. It then turns to imaginal practice, using the earth-touching Buddha and Buddhist mandala figures such as Akshobhya, Amitabha, Ratnasambhava, and Amoghasiddhi to show how image, emptiness, emotion, and action can be brought into a workable counterpoint.
short_summary: A talk on how samadhi, equanimity, and imaginal practice can create a dynamic balance for working with eros, using earth imagery and mandala figures as practical supports.

chapters.title: Picking up the thread
chapters.description: Burbea recaps the earlier material on ethics, trust, equanimity, and skill with emotions and energies before continuing the list of supports for this work.

chapters.title: Samadhi beyond focus
chapters.description: He redefines samadhi as well-being and less fabrication, not simply fixed attention.

chapters.title: Nourishment and balance
chapters.description: The talk explores how meditative nourishment reduces dependence on sense pleasure and ego reassurance, making the practitioner less easily thrown off balance.

chapters.title: Why understanding matters
chapters.description: Burbea argues that understanding how practice works changes what we choose in meditation and how we regard thoughts, images, and emotions.

chapters.title: Rootedness and earth
chapters.description: He turns to hara practice, earth connection, and the Buddha touching the earth as examples of imaginal rootedness and strength.

chapters.title: Spacious equanimity
chapters.description: The talk contrasts solidity with spacious, insubstantial forms of equanimity that allow things to come and go without entanglement.

chapters.title: Strength, libido, vitality
chapters.description: Burbea discusses images of strength, the role of libido and vitality, and the need to avoid pathologizing a person's natural tendencies.

chapters.title: Dynamic balance
chapters.description: He frames balance as fluid and responsive, then uses the jhanas to show that qualities of stillness and equanimity can be accessed, revisited, and trusted over time.

chapters.title: Range and inquiry
chapters.description: The talk widens into a range of being that includes arousal, stillness, mindfulness, and investigation, while questioning fixed ideals of balance.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How do we work with eros without either flattening it into craving or being carried away by it? The talk begins with two alchemical maxims—about the strength of the vessel and mastering the fire—and then uses them to explore how practice needs both elasticity and care when desire, image, and soul-making intensify the path. It moves through the dangers of over-simplified views of practice, the role of ethics and emotional skill as prerequisites, and a rethinking of equanimity as steadiness, spaciousness, and balance rather than detachment. By the end, the talk opens a middle way between dismissing eros and concretizing it, while also asking what duty to an image may mean and how it can coexist with freedom.
short_summary: The talk asks how to hold eros as fire within a vessel strong enough to contain it, and develops that question through ethics, emotional awareness, trust, equanimity, and the delicate role of imaginal duty.

chapters.title: Two alchemical maxims
chapters.description: Burbea introduces two alchemical sayings and frames them as broadly relevant to practice, psychotherapy, and healing.

chapters.title: Eros needs a vessel
chapters.description: He explains why opening to eros requires care, boundaries, and a vessel strong enough to hold the work without acting it out.

chapters.title: Elasticity and shattering
chapters.description: The vessel must be able to stretch, and sometimes break, as development outgrows old forms and structures.

chapters.title: Fire and its dangers
chapters.description: He turns to fire as effort, intensity, and eros itself, stressing both the need for mastery and the inevitability of risk and change.

chapters.title: Dionysus and dismemberment
chapters.description: Drawing on James Hillman and Euripides, he uses Dionysian imagery to show eros as disruptive, archetypal, and not reducible to personal blame.

chapters.title: Preliminaries for practice
chapters.description: Burbea asks what must be in place before deeper eros work: ethics, preliminary disciplines, conceptual framing, sangha, and a willingness to question assumptions.

chapters.title: Ethics and rootedness
chapters.description: He argues that eros practice needs a heartfelt, embodied commitment to ethics, not just obedience to rules or fear of consequences.

chapters.title: Energetics and trust
chapters.description: He adds skill with emotion and energy, then a modest trust that allows experimentation and reveals eros as something potentially wise and meaningful.

chapters.title: Equanimity and duty
chapters.description: The talk closes by rethinking equanimity as balance and spaciousness, then linking freedom, imaginal duty, and a disciplined middle way in relation to what matters.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 5 - Otherness and the Art of Disappearing)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Can eros really be a movement toward union, or does it preserve otherness and keep relationship alive? In this talk, the speaker revisits that question, contrasting soulmaking eros with older ideas of dissolution and then tracing how meditative oneness, imaginal practice, and relationship can coexist without collapsing into final merger. The talk then widens into a sustained reflection on participation: how images, experience, and Dharma may be both discovered and shaped through the way of looking, leaving the listener with a sense of an endless, re-enchanted path.
short_summary: A careful, wide-ranging talk on eros, otherness, participation, and why Dharma may be more endless than a path of disappearing.

chapters.title: Eros and otherness
chapters.description: Burbea redefines eros against older union-centered accounts and says it preserves two-ness rather than dissolving it.

chapters.title: Oneness and its limits
chapters.description: He examines longing for oneness, distinguishes temporary meditative unions from permanent dissolution, and questions aversion hidden inside transcendence-seeking.

chapters.title: Meditative dissolution
chapters.description: He describes jhana, brahmavihara, cessation, and tantric becoming as real but temporary openings that do not erase distinction.

chapters.title: Eros keeps tension
chapters.description: The talk develops the idea that erotic tension arises from desire for the other, from the object's increasing richness, and from the fact that more always remains.

chapters.title: The birth of us
chapters.description: Burbea describes how eros can constellate a third term, the 'us,' along with child, project, or shared world as new erotic objects.

chapters.title: Not the art of disappearing
chapters.description: He contrasts soulmaking eros with the art of disappearing, saying that valuable experiences of no-self or emptiness are not the same as eros.

chapters.title: Participation and sacredness
chapters.description: The talk moves into participation: images and eros can be granted sacredness and autonomy, or discovered as such, depending on how they are seen.

chapters.title: Ways of knowing
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion into epistemology, bringing in the mundus imaginalis, spiritual sensation, Sufism, and John Archibald Wheeler's participatory universe.

chapters.title: Endless Dharma
chapters.description: Burbea returns to Plato and the Buddha to suggest that emptiness, imaginal practice, Mahayana vastness, bodhisattva service, and soulmaking can each open an endless Dharma.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What happens when Eros is allowed to open perception instead of being reduced to appetite or fixed belief? The talk moves through a series of images and examples—Plato’s eros as an interpretive daimon, Kabbalistic readings of endless meaning, a dream of a luminous door, and the contrast between idol and icon—to show how desire can enlarge the sense of self, world, and divinity. It then turns practical, asking how containment, boundaries, and non-acting can preserve erotic tension so it becomes a crucible for soulmaking rather than collapse into one-dimensional gratification.
short_summary: A careful exploration of how Eros can open infinite meaning, and how containment can turn desire into a vessel for soulmaking.

chapters.title: Eros Opens Meaning
chapters.description: Burbea introduces eros as a force that can either support or block soulmaking, and frames it as something that opens interpretation, beauty, and depth.

chapters.title: Infinite Interpretation
chapters.description: He brings in Plato, Kabbalah, Sanford Drob, and Moses Chaim Rosato to show how eros can make the world and scripture feel inexhaustibly meaningful.

chapters.title: Buddha Nature Language
chapters.description: He translates the same opening into Buddhist terms, linking eros, divine seeing, conceptual creativity, and emptiness to the jhana of Buddha nature.

chapters.title: When Eros Narrows
chapters.description: Burbea describes how, without an imaginal dimension, eros can collapse into greed or horizontal pursuit, especially when nature is seen one-dimensionally.

chapters.title: Dream of the Door
chapters.description: He tells a dream of a carved door, music, rain forest, and stars to show how an image can carry beauty, reverence, and depth without needing to be fully unpacked.

chapters.title: Relationships And Limits
chapters.description: He applies the same pattern to nature, teacher-student devotion, and music, showing how eros fades when an image or relationship can no longer hold the soul's expansion.

chapters.title: Icon, Idol, Concept
chapters.description: Using Tibetan lineage, Henri Corbin, and Jean-Luc Marion, he distinguishes icon from idol and argues that the real danger is reification and identification, not expansion itself.

chapters.title: Temenos And Containment
chapters.description: Burbea explains how restraint and non-acting can create an alchemical vessel or temenos in which erotic tension is preserved and transformed into soulmaking.

chapters.title: James On Desire
chapters.description: He ends with William James on the extravagance of wants, using the quote to suggest that uneasiness and longing can point beyond present understanding.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What happens when eros meets a view that can either open depth or stop it at the surface? The talk keeps circling that question through the dynamics of eros, psyche, and logos, showing how certain ways of looking can nourish mystery, while others flatten experience into one dimension. It moves from broad distinctions between realist reduction, less-fabricating mindfulness, and soulmaking perspectives into concrete examples from sexuality, pornography, relationship, and even suffering itself. By the end, the emphasis is on how a living conceptual framework can re-enchant experience rather than close it down.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of how views shape eros, image, and depth, from materialist reduction to soulmaking and the re-enchantment of difficulty.

chapters.title: Opening the frame
chapters.description: Burbea sets up the question of what is needed for soulmaking and why the ideas have to be revisited and digested in lived practice.

chapters.title: Eros as open-ended
chapters.description: He develops the provisional definition of eros as desire to contact more, emphasizing that touch, opening, and knowing have no fixed endpoint.

chapters.title: Three ways of looking
chapters.description: He lays out three broad modes of perception: reductionist realism, less-fabricating practice, and soulmaking ways of looking.

chapters.title: Mystery and depth
chapters.description: The talk turns to the sense of unfathomability in another person, nature, or an image, and how that mystery itself feeds eros.

chapters.title: Eros and logos
chapters.description: Burbea shows how eros generates more image and more world, while logos and psyche must also be able to open enough to receive that movement.

chapters.title: When view flattens
chapters.description: Using materialism as a clear example, he shows how a narrow concept of reality can prevent imaginal depth and force eros into one dimension.

chapters.title: Pornography and greed
chapters.description: He uses pornography and related patterns as examples of objectification, soullessness, and blocked erotic expansion, while noting that kindness may still be present.

chapters.title: Multidimensional soulmaking
chapters.description: Burbea broadens the frame to show how any shrinking of reality to one level limits soulmaking, and how eros, psyche, and logos function together.

chapters.title: Relationship and re-enchantment
chapters.description: He works through fixed images of self, sex, and marriage, then ends with the possibility of meeting difficulty, even dukkha, in a way that restores imaginal depth.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How does erotic longing widen into a whole field of perception, rather than staying fixed on one person or one desire? The talk moves from a fourfold constellation of self, other, world, and eros into a series of concrete imaginal examples: being held by a goddess, feeling desire shift and deepen, and seeing the world itself become more dimensional, luminous, and responsive. It also pauses over the difference between eros and metta, the way interpretation can turn desire into craving, and the question of whether eros is something narrow or a cosmogonic force that keeps opening into sacredness.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of how eros, imaginal practice, and dependent arising can transform self, other, and world into a wider field of sacred perception.

chapters.title: Setting the Trajectory
chapters.description: He introduces the broader aims of the talk and reviews the thread connecting eros, the imaginal, and soul-making.

chapters.title: Dependent Co-Arising
chapters.description: He frames self, other, world, and eros as a fourfold constellation rather than a simple subject-object pair.

chapters.title: Images That Expand
chapters.description: An earlier example of goddess contact and bodily infusion shows how imaginal practice can deepen self and world.

chapters.title: Eros and Metta
chapters.description: He distinguishes eros from mettā with imagination, especially through eros's auto-erotic and dimensional character.

chapters.title: Desire and Interpretation
chapters.description: Using William Blake and a close look at craving, he shows how a way of looking can make desire harden or open.

chapters.title: Cosmopoiesis Opens
chapters.description: He turns to world-making, including Thomas Aquinas's line about beauty yearning to be seen and loved.

chapters.title: Examples in Practice
chapters.description: Several imaginal and erotic examples show how love can spread into body, matter, nature, and subtle energy.

chapters.title: Samadhi and Sacred Time
chapters.description: He describes the choice between absorption and staying with image, then brings in Corbin's idea of hierophantic time.

chapters.title: Expanding Sacredness
chapters.description: He recaps how eros can widen the sense of divinity, self, other, and world, while keeping the experience image-like rather than fixed.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if eros is not a problem to manage, but the movement that opens psyche, image, and world? The talk works through that question by redefining eros as contact, intimacy, and the longing for more, then showing how it expands into the imaginal, into self and world, and into a wider soulmaking process. It moves between concept and example, from Greek figures like Pothos and Himeros to detailed meditative images, including subtle tenderness, ritual union, and darker, more intense forms. It also keeps returning to a practical concern: what happens when the image is trusted, or when it is reduced, dismissed, or met with guilt.
short_summary: An extended exploration of eros as a soulmaking movement that deepens image, self, and world, with many meditative examples showing its subtle and intense forms.

chapters.title: Opening the frame
chapters.description: Rob introduces the talk as a careful unfolding of eros and the soulmaking dynamic, with an emphasis on both theory and practical consequence.

chapters.title: Why eros appears
chapters.description: He explains why eros naturally arises in imaginal practice, linking it to beauty, otherness, and the broader range of images that a practice can hold.

chapters.title: Desire for contact
chapters.description: Eros is reframed as a movement toward contact, intimacy, and touch, with Pothos and Himeros used to describe longing and ongoing movement.

chapters.title: Soul loves soul-making
chapters.description: He develops the mutual fertilizing of eros, psyche, and logos, arguing that the soul wants more soul and that image, idea, self, and world expand together.

chapters.title: Subtle erotic images
chapters.description: A series of gentler examples shows that erotic imagery can be tender, luminous, and non-escalatory, not limited to overt sexuality.

chapters.title: Samadhi and transformation
chapters.description: He describes how movie residue, prison imagery, the tiger, and deeper samadhi shift into a more luminous imaginal scene, showing the link between mind state and image.

chapters.title: The dark goddess rite
chapters.description: A long imaginal narrative on a raft at sea explores ritual, blood, devotion, dismemberment, and the wide range of erotic and sacred image-forms.

chapters.title: Trust or collapse
chapters.description: He closes by showing how trust, guilt, or reduction change the fate of the image and whether the soulmaking process opens or folds.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How do you take up eros, emptiness, or even a teaching itself without grasping it by the wrong handle? Starting from Emerson’s line that “all things have two handles,” the talk moves through desire, imagination, and Nagarjuna’s warning about emptiness, then turns to the more unsettled claim that eros is larger than any fixed definition. From there it becomes a careful inquiry into why delineations matter: how distinctions can deepen perception, create trouble, or open new room for practice. The talk ends by asking which conceptual refinements actually serve soul-making, and why the work should stay open rather than become a closed system.
short_summary: A careful exploration of eros, emptiness, and the double-edged power of making distinctions in practice.

chapters.title: Two Handles
chapters.description: Emerson’s aphorism is used to frame the whole talk: teachings and experiences can be taken up in more than one way.

chapters.title: Eros As Fire
chapters.description: Rob develops eros as a formidable, non-cutesy force that asks for sensitivity, boldness, and careful experimentation.

chapters.title: Diotima and Meaning
chapters.description: Drawing on Plato’s Diotima, he presents eros as a hermeneutical daemon that mediates between the human and divine, and between what is real and less real.

chapters.title: Limits of Understanding
chapters.description: He argues that eros can never be fully grasped and that its richness expands both the object of desire and our understanding of eros itself.

chapters.title: Dangers of Delineation
chapters.description: The talk turns to the risks of conceptual precision, including self-judgment, over-definition, and the way distinctions can create identity problems.

chapters.title: When Distinctions Help
chapters.description: Rob shows that some distinctions reduce fabrication while others increase it, and that both can sometimes serve insight and soul-making.

chapters.title: An Open Path
chapters.description: He ends by explaining that the teaching is meant to guide practice now while remaining open to additions, tributaries, and future creativity.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How do eros, image, and practice fit together without collapsing into either craving or a flattened psychology? The talk argues for a wider Dharma language that can recognize fantasy and imaginal life as already present, then uses contrasts like holiday and pilgrimage to show the difference between mere pleasure-seeking and soul-making movement. From there it carefully distinguishes love, sex, eros, and metta, and develops an eros-psyche-logos dynamic in which perception can either solidify through clinging or open, deepen, and become more alive.
short_summary: A careful exploration of eros, imaginal practice, and how a richer psychology can distinguish craving from soul-making.

chapters.title: Unfamiliar ideas
chapters.description: Burbea acknowledges that these ideas may feel unfamiliar and explains why they are harder to introduce than culturally familiar blends of Dharma with neuroscience or psychotherapy.

chapters.title: Fantasy and image
chapters.description: He argues that image and fantasy are already present in practice and life, and that a fuller Dharma psychology must be able to recognize them rather than dismiss them as papanca.

chapters.title: Holiday or pilgrimage
chapters.description: Using an oncologist conversation as a contrast, he distinguishes secular ideas of quality of life from a pilgrimage-like movement of soul-making.

chapters.title: Find out for yourself
chapters.description: He returns to the Buddha’s ehipassiko teaching and warns against flattening Dharma into quick conclusions, especially about desire and suffering.

chapters.title: Eros in practice
chapters.description: Burbea says eros is already part of imaginal practice, often intertwined with love, and can sometimes open samadhi or deepen compassion in unexpected ways.

chapters.title: Distinguishing love and eros
chapters.description: He carefully separates eros from love, sex, metta, and attraction, while showing how the terms can overlap without being identical.

chapters.title: Eros and perception
chapters.description: The talk develops the eros–psyche–logos dynamic and links it to dependent origination, showing how clinging solidifies perception while eros can loosen and enrich it.

chapters.title: Small and large eros
chapters.description: He closes by distinguishing a narrower definition of eros from the wider soulmaking constellation it implies, and notes how realism or blockage can turn eros into craving.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1- 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How do emptiness, imaginal practice, soul, divinity, and eros fit together without collapsing into either metaphysics or reductionism? The talk moves by way of a careful glossary: it starts from a phenomenological method, then unfolds terms like ways of looking, fabrication, emptiness, image, soul-making, logos, cosmopoiesis, theophany, divinity, and Buddha nature. Rather than treating these as abstractions, the speaker keeps returning to how they alter perception, deepen meaning, and open or close experience in practice.
short_summary: A glossary-style talk that traces how ways of looking, emptiness, image, soul-making, and divinity shape perception and practice.

chapters.title: Entering the framework
chapters.description: He situates the talk within a wider sequence of teachings and explains why the material needs to be heard in order.

chapters.title: Why these words matter
chapters.description: He reflects on reactions to terms like soul, divinity, and God, and argues for approaching them through experience rather than assumptions.

chapters.title: Phenomenology and looking
chapters.description: He defines the phenomenological method, then introduces ways of looking and fabrication as the basis for the whole framework.

chapters.title: Emptiness and freedom
chapters.description: He shows how emptiness follows from fabrication and how freedom lies in recognizing that there are only ways of looking.

chapters.title: What counts as imaginal
chapters.description: He distinguishes imaginal images from mere fantasy or projection, emphasizing otherness, autonomy, beauty, sacredness, and meaning.

chapters.title: Soul-making and eros
chapters.description: He defines soul-making as any way of being that generates soulfulness, then begins to introduce eros, libido, psyche, and logos as related terms.

chapters.title: The eros-psyche-logos dynamic
chapters.description: He expands on the mutual fertilization of eros, psyche, and logos, showing how concepts shape perception and practice.

chapters.title: Cosmopoiesis and theophany
chapters.description: He describes how imaginal practice can spill over into a transformed sense of the cosmos and how particular things can appear as faces of the divine.

chapters.title: Divinity and Buddha nature
chapters.description: He broadens divinity as a category of mystical quality, then closes by sketching a non-dual view of Buddha nature, Dharmakaya, and cosmic awareness.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: How can eros be named without flattening it into sexuality, pleasure, or a fixed definition? The talk begins by situating eros inside a larger practice framework, using the hermeneutic circle to show why any single term has to be read in relation to the whole path, the whole Dharma, and the larger web of imaginal and soulmaking teachings. From there it offers a working description of eros as a desire for more contact, more knowing, and more intimacy with the beloved or erotic object, then tests that definition against tension, juiciness, dynamism, arousal, delight, and the difference between soulmaking delight and meditative bliss.
short_summary: A careful, layered exploration of eros as a relational desire for more contact and more of the beloved, set within a wider framework of practice, imagination, and soulmaking.

chapters.title: Why This Delineation
chapters.description: Burbea explains why eros matters on the path and why it belongs within imaginal practice, sacredness, and sexuality rather than being reduced to a side issue.

chapters.title: Definitions and Context
chapters.description: He pauses before defining eros to reflect on the use of words in general and to insist that the definition is serving a practice purpose, not claiming final authority.

chapters.title: Hermeneutic Circle
chapters.description: Using the idea of parts and wholes, he shows how eros, mindfulness, and the Dharma can only be understood in context and through movement between small and large views.

chapters.title: Eros as Desire
chapters.description: He begins a simple working definition: eros is a desire for more contact, more connection, more touch, and more knowing of the erotic object or beloved.

chapters.title: The More and Otherness
chapters.description: Through Pothos and related ideas, he emphasizes eros as an impulse toward more, while also preserving otherness rather than collapsing into fixed union.

chapters.title: Recognizing Eros
chapters.description: Burbea describes how to notice eros in practice through sensitivity to mind states, the energy body, attraction, tension, and different levels of intensity.

chapters.title: Juiciness and Arousal
chapters.description: He turns to libido, moisture, and fruitfulness to point toward the juiciness of eros, then links eros with the imaginal, soulmaking, and a broader dynamism of interest.

chapters.title: Delight and Bliss
chapters.description: The talk closes by distinguishing soulmaking delight from meditative bliss, using jhana as a comparison and returning to the idea that eros enriches and expands experience.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if eros is not a problem to eliminate, but a living force that can be clarified, studied, and even sacralized? The talk begins by asking why this word needs to be distinguished at all, then moves through a teachers’ discussion, a long example of a practitioner wrestling with love and monastic life, and a wider argument that desire is already part of the Dharma’s terrain. By the end, the speaker is sketching a more elaborate practice map in which eros, soulmaking, and emptiness can be held together without collapsing into either craving or renunciation.
short_summary: An inquiry into eros that moves from confusion about the term to a fuller Dharma psychology of desire, love, and sacredness.

chapters.title: Setting the Inquiry
chapters.description: The talk opens by placing eros inside a larger retreat arc and explaining why it needs careful definition.

chapters.title: Why Context Matters
chapters.description: Rob frames the discussion within insight meditation, then recalls a broader teaching context that makes the topic harder and more interesting.

chapters.title: Many Meanings of Eros
chapters.description: He reviews how different teachers used eros to mean desire, life, or a counterpart to death, showing how little agreement there was.

chapters.title: Desire on the Path
chapters.description: He argues that desire is not simply a problem in Buddhism, citing the four bases of power and the Buddha’s own intensity of aspiration.

chapters.title: Previous Explorations
chapters.description: Rob points listeners to earlier talks on desire, effort, and the ecology of love, preparing the ground for a more developed account here.

chapters.title: A Monk’s Dilemma
chapters.description: A long email from a young monk in love becomes the central example for why eros cannot be handled by simple renunciation or suppression.

chapters.title: Eros Beyond Sex
chapters.description: He defines eros as broader than sexuality, linked with soulfulness, divinity, cosmopoiesis, and a field of inquiry that can sacralize desire.

chapters.title: Relationship and Practice
chapters.description: The talk turns to romantic love, long-term relationship, and the need to understand how eros lives, weakens, or deepens over time.

chapters.title: Toward a Fuller Psychology
chapters.description: Rob places eros alongside craving, emptiness, and non-fabrication, arguing for a more elaborate Dharma psychology that can open new dimensions of experience.

title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the problem is not clinging itself, but a too-small idea of what non-clinging is for? The talk starts by questioning the familiar ideal of simply “letting go,” then widens the frame to ask what that ideal means for art, love, activism, family, and the full range of human passion. From there it contrasts ordinary craving with subtler investigation, and finally sets two broad directions side by side: deepening insight into emptiness through clinging, and opening to eros as a movement that can deepen soulfulness, beauty, and sacredness.
short_summary: A careful challenge to simplistic non-clinging, moving from craving and emptiness toward eros, soulmaking, and the limits of a narrow Dharma framework.

chapters.title: Returning to non-clinging
chapters.description: He briefly recalls Part 2 and reopens the question of what non-clinging means when applied as a practice and a view.

chapters.title: What the ideal leaves out
chapters.description: He questions whether a life without clinging is even the right aim, especially when considering engagement, personhood, art, family, and love.

chapters.title: Concepts and Dharma fantasy
chapters.description: He contrasts a narrow Dharma framework with broader ways of living, and shows how fantasy and image shape our sense of awakening.

chapters.title: Why simple frames fail
chapters.description: He argues that reducing romantic love, sexuality, and other deep commitments to a small set of concepts cannot adequately hold lived experience.

chapters.title: Three directions of inquiry
chapters.description: He distinguishes ordinary sense desire, subtler clinging tied to fabrication, and a third movement he begins to call eros.

chapters.title: What practice is for
chapters.description: He asks what each kind of investigation can reveal, and whether ease, happiness, or stress reduction are really the whole point of practice.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and poetry
chapters.description: He introduces discrepancy-based processing, then tests it against poetry, beauty, music, and the lived force of wanting.

chapters.title: Eros and soulmaking
chapters.description: He defines eros as a movement that opens depth, beauty, meaning, and sacredness, and asks whether a framework serves soulmaking.

chapters.title: Two broad paths
chapters.description: He closes by saying some practitioners need more work with craving and letting go, while others are being called toward eros and a wider, richer practice.

title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the aim of practice is not to get rid of craving once and for all, but to see how craving, clinging, self, and world are being fabricated in each moment? The talk moves from ordinary meditation experience into a finer-grained exploration of “ways of looking,” showing how releasing contraction can thin out dukkha, loosen self-view, and even change how objects, time, and perception appear. It then widens into Mahayana and Vajrayana territory, where emptiness, appearance, and the role of practice-tools like dependent origination are held together without reducing the path to a simple instruction to “just let go.”
short_summary: A detailed exploration of how clinging fabricates experience, and how different ways of looking can reveal emptiness without reifying it.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Rob frames non-clinging as a deeper question about how suffering is made, and suggests approaching it directly in practice rather than only in moments of obvious craving.

chapters.title: Craving and subtle dukkha
chapters.description: He describes how grasping shows up as bodily and energetic contraction, and how releasing it brings calm, softness, and a more precise sense of dukkha.

chapters.title: Self, world, and fabrication
chapters.description: The talk turns to how craving fabricates self and world together, and how reducing clinging makes appearances lighter, blurrier, and less solid.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: Burbea defines a way of looking as the combined mode of perceiving, relating, and conceiving that shapes experience at every moment.

chapters.title: Spectrum of fabrication
chapters.description: Using dependent origination and papancha, he maps experience as a spectrum from dense obsession to increasingly less fabricated states.

chapters.title: Impermanence and the map
chapters.description: He compares impermanence practice with other views, then sets out the Four Noble Truths and dependent origination as a practical map rather than a final theory.

chapters.title: Empty appearances
chapters.description: A castle-and-tool analogy leads into a sustained argument that appearances are empty, dependent on mind and clinging, and that letting go needs a guiding way of looking.

chapters.title: Perception and Mahayana
chapters.description: The talk enters classical Buddhist puzzles about perception in an arahant or Buddha, and then a Mahayana discussion of emptiness, compassion, and appearance.

chapters.title: Progressive letting go
chapters.description: He concludes by showing how non-clinging develops through subtler forms of clinging, like a ladder or raft, and warns against treating simple letting go as the whole of the Dharma.

title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: What if the common teaching to “just let go” is true, but still too simple to carry the whole path? The talk starts by sorting through the overlapping Pali language of desire, craving, clinging, and fetters, then uses dependent origination to show how these movements actually build suffering in ordinary life. From there it moves into practical ways of meeting craving in real time—through attention to feeling tone, notice of bodily tension, reflection, and the quiet recognition that papanca, or proliferating story, keeps the whole thing spinning. By the end, the talk opens a larger question: whether non-clinging is not only release from suffering, but a doorway into a deeper freedom, emptiness, and a wider sense of beauty and sacredness.
short_summary: The talk examines craving, clinging, and bare attention, then argues that a simple “let go” view of Dharma is too limited for the deeper freedom practice can open.

chapters.title: Opening the context
chapters.description: Burbea explains that this talk is a preface to a later exploration of eros and practice, and sets up the question of what kind of Dharma framework best serves liberation.

chapters.title: Pali terms and fetters
chapters.description: He surveys overlapping Pali words for desire and clinging, using the early texts to show both the usefulness and the limits of fine conceptual distinctions.

chapters.title: Dependent origination map
chapters.description: The talk turns to the twelve links as a practical map for seeing how craving and clinging move into suffering.

chapters.title: Everyday craving examples
chapters.description: Chocolate cake and cigarette craving are used to make the abstract sequence feel immediate and embodied.

chapters.title: Anatomy of clinging
chapters.description: Burbea walks through craving, self-building, and proliferation as processes that tighten experience and reinforce suffering, while recommending actual entry points for practice.

chapters.title: Simple letting go
chapters.description: He considers the familiar instruction to let go of everything, then shows how mindfulness, reflection, and recognizing papanca can support it in limited but real ways.

chapters.title: Working with dukkha
chapters.description: The talk becomes more phenomenological, showing how craving feels in body and mind, including aversion, grasping at pleasure, and the pressure that can be tolerated and released.

chapters.title: Impermanence and its limits
chapters.description: Impermanence is presented as a powerful doorway to letting go, but Burbea says this alone still leaves a narrower view of practice than the Dharma allows.

chapters.title: Beyond simplism
