chapters.description: The closing section points toward deeper insight into the unfabricated, emptiness, and the relation between appearances and timelessness, preparing for the next talk in the series.

title: Tending the Flame
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: The talk begins with the problem of leaving retreat: how to carry openness back into ordinary life without overwhelming yourself or the people around you. From there it moves into the need for Sangha, reading, conversation, and other forms of nourishment that keep the fire of practice alive after silence ends. It then turns to the retreat’s more philosophical edge—how to relate to the imaginal, realism, and the middle way by asking not what is fixedly true, but what is helpful, freeing, and soul-making. The talk closes by widening the frame further, showing how imaginal work, samadhi, metta, mindfulness, emptiness, and daily life can support each other across different periods of practice.
short_summary: A closing talk on transitioning out of retreat, nourishing practice through Sangha and study, and using soul-making as a guide for working with the imaginal.

chapters.title: Leaving Retreat Gently
chapters.description: Burbea opens by speaking to the transition out of silence and the need to notice what the heart and soul may need after retreat.

chapters.title: Sharing With Care
chapters.description: He reflects on sharing retreat experiences with others, stressing sensitivity, timing, and attention to how one is received.

chapters.title: Sangha Keeps the Fire
chapters.description: The talk turns to the nourishment of Sangha, conversation, reading, and listening as essential support for practice after retreat.

chapters.title: The Imaginal Middle Way
chapters.description: He addresses questions around imaginal practice, realism, and the tension between what is real and not real.

chapters.title: Check the Soul-Making
chapters.description: Rather than fixed doctrine, he suggests testing a view by whether it brings aliveness, freedom, and beauty, while remaining wary of authority.

chapters.title: Practice Beyond Retreat
chapters.description: Burbea argues that these practices can be done in short periods and woven into ordinary life, not only on long retreats.

chapters.title: A Wider Practice Tapestry
chapters.description: He situates imaginal and cosmopoetic work within a larger set of mutually nourishing practices and closes by encouraging play, curiosity, and devotion to what the soul longs for.

title: This Fire, This Longing (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What does an image ask of us once it leaves the cushion? The talk moves through that question by considering how imaginal practice can continue off-retreat through reflection, writing, subtle changes in life, or simply reverence, rather than by any fixed rule. It then turns to the tension between healing and devotion, the difference between a mental agenda and the heart’s longing, and how imaginal practice can widen into a change in perception that “re-enchants” the world. In the final stretch, the conversation broadens to image and idea, artistic making, striving and non-striving, and a closing note on responsibility, keeping the focus on what actually helps a practice stay alive.
short_summary: A wide-ranging Q&A on imaginal practice, healing, devotion, art, striving, and how to keep the work soulful without forcing it.

chapters.title: Images Off Retreat
chapters.description: He describes how imaginal figures can be honored through meditation, reflection, writing, creative work, or subtle daily reverence.

chapters.title: Healing and Devotion
chapters.description: He responds to the tension between practicing for oneself and practicing for something larger, and suggests holding both without forcing a dichotomy.

chapters.title: From Mind to Heart
chapters.description: He distinguishes a mental agenda from the heart's yearning and links imaginal healing to a broader re-enchantment of perception.

chapters.title: Image and Idea
chapters.description: He clarifies that image and idea are deeply intertwined, including in insight practice, and that meaningful things often live as both.

chapters.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking
chapters.description: He considers whether suffering can become soulmaking and points to the hidden ideas that shape how dukkha is experienced.

chapters.title: The Artist's Dilemma
chapters.description: He reflects on being an artist and a practitioner at once, including the pull between process and result.

chapters.title: Striving and Result
chapters.description: He uses Natalie Goldberg and his own creative life to question Dharma ideas about effort, stress, and caring about the outcome.

chapters.title: What Is Helpful
chapters.description: He widens the discussion to intention, the danger of subtle self-inflation, and the place of striving and non-striving in different traditions.

chapters.title: Energy Body and Responsibility
chapters.description: He closes by discussing modulating effort in practice, the energy body across the jhanas, and the responsibility we have to the soul and to what feels soulmaking.

title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if meaningfulness is not something added to life, but something that lets a self come alive? The talk moves from Einstein and Jung on the necessity of meaning, into a delicate argument that images, duties, and ways of seeing must be embodied if they are to stay alive rather than remain fascinating ideas. From there it widens into a series of poetic and philosophical touchstones—Hammarskjöld, Chaim Vital, Nietzsche, Orthodox theosis, Kabbalah, and Buddhist practice—to show how the self can be re-seen as open-ended, multiple, and capable of loving its fate without flattening suffering or difference.
short_summary: A quiet, wide-ranging talk on how meaning, image, and practice re-enchant the self and turn ordinary life into an open-ended art.

chapters.title: Meaning and the self
chapters.description: Burbea begins by linking enchantment of self to meaningfulness, using Einstein and Jung to frame meaning as central to human well-being.

chapters.title: Images need embodiment
chapters.description: He argues that beauty, sacredness, and imaginal experience only become real when they are integrated through embodiment, duty, and life.

chapters.title: Yes to the more-than-human
chapters.description: Dag Hammarskjöld’s diary and a Latin phrase are used to suggest that meaning can arise from answering something larger than the human.

chapters.title: Many roots, many worlds
chapters.description: The talk turns to Chaim Vital and the idea of multiple roots, then to the Torah as read differently in each world, widening the frame of interpretation.

chapters.title: Loving fate and divinity
chapters.description: Burbea develops Amor Fati, Nietzsche’s eternal recurrence, and the possibility of becoming divine as a way of loving life without flattening its pain.

chapters.title: Seeing truly
chapters.description: He brings in Christian and Buddhist language about fallen perception, authentic seeing, and theosis to show perception itself as transformable.

chapters.title: Creating and shaping God
chapters.description: Kabbalistic themes of prayer, Shekhinah, and tikkun ha-olam lead into a radical suggestion that practice can both manifest and shape the divine.

chapters.title: Levels of self
chapters.description: Burbea gathers Plotinus, Vajrayana, and related traditions to argue for layered dimensions of being and a trainable way of seeing self and others as Buddha or deity.

chapters.title: Infinite soulmaking
chapters.description: He ends by describing how even the smallest act can carry infinite resonance when the conceptual frame allows ongoing soulmaking, beauty, and sacredness.

title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if the self is not just something to be explained, but something to be re-enchanted? The talk moves through the ways reductionist and overly narrow spiritual views flatten experience, then turns to desire, mind, suffering, and imagination as dimensions that may need to be seen as divine, or at least held with far more room. From there it opens into a wider question of causality: whether the past, the present, the future, or the imaginal may all shape what we are becoming, and how practices and views can either close that down or let it unfold.
short_summary: The talk explores how self, desire, suffering, and imagination might be re-enchanted through flexible ways of looking that go beyond a purely past-based account of experience.

chapters.title: Why disenchantment happens
chapters.description: He begins by naming the flattening effects of reductionist views and some spiritual emphases that can narrow the sense of personhood and divinity.

chapters.title: What needs re-enchanting
chapters.description: He asks which layers of self need re-enchanting, and expands the question to include imaginal forms and the whole spectrum of self-fabrication.

chapters.title: Desire, eros, and legitimacy
chapters.description: He turns to emotions, desire, and eros, drawing on Corbin, Boehme, and Gregory of Nyssa to argue that longing can be holy and must be given room.

chapters.title: Mind and emptiness
chapters.description: He looks at mind, imagination, and the continuum with the divine, then contrasts this with anatta as a skillful way of looking rather than the only one.

chapters.title: The dark and the good
chapters.description: Using Jung, he asks about the order of practice: cultivate the good first, then learn how even darker or rejected material may be seen as divine.

chapters.title: Suffering and re-storying
chapters.description: He explores how judgment, blame, and inherited explanations can hold suffering in place, and how new stories can loosen and heal it.

chapters.title: Frames of causality
chapters.description: He widens the discussion to archetypal causality and then to dependent co-arising, distinguishing past causes from present fabrication.

chapters.title: Dreams, future, and imaginal life
chapters.description: He considers dreams and images as forward-moving as well as past-bearing, and draws on Corbin’s angel out ahead to describe imaginal transformation.

chapters.title: Artifice and soul-making
chapters.description: He closes by linking Jung, Nietzsche, Wilde, and metta practice to the idea that some artifice is necessary for healing, creativity, and soul-making.

title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if the modern sense of self is not simply mistaken, but thinly imagined? The talk moves through cultural critiques of secular humanism, consumerism, and reductionist views of mind, then turns toward Buddhist, Christian, Islamic, and other frames that treat the person as deeper, more sacred, and more open than a fixed identity. From there it asks what kind of view can actually hold both freedom and suffering, and why flexibility of self-view may be central to re-enchanting life without flattening it.
short_summary: A wide-ranging exploration of how culture shapes the self, and how a more flexible view may make room for depth, sacredness, and soul-making.

chapters.title: Cultural confusion
chapters.description: Burbea opens by tracing the mixed cultural inheritance that shapes modern ideas of self, virtue, individualism, and humility.

chapters.title: Empty self, wary culture
chapters.description: He turns to Buddhist notions of no-self and explains how they can be misheard in ways that leave people suspicious of selfhood itself.

chapters.title: Fantasy of origins
chapters.description: He challenges narratives about the pure origins of Buddhism and argues that modern realism and reification are also part of the problem.

chapters.title: Re-enchanting self
chapters.description: Burbea distinguishes different kinds of enchantment and begins to sketch what a re-enchantment of self and other would require.

chapters.title: Secular humanism
chapters.description: Using Charles Taylor and Freud, he describes secular culture as oriented toward purely human flourishing and asks what that leaves out.

chapters.title: What cultures make room for
chapters.description: He contrasts modern Western norms with a Burkina Faso story and then with social critique from Foucault and the Frankfurt School.

chapters.title: Sacred depth of person
chapters.description: Burbea gathers Islamic, Buddhist, and Christian references to suggest that the human being can be seen as far deeper and more sacred than modern flatness allows.

chapters.title: Process and flexibility
chapters.description: He qualifies the view of self as process and argues that dependent origination supports a flexible, non-final self-view.

chapters.title: Dukkha and soul-making
chapters.description: The talk broadens into a soulful frame in which personal events, suffering, and time can become part of soul-making rather than mere problems to escape.

title: The Imaginal and the Middle Way (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you keep an imaginal practice alive without turning it into belief, fantasy, or a new identity? The talk moves through a series of Q&A exchanges on the middle way: practicing for the Buddha or the divine, holding multiple archetypes without forcing an either/or choice, and using practice to find more cooling, equanimity, and space. It also turns toward sacredness in a more staged, pragmatic way, then finishes by opening up the darker side of the imaginal as part of a wider soul-making field.
short_summary: A Q&A on the middle way in imaginal practice, moving from flexible views and devotion to sacredness, renunciation, and the inclusion of dark as well as light images.

chapters.title: Opening the Q&A
chapters.description: Rob begins by setting up the question period and answers the first concern about how to continue imaginal practice when the surrounding culture does not support it.

chapters.title: Support off retreat
chapters.description: He emphasizes nourishment, small openings, and the value of short moments of practice in daily life rather than relying on long continuous retreat conditions.

chapters.title: Practice and intention
chapters.description: In response to a question about intention, he distinguishes practice for self-improvement from practice for God, the Buddha, or the bodhisattva path.

chapters.title: A spectrum of views
chapters.description: Rob expands the frame from humanist service to a more multidimensional view, including deity practice, the cosmic Buddha, and the idea that practice can move through different perspectives over time.

chapters.title: Middle way and wobble
chapters.description: He turns to the middle way between reification and dismissal, saying that apparent razor-edge questions often become more spacious with practice and that wobbling is normal.

chapters.title: Renunciation as imaginal
chapters.description: A question about renunciation leads to a discussion of archetypes, fantasy, and how the monk, artist, or renunciate can remain alive as an image without becoming a rigid life identity.

chapters.title: Permission and practice
chapters.description: Rob stresses the role of permission in retreat, showing how group practice can legitimize hidden sensibilities and make them feel shared rather than private or strange.

chapters.title: Cooling intense experience
chapters.description: He addresses fear around strong experiences by placing them in the context of the wider path, recommending more access to coolness, space, and equanimity alongside bliss and intensity.

chapters.title: Making the sacred
chapters.description: In the discussion of pragmatism, he explains that sacredness often begins with bounded ritual or places before widening into an all-pervasive sense of holiness.

title: Sensing Divinity
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Can divinity be sensed both as a universal oneness and as something alive in the particulars of a self, another person, or even desire itself? The talk moves between those two modes, tracing how meditation, emptiness, metta, and imaginal practice can cool clinging while also re-enchanting personhood rather than erasing it. It then turns into a guided paired exercise: settling in the body, offering well-wishing, receiving it, and looking for the brightness and mystery of consciousness in the other’s eyes.
short_summary: A talk on universal and personal divinity that ends in a guided metta exercise of giving, receiving, and seeing consciousness in the other.

chapters.title: Enchantment and divinity
chapters.description: Rob introduces the retreat's use of enchantment and divinity, and says he will focus on how these meanings shape the path.

chapters.title: Universal oneness
chapters.description: He describes a universal sense of divinity that can appear as love, awareness, silence, peace, or oneness, often through deep practice.

chapters.title: Desire and equanimity
chapters.description: Using Thomas Merton and Catherine McGee as context, he shows how universal divinity can cool desire and widen the space around life.

chapters.title: Personal and imaginal
chapters.description: He turns to a more personal divinity in and through figures, objects, the self, and the imaginal, emphasizing that the two modes overlap.

chapters.title: Re-enchanting the self
chapters.description: Rob asks what it would mean to know one's own divinity and how emptiness, metta, and imaginal practice can support that seeing.

chapters.title: Perfection and surrender
chapters.description: He reflects on Neem Karoli Baba and Thomas Merton to show how surrender, poetry, and universal perception can reveal a deeper perfection without bypassing suffering.

chapters.title: Buddha nature and tantra
chapters.description: Rob moves through Tantra, Judaism, and Buddha nature to suggest that desire, anger, action, and consciousness itself can be experienced as blessed or divine.

chapters.title: Divine fire in particulars
chapters.description: He settles the teaching into the claim that personhood need not be erased but can be inflamed and perceived as divine in its unique details.

chapters.title: Metta and the gaze
chapters.description: The talk becomes a guided pair practice: giving and receiving metta, then sensing the consciousness and radiance in another person's eyes before closing with blessing and care.

title: Creating the Path (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How does an image become sacred without being reduced to either a fantasy of the mind or an object with powers all its own? In this Q&A, the talk moves from Tara, Mickey Mouse, and the dependent arising of art into a careful middle way: perception is shaped by practice, culture, devotion, and the energy body, and what appears can change as those conditions change. It then turns to erotically charged imaginal connection, the danger of getting swept away, and the question of what a path is really for if freedom is not the only thing you most want.
short_summary: A Q&A that explores imaginal perception, cosmopoiesis, erotic connection, and the risks and values that shape a Dharma path.

chapters.title: Imaginal forms
chapters.description: Rob weighs different ways of understanding Tara and other divine figures, from an objective imaginal realm to the role of mind and culture in perception.

chapters.title: Art and dependence
chapters.description: He uses Beethoven and art theory to show that aesthetic response arises dependently from the work, the listener, and the surrounding culture.

chapters.title: Practice and sacredness
chapters.description: The discussion turns to how practice changes perception, including seeing Tara in ordinary or even difficult objects, and to a practical way of judging whether something is truly wholesome.

chapters.title: Cosmopoesis
chapters.description: Rob explains how imaginal perception can spill over into the world so that the whole cosmos is seen as light, fire, music, or Tara's presence.

chapters.title: Eros and otherness
chapters.description: He distinguishes oneness experiences from an imaginal mode in which a figure retains character, otherness, and erotic charge without collapsing into merging.

chapters.title: Texture and autonomy
chapters.description: A listener asks about kinesthetic experience, and Rob clarifies that image may be nonvisual, may hold a subtle self-sense, and can feel autonomous without being separate.

chapters.title: Emptiness and fluidity
chapters.description: He brings the teaching back to dependent arising and emptiness, emphasizing that self and image have no inherent existence and can shift fluidly from moment to moment.

chapters.title: Paths and dangers
chapters.description: A question about Theravada and Mahayana leads into a wider reflection on spiritual ambition, fear about outcomes, and the specific dangers of each path, including being swept away or reifying experience.

chapters.title: What matters most
chapters.description: In the closing movement, Rob asks whether freedom is really the deepest value for each person, and invites an honest look at sacredness, beauty, art, and authenticity.

title: Images of the Breath: Energy Body, Colour, and the Beloved (Guided Meditation)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: The talk begins with a simple question: what changes when breath is felt not just as air in the body, but as energy, image, and field? It moves slowly through several entry points — lower belly, feet, back, heart, crown, and front of the body — while repeatedly inviting the listener to notice what feels good, subtle, or right rather than forcing a fixed method. From there it opens into light and color, then into the possibility of an imaginal beloved, so that breath becomes a way of sensing relationship, blessing, and the felt shape of the world.
short_summary: A guided meditation that explores breath as energy body, light, color, and imaginal relationship, moving from inner sensation into a wider sense of blessing and connection.

chapters.title: Why images matter
chapters.description: Burbea briefly frames imaginal practice as something that develops with familiarity and interest, and notes how it can interweave with samadhi and emptiness practice.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The guided meditation begins by establishing posture, spreading awareness through the body, and sensing the energy field as alive and responsive.

chapters.title: Lower centers and grounding
chapters.description: He explores breathing at the lower belly, perineum, feet, and lower back, inviting attention to what feels comfortable and good.

chapters.title: Heart and crown
chapters.description: The practice expands to the back of the heart and then the crown of the head, always emphasizing that the whole body remains involved.

chapters.title: Front of body opening
chapters.description: Attention moves to the chest and solar plexus, with the breath imagined as opening and softening the body from the front.

chapters.title: Porous field of breath
chapters.description: Burbea invites the sense of breath and energy arriving from all sides or growing from within, and lets the breath become very subtle or still.

chapters.title: Light and color
chapters.description: The breath is then felt as light, given color, and noticed for its different qualities, with green, red, and blue used as examples of distinct felt tones.

chapters.title: Blessing radiance
chapters.description: The light is allowed to radiate outward as healing or blessing, reshaping the surrounding world into a sacred mandala or Buddha realm.

chapters.title: Meeting the beloved
chapters.description: An imaginal figure, perhaps a beloved, is brought into the field and the meditation explores breathing together, passing breath back and forth, and opening to love.

title: From Inertia, Towards the Solutio (A Fragment)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do we loosen the mind’s inertia enough for perception to become more fluid, more creative, and less locked into habit? The talk moves from the basic support of inhabiting the energy body toward opening the senses, especially in walking and standing meditation, where a different awareness of the elements and the landscape can be invited. Rather than forcing a special state, the emphasis is on softening the gaze and attitude so that a more prayerful, sacred way of relating can emerge and be gently supported.
short_summary: A short talk on loosening habitual perception through the energy body, softening, and walking meditation in a sacred landscape.

chapters.title: Opening the senses
chapters.description: The talk begins by extending the retreat’s instruction to stay in the energy body into a broader openness of the senses.

chapters.title: Habit and inertia
chapters.description: Burbea names the mind as habitual and describes the repeated ways we see, think, and perceive.

chapters.title: Softening perception
chapters.description: He explores how a slight softening in gaze, attitude, and heart can loosen rigid perception and allow more fluid seeing.

chapters.title: Walking prayerfully
chapters.description: The teaching turns to walking meditation, sacred landscape, and the possibility of moving with a prayerful quality.

chapters.title: Body and self-consciousness
chapters.description: He notes the inertia of body and culture, including self-consciousness about movement practices, and encourages attention to that resistance.

chapters.title: From loosening to creation
chapters.description: Burbea emphasizes that only after loosening do we notice how locked in we were, and that this openness makes room for sculpting and creativity.

chapters.title: Returning to practice
chapters.description: He closes by inviting listeners to notice what calls them in the time before the next meeting.

title: Matter, Bodies, Worlds (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if the world we take to be solid, single, and objective is partly shaped by the way we look at it? The talk moves from a critique of modern reductionism and mind-matter dualism into Buddhist, Kabbalistic, and tantric ways of seeing many worlds, many bodies, and different levels of time, always returning to experience rather than abstract belief. It then turns practical: how perception can be trained, how the body can be re-enchanted, and how sacredness may be approached in stages without rushing past what is actually workable.
short_summary: A wide-ranging exploration of how perception, body, world, and time are co-created, and how practice can open them into a more sacred and imaginal experience.

chapters.title: Bottom-Up Assumptions
chapters.description: Burbea opens by examining the modern reductionist picture of reality and the hidden assumptions that make it feel self-evident.

chapters.title: Many Worlds, Many Traditions
chapters.description: He brings in Kabbalah, Buddhism, Islam, and related traditions to contrast modern flat materialism with older cosmologies of multiple worlds.

chapters.title: Quantum and Mind-Matter
chapters.description: The talk turns to quantum examples, the observer, and Descartes, showing how scientific ideals can harden into dogma.

chapters.title: Mind-Made Body
chapters.description: Burbea moves into Buddhist sources and a retreat anecdote to show how meditative practice can reveal other kinds of body and perception.

chapters.title: Phenomenology and Plotinus
chapters.description: He emphasizes experience over speculation, then uses Plotinus to illustrate a flexible way of conceiving matter and divinity.

chapters.title: Energy Body and Alchemy
chapters.description: The talk develops the energy body as one route to re-enchantment and then frames alchemy as a transubstantiation occurring in perception.

chapters.title: Mandala and Mantra
chapters.description: Burbea describes the world as sacred space, including mandala practice and hearing sound as mantra, with a looser, more creative retreat style.

chapters.title: Time and Timelessness
chapters.description: He turns to imaginal time, citing Corbett, Alex Wayman, and Walter Wink to describe a timeless dimension within appearance.

chapters.title: Pragmatic Closing
chapters.description: The talk ends by stressing stages, provisional dualities, and the recurring question of what is actually helpful in practice.

title: Matter, Bodies, Worlds (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What changes when matter is not taken as a fixed, self-evident reality, but as something shaped by view, imagination, and practice? The talk begins with a bird-garden story to contrast a reductionist, survival-based reading of life with more enchanted ways of seeing, then moves through self and world, psychotherapy, and the question of whether practice is oriented toward the self or toward the divine. From there it opens into matter, body, space, and time, bringing in emptiness, dependent origination, and critiques of scientism to ask what kinds of worlds our assumptions actually create.
short_summary: A wide-ranging inquiry into how views of matter, body, and science shape the worlds we inhabit, and how re-enchantment might open them again.

chapters.title: Birds and reductionism
chapters.description: A garden story introduces the contrast between a harsh reductionist reading of life and a more open, less deluded way of seeing.

chapters.title: Self and world together
chapters.description: Rob shows how views of self and world co-arise, and how healing is limited when it sits inside a soulless background story.

chapters.title: Re-enchantment and devotion
chapters.description: He develops the idea that self and world can be re-enchanted together, with practice oriented either toward self-improvement or toward the divine.

chapters.title: Objects as theophanies
chapters.description: Using amulets, charms, and ordinary objects, he contrasts self-centered infatuation with seeing things as faces of the divine or as appearances rather than fixed realities.

chapters.title: Matter, mater, origins
chapters.description: The talk turns to matter and its links with mother, origin, and fantasy, showing how assumptions about embodiment are often concretized without being noticed.

chapters.title: Mature imaginal vision
chapters.description: Rob argues for a mature imaginal enchantment in which particulars disclose divinity in and through matter rather than outside it.

chapters.title: Nietzsche on scientism
chapters.description: He brings in Nietzsche to criticize scientism, measurable truth, and the flattening of existence into calculation, while defending ambiguity and reverence.

chapters.title: Horkheimer, Adorno, and limits
chapters.description: Horkheimer and Adorno deepen the critique by linking mechanistic reason to domination, alienation, and a diminished understanding of nature.

chapters.title: Science, space, and openness
chapters.description: He closes by noting how science itself can unsettle fixed ideas of matter, space, and time, and by returning to emptiness, dependent origination, and resistance to single vision.

title: Neither 'Is' nor 'Is Not': Of Emptiness and the Imaginal (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What happens when truth, emptiness, and image pull in different directions? The talk moves from an ethical question about when life demands a plain response into examples like climate change, art, and personal practice, showing how emptiness need not mean indifference but can support care, responsibility, and re-enchantment. In the second half, the conversation turns to soul-making and imaginal practice: how images can serve growth, or be held as primary presences, and how an image can become lively enough to seem to spread into the world.
short_summary: A Q&A on how emptiness, truth, ethics, and the imaginal can be held together without collapsing into realism or nihilism.

chapters.title: Truth Demands Response
chapters.description: Rob begins with the idea that truth can require action, using climate change and meditation as examples of when to yield to reality rather than fantasy.

chapters.title: Ethics And Commitment
chapters.description: He gives a personal example of dropping Zen practice after learning about wartime complicity, and clarifies that imaginal relationship is a conscious entering into, not a fixed stance.

chapters.title: Climate Change And Fantasy
chapters.description: The discussion broadens into how facts gather fantasies around them, including fantasies of the meditator and activist, while emptiness still allows care and embodied response.

chapters.title: Art Into Emptiness
chapters.description: Rob points to art and poetry as a way in for people who relate more easily through aesthetic or symbolic meaning, then links that to freeing things from rigid ideas of real and not real.

chapters.title: Ways Of Looking
chapters.description: He states the emptiness teaching more directly: there is no fixed thing behind appearances, only ways of looking, and the loss of care is a sign that the understanding is off.

chapters.title: Plural Souls
chapters.description: A question about soul-making leads into a wider account of multiple inner voices, with comedy and ethical action both named as possible and sometimes contrasting forms of nourishment.

chapters.title: Images And Divinity
chapters.description: Rob explains that 'imaginal' already carries a sense of divinity, and distinguishes using images to cultivate qualities from serving the image as a primary presence.

chapters.title: Cosmopoiesis
chapters.description: He clarifies cosmopoiesis as an image becoming alive and spreading into the world, using Tara as the example, and closes by encouraging the listener to trust what is working.

title: Hearing All Sounds as Mantra: A Jewelline World
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if the point of practice is not to hold one object, but to learn how attention can move: from body well-being, to emotion, to image, to the sensed world, and finally to sound itself? The talk begins by laying out those overlapping directions, then turns into a long guided experiment with Om Mani Padme Hum, first as chant, then as a way of opening the heart, the energy body, and the imagination. From there the listening widens further: sounds in the room, in the wind, in speech, even in pain and awkward noise are invited as mantra, so the practice becomes an exercise in re-sensing the world as a kind of living mandala. The talk ends by stressing that this is not a formula but an invitation to play, test, and find what actually opens perception.
short_summary: A guided chant and listening practice that moves from the energy body into a wider, imaginative hearing of all sounds as mantra and divine speech.

chapters.title: Retreat directions
chapters.description: Rob briefly reviews five overlapping practice directions, including samadhi, emotion, imaginal practice, cosmopoiesis, and the energy body.

chapters.title: Mantra as experiment
chapters.description: He introduces Om Mani Padme Hum and explains that the point is not a fixed doctrine but a flexible, creative use of chant.

chapters.title: Settling into chant
chapters.description: The group is guided into posture, heart-centered awareness, and shared chanting led by Catherine McGee.

chapters.title: Spell and blessing
chapters.description: Rob deepens the chant as enchantment, divine speech, and a field of blessing woven into the cosmos.

chapters.title: Imaginal listening widens
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward cosmopoiesis, with the mantra held lightly and the sense of sacred sound extending beyond the spoken phrase.

chapters.title: Walking as mandala
chapters.description: Participants move around the room while keeping awareness of the energy body, the heart, and the room as a kind of mandala.

chapters.title: All sounds as mantra
chapters.description: Rob extends the practice to voices, birdsong, coughing, wind, and even difficult sounds, as a way of re-enchanting perception.

chapters.title: Sound, meaning, and play
chapters.description: He reflects on speech as miracle and closes by urging practitioners to experiment freely and find their own way.

title: Healing the Imagination (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What does it mean to trust what works in practice when the mind insists one thing and the body says another? In this Q&A, the talk moves from that question into a practical compass of release, relief, harmony, and loosening in the energy body, especially in imaginal and mindfulness practice. It then widens into questions of lingering with images, the balance of soul and spirit, and how different people may need different paths over time. The closing exchange turns to a strong metta experience and how to work with it when it feels overwhelming, boring, or deeply alive.
short_summary: A Q&A on using the energy body as a guide in imaginal practice, with reflections on soul and spirit, Tantra, and metta.

chapters.title: What works in practice
chapters.description: Rob first clarifies that “follow what works” is usually about meditation in the moment, not only a life path or career decision.

chapters.title: Energy body as compass
chapters.description: He explains how the energy body can show harmony, openness, or loosening even when the mind is skeptical about an image or practice.

chapters.title: Release and relief
chapters.description: Rob describes subtle release, relief, and ease as signs that insight or mindfulness practice is moving in the right direction.

chapters.title: Working with images
chapters.description: He responds to questions about rapidly changing imaginal material, suggesting steadiness, patience, and attention to whether apparently separate images may be aspects of one unfolding image.

chapters.title: Gestures and posture
chapters.description: The conversation turns to mudras and how small changes in hand position can alter the felt tone of practice, sometimes making a marked difference.

chapters.title: Tantra and emptiness
chapters.description: A question about Tantra leads into a broader reflection on pleasure, grasping, and the Buddhist teaching on kilesas, including the deeper move of seeing them as empty.

chapters.title: Soul, spirit, and fit
chapters.description: Rob develops a nuanced account of soulmaking, spirit, discipline, and transcendence, arguing that different people need different proportions of imagery, eros, purity, and spaciousness.

chapters.title: Metta as energy
chapters.description: In the final question, he offers practical ways to work with strong metta: letting energy move outward, opening to its non-personal or divine dimension, and bringing it into contact with pain.

title: The Movement of Devotion (Live and Shortened Version)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: The talk asks how practice changes when the aim is not tight concentration but a more open, flexible field of experience. It moves through a careful exploration of what supports imaginal practice: less clinging and fabrication, a blending of the senses, and a heart posture of receptivity, humility, and surrender. From there, it turns practical, showing how images, prayer, devotion, the energy body, and even simple shifts in posture can alter what becomes available in meditation.
short_summary: An exploration of how openness, devotion, and subtle bodily shifts can open imaginal practice and deepen perception.

chapters.title: Framing the retreat
chapters.description: Rob begins by situating the talk within a retreat where practice is expected to unfold differently for different people and at different stages.

chapters.title: Openness over concentration
chapters.description: He explains that the retreat is oriented less toward concentration as a goal and more toward openness of mind, perception, heart, and experiment.

chapters.title: Experimenting with view
chapters.description: Rob shows how eyes open or closed, and especially the way one relates to what is perceived, can either contract or open practice.

chapters.title: Flexibility and imaginal work
chapters.description: Drawing on Catherine McGee’s framing, he describes moving flexibly between emotion, samadhi, and imaginal practice while staying sensitive to the body.

chapters.title: What opens the imaginal
chapters.description: He names less fabrication and sensory blending as important conditions for imaginal opening, and explains these as effects of reduced clinging.

chapters.title: The heart's posture
chapters.description: Rob emphasizes receptivity, surrender, and humility as practical stances of the heart that allow imaginal experience to become alive and soulmaking.

chapters.title: Divinity and devotion
chapters.description: He uses illness, blessing, and Tibetan yidam practice to show how devotion, prayer, and tantric preliminaries can deepen imaginal contact.

chapters.title: Prayer, receiving, and union
chapters.description: Rob expands prayer into supplication, praise, and blessing, and describes how relationship with an imaginal figure can move through receiving, harmonizing, and union.

chapters.title: Body, movement, and discernment
chapters.description: He closes by showing how compassion, posture, mudra, walking meditation, and even prostration can shift energy and perception, then asks listeners to keep only what is useful.

title: The Movement of Devotion (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Prayer here is taken apart and widened: from asking for help, to praise, blessing, devotion, surrender, and a sense of receiving or emanating something larger than the self. The talk then turns practical, tracing how these qualities show up in body, energy body, posture, mudra, and even subtle shifts in how perception itself is organized. It ends by asking when outer enactment helps and when a quieter inner bowing or inward devotion is more alive and soulmaking.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of prayer, devotion, and the subtle ways body, perception, and energy shape one another in practice.

chapters.title: Prayer beyond asking
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes supplication from a more open, humble form of prayer that does not tighten the self.

chapters.title: Praise and blessing
chapters.description: He broadens prayer into praise and blessing, describing how these qualities can become hard to put into words and remain rooted in the imaginal.

chapters.title: Widening perception
chapters.description: The talk traces how blessing can move from one object to body, world, and knowing itself, through the senses and a cosmopoetic way of seeing.

chapters.title: Flavors of devotion
chapters.description: He turns to the different intensities and mixtures of prayerfulness, devotion, love, compassion, and metta, emphasizing discernment over hierarchy.

chapters.title: Body and samadhi
chapters.description: Rob outlines the interrelationship of body, energy body, and psyche, including the role of pīti and how well-being can affect posture and feeling.

chapters.title: Subtle movement and mudra
chapters.description: He invites experimentation with uprightness, chin angle, hand posture, and spontaneous inner or outer movement as ways to tune practice.

chapters.title: Touching the earth
chapters.description: Using the Buddha-touching-earth image, he shows how mudra and posture can shift emotion, energy, attitude, and attention.

chapters.title: Inner or outer devotion
chapters.description: The closing section weighs physical enactment against inward bowing and surrender, asking which mode is more soulmaking in the moment.

title: The Movement of Devotion (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: If the point of practice is not to win a battle with thought, what actually changes the mind? The talk moves from samadhi as a more subtle, less grasping relation to thought into a wider experiment with openness, flexible attention, and the energy body, then into imaginal practice as a way of perceiving the world differently. From there it maps the conditions that let devotion, surrender, and imaginative perception become alive: less fabrication, a receptive heart, and a way of sensing that blends body, image, and world. The talk closes by sketching how imaginal practice can move from supplication to harmony and, at times, to a felt union that changes how the cosmos is perceived.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of how less fabrication, openness, and devotional imagination can open perception into imaginal and cosmopoetic experience.

chapters.title: Practice Directions
chapters.description: He outlines the retreat’s flexible orientations—samadhi, emotional work, and imaginal practice—and cautions against reducing meditation to suppressing thought.

chapters.title: Beyond Concentration
chapters.description: The talk distinguishes true practice from chasing blankness, emphasizing subtlety, openness, and agility of attention instead of rigid focus.

chapters.title: Experimental Practice
chapters.description: He frames practice as inquiry into what feeds or starves wholesome states, and extends that experimental stance to the retreat’s broader methods.

chapters.title: Imaginal Opening
chapters.description: Rob introduces imaginal practice and disagrees with the idea that it only arrives as spontaneous grace, suggesting it can be deliberately supported.

chapters.title: Four Supporting Factors
chapters.description: He lays out four conditions for imaginal and cosmopoetic perception: less fabrication, synesthesia, an open heart, and imaginal perception itself.

chapters.title: Humility and Surrender
chapters.description: Using his illness as an example, he shows how acknowledging helplessness can open the heart into devotion, surrender, and encounter with the divine.

chapters.title: Preliminaries and Devotion
chapters.description: He compares the imaginal path to tantric preliminaries, stressing that the heart’s posture must be prepared before the imaginal can feel alive.

chapters.title: Three Modes of Relation
chapters.description: Rob maps imaginal practice as invocation, harmonizing with qualities, and deeper union, using Avalokiteshvara and Yamantaka as contrasting examples.

chapters.title: Transubstantiated Perception
chapters.description: He closes by comparing imaginal communion to Eucharistic transubstantiation, where the world itself is sensed as altered and divine.

title: The Art of Perception
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if perception is not a neutral report of reality, but a field we help make, inhabit, and transform? The talk moves from historical views of the imaginal, through emptiness and dependent arising, into a detailed account of how image, body, heart, and meaning work together in practice. Along the way it uses art, alchemy, and a series of literary and philosophical examples to argue that seeing image as image can loosen modern certainty without collapsing into fantasy.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on how emptiness, imagination, and art can turn perception into a practice of re-enchantment.

chapters.title: The imaginal realm
chapters.description: Burbea opens by sketching older views of an imaginal realm with its own reality and influence, before turning to the modern prejudice against it.

chapters.title: Religion and reading
chapters.description: He uses Walter Wink and Hugh of St. Victor to show how resurrection, ascension, and reading were once understood as transformative and sympathetic rather than merely literal or informational.

chapters.title: Knowledge shifts
chapters.description: A historical arc through Aristotle, Plato, Plotinus, and the rise of modernism shows how reasoning and fixed categories came to outweigh imaginal ways of knowing.

chapters.title: Truth and play
chapters.description: He brings in Niels Bohr and emptiness to suggest that truth is not neatly packageable and that openness allows a more playful relation to views.

chapters.title: Art of perception
chapters.description: Burbea develops the phrase 'the art of perception,' linking alchemy, image as image, and the transformation of perception into a poetic practice.

chapters.title: What makes the imaginal
chapters.description: He defines the imaginal through several qualities: soulfulness, depth, otherness, meaningfulness, and beauty, while stressing that it is more than ordinary fantasy.

chapters.title: Importing meaning
chapters.description: Nietzsche and other examples help him show that we always bring something to what we perceive, and that interpretation is open and multiple rather than fixed.

chapters.title: Imagination as salvation
chapters.description: With Blake, Jesus, and the Buddha bodies, he frames imagination as a way out of flatness and nihilism, and as a redemptive mode of perception.

chapters.title: Questioning the familiar
chapters.description: The closing movement broadens the talk into criticism, culture, and modernity: art, myth, and inquiry can loosen inherited assumptions and make perception a lived path.

title: Energy and Transubstantiation: The Alchemy of the Body (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: How do you work with the energy body without turning it into one fixed experience? The talk moves from clarifying language and the difference between energy body and pīti, into practical guidance on resting with pleasure, steadiness, or agitation, and then into how imagery and imagination can open perception without losing contact with the body. Later it widens further to jhana, the flexibility of the practice, and the idea that perception itself can re-enchant the world.
short_summary: A Q&A on energy body practice that moves from pīti and jhana into imaginal work, agitation, and the idea that perception can creatively re-enchant experience.

chapters.title: What the Energy Body Means
chapters.description: Rob explains why he says “the energy body” rather than “my energy body,” using it to loosen identification and reduce the sense of self around bodily experience.

chapters.title: Getting Familiar
chapters.description: He responds to questions about difficulty sensing the energy body outside the hall, encouraging easier conditions, repeated contact, and a gradual widening of awareness.

chapters.title: Awareness, Space, Core
chapters.description: Rob suggests enlarging awareness or gently centering in the body, and introduces imagination as a practical support for sensing a central axis.

chapters.title: What Is Pīti
chapters.description: He defines pīti as pleasant bodily-space experience from a non-sensual origin, then distinguishes it from the broader field of the energy body.

chapters.title: Pleasure and Samadhi
chapters.description: Using a musical overtone analogy, Rob explains different wavelengths of bodily pleasure, how they can be explored, and how they relate to the Buddha’s map of the jhanas.

chapters.title: Working With Jhana
chapters.description: He outlines three broad modes of practice—steadiness in samadhi, sensitivity to emotional resonances, and imaginal work—and notes that jhana is a territory rather than a switch.

chapters.title: Love Figure and Cosmopoiesis
chapters.description: Rob answers questions about the imaginal figure of love, contrasting personal dissolution with a more distinctive “cosmopoietic” unfolding that keeps the figure’s character alive.

chapters.title: Agitation and Care
chapters.description: He reassures listeners that agitation, contraction, and grief are part of the field, and emphasizes responding with care, warmth, spaciousness, and attention to aversion.

chapters.title: Re-Enchantment
chapters.description: The talk closes by returning to the retreat’s language of re-enchanting the cosmos, with perception described as creative and poetic, something that can sing beauty into being.

title: On Orientations in Practice (Live and Shortened version)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rather than trying to stay continuously mindful, the talk keeps returning to a more flexible question: what changes when practice moves deliberately between different ways of looking? It begins with the energy body as a felt field around the body, then shows how that sensitivity can support samadhi, emotional work, and the signs that a practice is on track. From there, the speaker widens into a critique of entrenched Dharma habits—focus on one object, atomistic attention, strong reality claims, and self-sufficiency—and invites a more experimental, creative, and soul-guided approach.
short_summary: A talk on the energy body, flexible ways of looking, and loosening the habits that can limit practice.

chapters.title: Opening the retreat
chapters.description: Rob opens the morning talk and says the retreat will begin with emphasis on the energy body.

chapters.title: What the energy body is
chapters.description: He defines the energy body as a field of vibration and a different sensibility from ordinary mindfulness of sensation.

chapters.title: Why it matters
chapters.description: He explains how the energy body supports samadhi, emotional work, and a kind of inner navigation that can show when practice is on track.

chapters.title: Listening beyond the box
chapters.description: He pauses to reflect on how we hear teachings inside our existing framework and invites listening for the larger structure.

chapters.title: Loosening entrenched habits
chapters.description: He questions the assumptions of continuity, one-pointed focus, atomistic attention, realism, and self-sufficiency.

chapters.title: Flexibility and precision
chapters.description: He presents deliberate shifts in ways of looking as the path to insight, and says precision lies in subtle distinctions, not microscopic scrutiny.

chapters.title: Subtlety and soulfulness
chapters.description: He develops the role of subtlety in practice and encourages using soul resonance to tell whether a practice is alive.

chapters.title: Practice as art
chapters.description: He describes the retreat as a studio for experimentation, where the deeper point is loosening, beauty, sacredness, and a more spacious knowing.

chapters.title: Sitting and walking instructions
chapters.description: He ends with practical guidance for open-schedule sitting and walking meditation, asking students to keep the energy body in view and adjust pace and posture by what helps.

title: "I Sing The Body Electric": On Orientations in Practice
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if practice is less about holding one object continuously and more about learning how attention changes the world it meets? The talk begins with the energy body as a subtle field of sensation, then shows how that sensitivity can support samadhi, metta, emotional work, imaginal practice, and insight. From there it turns critical, questioning five common Dharma assumptions—summed up as CRASS—and ends by arguing that flexibility, play, and the attitude of art or poetry can loosen fixed views and open recurring experiences of beauty, sacredness, and timelessness.
short_summary: A talk on the energy body, samadhi, and flexible ways of looking, moving from a critique of narrow Dharma habits toward practice as loosening, re-enchantment, and subtle perception.

chapters.title: Opening: Energy Body
chapters.description: Rob sets the retreat's main emphasis on sensing the energy body rather than repeating basic instructions about it.

chapters.title: What Energy Body Means
chapters.description: He defines the energy body as a subtler mode of mindfulness that overlaps with ordinary sensation but tunes attention differently.

chapters.title: Useful Across Practices
chapters.description: He shows how energy-body awareness supports samadhi, emotional life, imaginal work, metta, and insight by revealing shifts in contraction and release.

chapters.title: Body Electric
chapters.description: Whitman's line becomes a frame for the body as alive, electric, and shaped by attention, with perception itself understood as fabricated.

chapters.title: Walking in the Field
chapters.description: He gives practical guidance for walking and moving in a way that stays sensitive to texture, vibration, and pace.

chapters.title: Five Crass Assumptions
chapters.description: Rob names concentration, atomism, realism, solitude, and sanitization as common habits that can limit Dharma practice.

chapters.title: Crass to Scars
chapters.description: He sharpens the critique with the CRASS and SCARS mnemonics, then returns to the central role of subtlety and dependent origination.

chapters.title: Precision and Subtlety
chapters.description: Precision is redefined as discernment among ways of looking, and he uses the Anapanasati Sutta to show that breathing practice includes well-being as well as attention.

chapters.title: Loosening Into Beauty
chapters.description: In the closing movement, he contrasts passive openness with deliberate practice, and says the real point is loosening so that timelessness, beauty, and sacredness can arise more often.

title: Mindfulness and Myth
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if mindfulness is not the place where reality is finally revealed, but one possible way of looking among many? The talk moves from a clarification of priorities on the path, through a critique of realism, scientism, and modern disenchantment, toward a case for emptiness and dependent origination as the basis of practice. From there it turns practical: how to hear teachings without forcing them into old boxes, and how imaginal or re-enchanting approaches can open into a wider field rather than clash with the Dharma.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk arguing that emptiness, not hidden realism, should ground practice, and that teachings need to be heard in a more open, structural way.

chapters.title: Setting the spectrum
chapters.description: He clarifies the retreat's basic orientation by describing a spectrum between freedom and healing on one side and beauty, sacredness, and soulmaking on the other.

chapters.title: Personal framework
chapters.description: He frames teachings as personal confessions, then explains that the retreat's emphasis is a response to the surrounding cultural and Dharma context.

chapters.title: Realism everywhere
chapters.description: He surveys religious, secular, psychological, and Dharma forms of realism and argues that non-realist practice is rare and often unstable in received forms.

chapters.title: Secularism and disenchantment
chapters.description: He critiques aggressive secularism, compares it with religious fundamentalism, and begins to describe the broader cultural loss of enchantment.

chapters.title: Scientism and flat cosmos
chapters.description: He turns to modern disenchantment, scientific materialism, and scientism, contrasting these with emptiness as a radically different basis for practice.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: He explains practice as the flexible entertaining of different ways of looking, with all Dharma concepts treated as skillful perspectives rather than truths.

chapters.title: Emptiness as basis
chapters.description: He argues that emptiness and dependent origination can become the actual ground of the path, and that many modern renderings of mindfulness still smuggle in realism.

chapters.title: Listening structurally
chapters.description: He shows how imaginal practice can coexist with emptiness, then shifts to the practical question of how to listen for what is new, relevant, and structurally coherent.

chapters.title: Teaching in chunks
chapters.description: He closes by reminding listeners that teachings arrive in pieces, that later sections can reframe earlier ones, and that the retreat's themes are mutually dependent.

title: Beyond the Search for Freedom
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if the path is not mainly about getting less stressed, but about opening more deeply to beauty, sacredness, meaning, and the imaginal? The talk begins by questioning why one person’s happiness seems to come less from technique than from living inside an enchanted cosmos, then widens into a careful challenge to the usual Buddhist framing of practice as coping with suffering. It keeps returning to the tension between self-healing and a more radical orientation toward soulmaking, before ending with Jung’s sense that the deepest human tasks are never fully finished.
short_summary: A wide-ranging inquiry into whether practice is really about less suffering, or about a deeper opening to beauty, sacredness, meaning, and an open-ended freedom.

chapters.title: Retreat frame
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the retreat’s themes and situates re-enchantment within the larger context of practice and contemporary life.

chapters.title: Matthieu Ricard question
chapters.description: He uses Matthieu Ricard’s unusual happiness as a pivot to ask whether joy comes more from technique or from inhabiting an enchanted cosmos.

chapters.title: Coping or opening
chapters.description: The talk contrasts mindfulness as coping with a wider possibility: practices that deepen sensitivity, reveal sacredness, and loosen fabrication.

chapters.title: What practice is for
chapters.description: He asks listeners to examine their own aim on the path and considers a more radical orientation toward beauty and sacredness rather than only suffering reduction.

chapters.title: Buddhism and modern models
chapters.description: Burbea reflects on whether this emphasis still counts as Buddhism, and how Buddhist, medical, and modern spiritual framings tend to center suffering relief.

chapters.title: Self, God, and meaning
chapters.description: He speaks more personally about what he cares about in practice, then develops the role of self-orientation, divine orientation, and meaningfulness.

chapters.title: Priority reversal
chapters.description: The talk explores the complex effects of reversing priorities from self-healing to beauty and sacredness, including why that may be healing in itself.

chapters.title: Who practice serves
chapters.description: Burbea addresses the people for whom art, soulfulness, and sacredness matter most, and links these concerns to the open-endedness of the path.

chapters.title: Jung and open-ended freedom
chapters.description: He closes with Jung’s remarks on individuation and life’s problems as support for a path that stays unfinished, alive, and meaningful.

title: The Nature of Enchantment
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: What if re-enchantment is not a return to nature, but a shift in how perception itself is made? The talk begins by using a Guardian article about trees, stress, and city life as a foil, then turns to what is hidden in modernist and scientistic ways of framing value, reality, and nature. From there it opens into a practice-based account of subtlety, sensitivity, imaginal perception, and the need for depth, dimensionality, and a more poetic way of looking.
short_summary: Using a newspaper article about trees in cities as a starting point, the talk develops a practice-centered case for re-enchantment as a shift in perception, not just a greener environment.

chapters.title: Retreat framing
chapters.description: Rob welcomes participants and outlines the four areas he will introduce: what re-enchantment means, why it matters, the retreat's context and bases, and how to approach the practices.

chapters.title: Emptiness as basis
chapters.description: He explains that the retreat assumes familiarity with emptiness, ways of looking, fabrication, imaginal practice, emotions, samatha, metta, and the energy body, and he emphasizes emptiness as a basis rather than a distant goal.

chapters.title: Trees and critique
chapters.description: Using a Guardian article about trees in cities, he highlights what is useful in the piece and then critiques its modernist assumptions, its flat view of nature, and its reliance on measurable value.

chapters.title: Depth and scientism
chapters.description: He develops the idea that re-enchantment must include depth and dimensionality, while also naming scientism as a cultural habit that treats measurement and physical explanation as the final standard of reality.

chapters.title: Subtlety in perception
chapters.description: Rob turns the article's concern with being affected by places into a meditation instruction, showing how refined attention can notice the energetic, emotional, and psychic effects of materials, landscapes, and environments.

chapters.title: Kinds of enchantment
chapters.description: He distinguishes immature, cultural, spiritual, and imaginal forms of enchantment, describing how each narrows or opens the self in different ways.

chapters.title: Imaginal and soul
chapters.description: The talk deepens into mature enchantment: non-flat perception, divinity, image as image, and the difference between a universal spiritual opening and a more particular soul-making orientation.

chapters.title: Poetic language
chapters.description: He returns to the basic qualities of the path he is describing—non-flattening, divinity, and poetic use of language—while stressing that these are not beliefs or dogmas but ways of encouraging transformation.

chapters.title: Open frameworks
chapters.description: Rob closes by describing concepts as elastic, experience-linked, and not truth-claims in a rigid sense, then uses Kabbalah's messianic eon to suggest that spiritual life opens into many possible perceptions of God.

title: An Ecology of Love (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) A talk about love, eros, metta, and the Dharma; about our sense of the Earth, and a sense of the sacred.
podcast_description: What if activism is not only about getting results, but also about preserving the heart that can still respond? The talk moves from self-view and noble action to a critique of how Dharma, science, and modern culture can narrow what we count as real, then opens into a wider view of climate engagement shaped by eros, metta, emptiness, and sacredness. Along the way it uses Wendell Berry, Confucius, and Francis Bacon to ask whether practical effectiveness is enough, or whether the soul and the earth both need a richer way of seeing.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on love, activism, and climate crisis that argues for opening perception beyond narrow self-views, instrumental thinking, and one-dimensional Dharma.

chapters.title: Eros and self-view
chapters.description: Burbea begins by describing eros as involving psyche and logos, and shows how any view of self and other already shapes love and action.

chapters.title: Berry and Confucius
chapters.description: He turns to Wendell Berry and Confucius to frame activism as preserving qualities of heart and remaining true to conscience.

chapters.title: Images of nobility
