By the end, the speaker suggests that meaningfulness and love are already saturated with image and fantasy, and that exploring that can open something practical rather than merely theoretical.
short_summary: A careful case for imaginal practice, moving from fears about literalism to a Buddhist view of fabrication, emptiness, and mythos in practice.

chapters.title: Setting Up the Question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the theme, names the discomfort some people feel, and begins by describing imaginal practice as something that often arises in a sensitive one-to-one setting.

chapters.title: Healthy and Unhealthy Imagining
chapters.description: He addresses fears about madness by contrasting nuanced imaginal relationship with literal hallucination, drawing on Mary Watkins and Percival's Narrative.

chapters.title: Metaphor, Canon, and Presence
chapters.description: He uses examples of holy war, death contemplation, and recollecting the Buddha to argue that imagination is already present in Buddhist practice and that the issue is often literalism.

chapters.title: Reality and Assumptions
chapters.description: Burbea distinguishes objective and experiential reality, then breaks down the hidden metaphysical assumptions carried by ontology, epistemology, and cosmology.

chapters.title: Modernism and Matter
chapters.description: He critiques the modern assumption that matter is the only reality and uses physics to suggest that even science does not support such a simple picture.

chapters.title: Reading the Pali Canon
chapters.description: The talk turns to the Buddha's path as a transcendent one that fabricates less, with mindfulness as support rather than the final aim and the deathless as the end point.

chapters.title: Three Paths of Practice
chapters.description: He lays out three possibilities: bare mindfulness, stronger practices that reduce fabrication further, and practices that openly honor image, fantasy, and mythos.

chapters.title: Mythos, Love, and Meaning
chapters.description: Burbea concludes by showing how emptiness can open imaginal practice, how mythos is already part of religious life, and how love and meaningfulness often depend on imaginative amplitude.

title: Guided Meditation 1
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: The practice begins with posture and a quiet body scan, softening tension in the face, throat, shoulders, belly, and hands before widening attention to the whole seated field. From there, the talk keeps returning to a simple movement: open the awareness again and again, feel the body as an energy space, and notice what helps that space feel bright, relaxed, and alive. It then introduces optional images—lines of energy through the center and down the legs, the breath as a gentle expansion and contraction, and even the body as translucent light—while reminding listeners to keep only what feels useful.
short_summary: A sparse guided meditation that moves from settling posture to widening awareness, then experimenting with energy lines, breath, and a luminous body of light.

chapters.title: Settling the posture
chapters.description: The meditation begins by establishing an upright but relaxed posture and sensing a balance of wakefulness and softness.

chapters.title: Releasing tension
chapters.description: Attention moves through the face, throat, neck, shoulders, back, chest, and belly, encouraging gentle relaxation and release.

chapters.title: Grounding in contact
chapters.description: The practice widens to simple sensations in the hands, backside, legs, and feet, grounding awareness in immediate bodily contact.

chapters.title: Opening the field
chapters.description: Awareness is invited to expand beyond the body into a larger space filled with bright, sensitive presence.

chapters.title: Restoring spacious presence
chapters.description: The talk emphasizes repeatedly re-expanding attention when it shrinks, using a wind-and-sail image to support an open, filled space.

chapters.title: Imagining energy lines
chapters.description: A central line of energy is introduced, then extended into additional lines down the legs, with an emphasis on sensing what feels supportive.

chapters.title: Breath and support
chapters.description: The meditation offers the breath as an optional support for the whole-body field, including gentle expansion on the in-breath and relaxation on the out-breath.

chapters.title: Experimenting with form
chapters.description: Listeners are encouraged to try different combinations of breath, energy lines, and imagination, always returning to the felt sense of the whole space.

chapters.title: Body of light
chapters.description: The practice deepens into a radiant or translucent body of light, with color and luminosity left open to the listener's own response.

title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part One)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What does it mean when an inner image is not just a passing picture, but a living field of love, fear, memory, and meaning? The talk moves through a series of examples — a thicket and blue bird, a black devil figure, a train ride, a phoenix, a monastery in Thailand, Gaia House itself — to show how image, fantasy, and mythos can shape both practice and perception. From there, it turns toward the danger of flattening these experiences into fixed symbols, and toward the question of how Dharma practice might meet this subtler, more open-ended territory.
short_summary: A wide-ranging first talk on image, fantasy, and mythos, using vivid examples to explore how imaginal experience can carry love, soulfulness, and practice-relevance without being reduced to fixed meaning.

chapters.title: Opening the territory
chapters.description: He introduces image, fantasy, and mythos as a broad field of practice and explains that he will use examples rather than strict definitions.

chapters.title: Blue bird and thicket
chapters.description: A personal meditation image of a thicket and blue bird shows how imaginal material can be subtle, moving, and not reducible to interpretation.

chapters.title: Devil, train, and change
chapters.description: A frightening recurring figure becomes an ally over years, followed by an ordinary train image that reveals fatigue, restlessness, and underlying peace.

chapters.title: Embodied tradition
chapters.description: He uses music and the feel of the instrument to show how image can be tactile, kinetic, and infused by lineage.

chapters.title: Phoenix and healing
chapters.description: A phoenix image is linked to shame around an old relationship and shows how an imaginal figure can reframe memory with love.

chapters.title: Fantasy in life and place
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion to shared fantasy in relationships, then to Gaia House and a Thai monastery as places made vivid through love and narrative.

chapters.title: Visionary seeing
chapters.description: William Blake and tantric visualization are used to show imagination as a deliberate way of perceiving the world.

chapters.title: Condensed meaning
chapters.description: Drawing on Ezra Pound and John Keats, he describes image as compressed emotional-intellectual complexity and soul-making through relationship.

chapters.title: Literalism and reality
chapters.description: He warns against literalizing archetypal images, argues that images are not flat symbols, and distinguishes individual truth from public certainty.

title: In Praise of Restlessness
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This talk is the third part of a talk in three parts. (Part 1 is "Questioning Awakening"; Part 2 is "Buddhism Beyond Modernism".)
podcast_description: What if the problem is not restlessness itself, but the way we keep confusing agitation, inquiry, and a deeper kind of fire? The talk moves from the hidden metaphysics and myths inside any Dharma view into a set of contrasting images: the seeker, the fugitive, the settled sage, and the Hermes-like wanderer who does not want a final theory. From there it opens into a wide argument that desire, beauty, and even spiritual longing may be part of the path, not obstacles to it, and that practice may be less about reaching a fixed end than about staying with an open-ended work.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk that rethinks restlessness as a possible engine of inquiry, desire, and an open-ended Dharma shaped by myth, beauty, and practice.

chapters.title: Setting the frame
chapters.description: Rob briefly recaps the first two talks and introduces the central claim that Dharma always carries hidden assumptions about what is real and how it is known.

chapters.title: Metaphysics and myth
chapters.description: He unpacks how metaphysics and mythos quietly shape practice, then asks what happens when those hidden supports are brought to the foreground.

chapters.title: Homelessness and inquiry
chapters.description: The talk turns to images of homelessness, seekerhood, and fugitive running, before distinguishing agitation from the more fruitful restlessness of inquiry.

chapters.title: Creative unrest
chapters.description: Using Critchley, Picasso, and alchemy, he argues that some disturbance is part of real work and that the path may need heat rather than premature settling.

chapters.title: Desire on the path
chapters.description: He explores desire as a legitimate force in science, religion, and practice, drawing on Jung, the Buddha, the Dalai Lama, St. John of the Cross, and others.

chapters.title: Pothos and endless work
chapters.description: The talk widens into longing, beauty, and the sense that practice can be driven by an unending task rather than a final endpoint.

chapters.title: Beauty and enchantment
chapters.description: He suggests that people often practice not only to reduce suffering but for wonder, beauty, and a more enchanted relation to the world.

chapters.title: Dharma as art
chapters.description: The closing section develops the strongest form of the argument: Dharma as art, with poetic truth, multiple readings, and a liberating openness that cannot be boxed in.

title: Buddhism Beyond Modernism
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This talk is the second part of a talk in three parts. (Part 1 is "Questioning Awakening"; Part 3 is "In Praise of Restlessness".)
podcast_description: What if the real issue on the path is not how well we attend, but what hidden ideas, myths, and metaphysical assumptions are already shaping what we notice? The talk moves from the basics of inquiry — noticing, interpreting, questioning — into a broad critique of modernist Dharma, using Einstein, Kuhn, Heisenberg, and then Kabbalah, philosophy, and Buddhist history to show how frameworks both help and limit what becomes visible. It keeps returning to one difficult claim: that liberation, too, is imagined through a worldview, and that even the urge for “bare attention” may be carrying its own unseen assumptions.
short_summary: A wide-ranging critique of modernist Dharma that asks how ideas, myths, and metaphysics quietly shape inquiry, practice, and even the meaning of liberation.

chapters.title: Setting up the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea explains that this talk will not follow a linear teaching style, but will instead open up the assumptions beneath Dharma practice and awakening.

chapters.title: Ideas and their power
chapters.description: He defines ideas as the lenses shaping perception, and shows how concepts, fantasies, and mythos can open life or close it down.

chapters.title: Freedom to inquire
chapters.description: Using Einstein and the Gaia House sign, he argues that real inquiry needs freedom, and that inquiry must include what feels taboo or hard to question.

chapters.title: Kuhn and the Dharma
chapters.description: Burbea uses Thomas Kuhn's account of paradigms and revolutions to ask whether Dharma itself can become trapped in conceptual boxes.

chapters.title: Modernism examined
chapters.description: He turns to Richard Tarnas and sketches modernism as a worldview shaped by materialism, objectivity, and a narrow sense of what counts as real.

chapters.title: Breaking the vessels
chapters.description: With Heisenberg, Einstein, and Lurianic Kabbalah, he explores how crises shatter inherited structures and make room for new ways of holding experience.

chapters.title: Styles of inquiry
chapters.description: He contrasts Apollonian, Dionysian, and Hermes-like modes of inquiry, while questioning the modern preference for bare attention and closed systems.

chapters.title: Multiple worlds of meaning
chapters.description: Burbea widens the frame to show how different metaphysics, stories, and images generate different worlds, and why no single discourse can claim final authority.

chapters.title: Liberation and fantasy
chapters.description: He closes by arguing that liberation, awakening, and the Buddha are always entangled with fantasy, interpretation, and cosmology, and that recognizing this is part of honest practice.

title: Questioning Awakening
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This talk is the first part of a talk in three parts. (Part 2 is "Buddhism Beyond Modernism"; Part 3 is "In Praise of Restlessness".)
podcast_description: What if the real question is not how to practice, but what we already assume practice, liberation, and reality are? The talk starts by questioning the modern habit of treating mindfulness as a coping strategy or awakening as a softened, psychological ideal, then opens into a wider inquiry about the hidden views underneath Dharma language, the Four Noble Truths, and the meaning of freedom. From there it moves through comparisons with the Pali Canon, modern culture, psychology, philosophy, and physics to ask whether our sense of what is real has been too quickly accepted as common sense.
short_summary: A sustained inquiry into the hidden assumptions behind mindfulness, awakening, and reality itself.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea frames the talk as the first of three and explains that he wants to question assumptions beneath familiar Dharma teaching rather than offer practical tips.

chapters.title: Many versions of awakening
chapters.description: He surveys the range of views on awakening, from its disappearance to its dilution or rigid fixation, and asks what is driving those shifts.

chapters.title: Four truths, many goals
chapters.description: Using the Four Noble Truths, he shows how different traditions and modern readers can mean very different things by the path, dukkha, and liberation.

chapters.title: What do we want?
chapters.description: He turns to the aims of practice—presence, simplicity, reducing suffering, and different styles of striving—and asks whether these are enough or even coherent.

chapters.title: Presence and the now
chapters.description: Burbea questions the popular ideal of savoring the moment and living life to the full, arguing that it carries hidden assumptions about reality and significance.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and reality
chapters.description: He examines mindfulness as a coping strategy and contrasts taking meditation states as final truths with investigating how perceptions arise and cease.

chapters.title: Ontology behind practice
chapters.description: The talk broadens into philosophy, with ontology, epistemology, and cosmology presented as unavoidable background assumptions in any practice path.

chapters.title: Modern materialist models
chapters.description: He critiques the machine model of the universe and mind, drawing on Heidegger, Pascal, and Heisenberg to show how modern assumptions shape Dharma.

chapters.title: Questioning hidden views
chapters.description: Burbea closes by stressing that imagination, therapy narratives, and even claims of being non-conceptual all rest on views that should themselves be questioned.

title: Creative Samadhi
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if samadhi is not just concentration on a point, but a way of working that includes the whole body, a steadier energy field, and a deeper openness of heart? The talk starts by questioning a narrow idea of practice, then builds a broader map: the path aims beyond mere presence, and it has two wings, insight and cultivation. From there, it moves into practical advice on deepening samadhi—relaxing effort, valuing quality over quantity, opening the whole body in awareness, and noticing how kindness, gratitude, and letting go support the mind’s settling.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on samadhi that redefines practice as embodied, artful, and supported by insight, cultivation, and an open heart.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces samadhi, asks what the path is really for, and invites listeners to question inherited assumptions about practice.

chapters.title: The path goes somewhere
chapters.description: He critiques a thin reading of 'just be present' and reframes the path as aiming at the undoing of fundamental delusion.

chapters.title: Two wings of practice
chapters.description: The talk turns to the paired roles of insight and cultivation, and to wholesome qualities like generosity, kindness, and mindfulness as sources of nourishment.

chapters.title: Samadhi as wholeness
chapters.description: Samadhi is described as a spectrum of deepening consciousness that involves the whole body, unification, and a felt sense of well-being.

chapters.title: Why focus is not enough
chapters.description: He lays out common one-pointed methods, then shows how mindfulness, patience, kindness, and the wider field of practice matter more than mere steadiness.

chapters.title: Ways to deepen
chapters.description: Burbea offers practical refinements: widen the conception of practice, value quality over quantity, allow subtlety, and include the whole body as a background field.

chapters.title: Whole-body experiment
chapters.description: He leads a guided inquiry into relaxing, opening awareness, sensing energy, and using breath or imagined channels to feel the body as a living field.

chapters.title: Space, letting go, and looking
chapters.description: The instructions widen into open awareness, where phenomena arise and pass while attention steadies on space, relationship, and release.

chapters.title: Open heart, open practice
chapters.description: In the closing movement, he questions the ideal of no effort, reflects on doing and desire in life, and returns to the importance of nourishing the heart through gratitude, nature, beauty, and community.

title: Ayya Santacitta, Ayya Santussika, David Loy, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 5
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with David Loy, Ayya Santussika Bhikkhuni and Ayya Santacitta Bhikkhuni. Unfortunately, Ven. Bhikkhu Bodhi was ill on the day of our call and was not able to join us. We wish him a speedy and thorough recovery. In this call, Ayya Santussika Bhikkhuni mentioned two resources: the international, interfaith effort Our Voices as well as the “Pledge to Mobilize” at the Climate Mobilization. She also invited those interested in taking action together as Buddhists on the national or local/regional level to email her at santussika at gmail.com. Finally, Dave Damm-Luhr mentioned the upcoming film from the same group that brought us “The Overview Effect.” This new film, “Planetary” (see the trailer here) maybe well-timed for action around the March deadline for targets into the UN climate talks that Lou mentioned.
podcast_description: How do inner practice and public action meet when the climate crisis can no longer be treated as a distant concern? The talk begins by naming the tension between hopelessness and responsibility, then moves from a reexamination of Buddhist practice to concrete organizing: the bodhisattva path, local and national Buddhist networks, and faith-based efforts like People's Climate March and Our Voices. It closes by stressing that awakening has to become both steadiness and courage—an inward groundedness that can support outward pressure, coalition-building, and voting.
short_summary: A discussion of climate action through Buddhist practice that moves from the bodhisattva path to concrete organizing, coalition-building, and political engagement.

chapters.title: Opening the final call
chapters.description: Thanissara opens the last session of the series, notes Bhikkhu Bodhi’s absence, and names the talk’s focus on turning reflection into action.

chapters.title: What can we actually do?
chapters.description: Lu introduces the central question and points to hope through existing signs of change, especially renewable energy and political engagement.

chapters.title: Reexamining Buddhist practice
chapters.description: David Loy argues that Buddhism needs to clarify what awakening means in a climate crisis, challenging both transcendence-as-escape and purely psychological inwardness.

chapters.title: The bodhisattva path
chapters.description: Loy develops the bodhisattva path as a double practice of meditation and engagement, linking wisdom, compassion, and the willingness to act without certainty of results.

chapters.title: Building a Buddhist movement
chapters.description: Ayya Santussika describes how Buddhists can organize locally and nationally, drawing on the People's Climate March, faith coalitions, divestment, and voter engagement.

chapters.title: Precepts, urgency, and earth
chapters.description: The discussion turns to ethical responsibility, the first two precepts, climate deadlines, fossil-fuel dependence, and the Buddha touching the earth as a model for fearless speech and action.

chapters.title: Questions from the sangha
chapters.description: Listeners respond with gratitude, questions about resources, and stories of being moved into new forms of leadership, including local teaching and voting.

chapters.title: Joy, continuity, blessing
chapters.description: The closing section returns to joy as a condition for sustained action, outlines plans for monthly follow-up calls, and ends with a Buddhist blessing.

title: Bonnie Duran, Chas DiCapua, Thanissara, Vinny Ferraro: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 4
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with Bonnie Duran, Chas DiCapua and Vinny Ferraro.
podcast_description: How do climate action, Dharma practice, and social justice meet when the deeper problem is disconnection? The talk begins with a climate-and-mindfulness conversation that moves from plant-based ethics and indigenous views of colonization into a sustained exchange about patriarchy, the overvaluing of intellect, and the need to come back into the body. Later, it shifts into practice questions about despair, effectiveness, and what it means to act from integrity rather than outcomes, with readings from Joanna Macy, a Jataka tale, and a closing dedication of merit.
short_summary: A climate and Dharma conversation that moves from colonization and patriarchy to embodied practice, interdependence, and action rooted in integrity.

chapters.title: Opening and Introductions
chapters.description: Thanissara welcomes the teachers and outlines the conversation on mindfulness, Dharma, and climate action.

chapters.title: Plant-Based Ethics
chapters.description: The opening teaching argues for reducing animal products by linking factory farming to emissions, land use, water use, and compassion.

chapters.title: Colonization and Root Poisons
chapters.description: Bonnie Duran relates climate change to colonization, greed, hatred, and delusion, and introduces Indigenous ecological knowledge as part of the response.

chapters.title: Rights of Nature
chapters.description: The teaching moves through Pachamama, Bolivia and Ecuador, and a quote from John Mohawk to emphasize humility and the intelligence of the larger whole.

chapters.title: Technology and Patriarchy
chapters.description: Chas DiCapua expands the critique into patriarchy, overvaluing intellect and science, and the devaluing of body, feminine energy, and nature.

chapters.title: Returning to the Body
chapters.description: He brings the talk back to embodied practice, the Satipatthana Sutta, and the idea that connection with the body is the starting point for wise action.

chapters.title: Belonging and Grief
chapters.description: Vinny Ferraro speaks about interdependence, the pain of the world, and how despair can become connection rather than paralysis.

chapters.title: Listening and Action
chapters.description: The teaching becomes more explicitly guided as Vinny leads inward listening, quotes John Seed, Buddhadasa, and Rumi, and frames action as participation in a larger life.

chapters.title: Questions and Dedication
chapters.description: The group responds with questions about discouragement, integrity, and livelihood, then closes with a broad merit dedication and final housekeeping.

title: Catherine McGee, Chris Cullen, James Baraz, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 3
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with James Baraz, Catherine McGee and Chris Cullen.
podcast_description: What if climate action begins not with urgency alone, but with the way practice lets us meet fear, grief, and responsibility without hardening? The talk moves from an opening reminder of the global climate series into three linked reflections: one speaker explores the meeting place of the silence of practice and the fire of activism, another frames the climate crisis as a problem of reconnection using the fourfold spiral from Active Hope, and a third turns to the need for inspiring vision and a willingness to feel the pain of the crisis clearly. Along the way, the talk uses the Eightfold Path, the Buddha’s response to awakening, Bill McKibben’s Earth, and concrete questions about daily choices and political momentum to bring contemplation and action closer together.
short_summary: A three-part climate and Dharma conversation on how to stay open to grief, reconnect with the world, and move into wise action.

chapters.title: Opening and Welcome
chapters.description: Thanissara welcomes listeners, introduces the series, and frames the conversation as an invitation to engage climate change in community.

chapters.title: Why This Matters
chapters.description: Lou Leonard outlines the climate context and the need for both systemic and individual responses, then hands to Catherine McGee.

chapters.title: Action and View
chapters.description: Catherine begins by asking where the silence of practice meets the passion of activism, and grounds action in intention, view, and an open system of relationship.

chapters.title: Bone-Jangling Reality
chapters.description: She shares a story of bringing climate data to an old Dharma teacher, using his shaken response to show how practice can hold painful truth without shutting down.

chapters.title: Silence and Fire
chapters.description: Catherine reflects on figures like Nelson Mandela and the possibility of combining depth, steadiness, and justice-oriented energy in action.

chapters.title: Reconnecting With the Earth
chapters.description: Chris takes up Joanna Macy and Chris Johnston's Active Hope as a four-part spiral: gratitude, honoring pain, seeing with new eyes, and going forth.

chapters.title: From Practice to Action
chapters.description: He turns to concrete steps, from home energy use and travel to public conversation and divestment, while stressing that action and contemplation support each other.

chapters.title: James on Inspiration
chapters.description: James speaks about holding an inspiring vision, how Bill McKibben's Earth changed his relationship to climate grief, and why climate work can be treated as Dharma practice.

chapters.title: Questions and Blessing
chapters.description: The discussion continues with audience questions about leverage and institutions, then closes with gratitude, a short announcement, and Catherine's blessing for all beings.

title: Ayya Anandabodhi, Rev. angel Kyodo williams, Susie Harrington, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 2
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with Rev. angel Kyodo williams, Ayya Anandabodhi Bhikkhuni and Susie Harrington. In this conversation, Susie Harrington mentioned two sources: Kerry Nelson’s offering of online workshops at A Place for Peace as well as Vinit Allen’s Sustainable World Sourcebook. Both offer ways to engage individually but also at the community level.Rev angel Kyodo williams mentioned the site What Is Missing? from Maya Lin offering an interactive experience of all that has been and continues to be lost to which you can add your own accounting.
podcast_description: What does practice ask of us when the climate crisis is no longer abstract, but a room that is already burning? This conversation moves from a grounding breath into a frank exchange about climate action, social justice, and the Buddhist roots of greed, hatred, and delusion in ecological harm. The speakers keep returning to the same pressure point: overwhelm, grief, and fear can either shut us down or be turned into clarity, compassion, and concrete action. Along the way, they move between personal responsibility, systemic critique, and practical ways to join others without retreating into isolation.
short_summary: A grounded conversation on climate action that links Buddhist practice with justice, grief, and the courage to respond.

chapters.title: Opening the conversation
chapters.description: Thanissara introduces the panel and frames climate change as a Dharma and justice issue, with a brief grounding breath for everyone joining the call.

chapters.title: Climate as practice
chapters.description: Ayya Anandabodhi speaks about climate change as an ethical and spiritual matter, naming fear, overwhelm, and the need for right action.

chapters.title: Interbeing and the poisons
chapters.description: Ayya develops the teaching of interconnection and links ecological crisis to greed, hatred, and delusion, urging practitioners not to hide behind impermanence.

chapters.title: Justice and systemic harm
chapters.description: Reverend Angel Kyodo Williams expands the lens to colonialism, hyper-capitalism, racism, and the need for sangha to become a deeper form of collective responsibility.

chapters.title: Not looking away
chapters.description: Susie Harrington reflects on truth, grief, extraction, and the courage to face what is happening without turning away, including examples from the Alberta tar sands.

chapters.title: From overwhelm to action
chapters.description: The teachers explore overwhelm as a form of collapse, then point toward compassion, nature, local action, and community resources as ways through it.

chapters.title: Questions and next steps
chapters.description: The discussion turns to breakout groups, an upcoming training, and questions about how to speak about climate change in resistant settings.

chapters.title: Facing resistance
chapters.description: Ayya and Reverend Angel answer questions about engaging people non-threateningly, finding the right community, and speaking from a truthful place.

chapters.title: Closing blessings
chapters.description: The call ends with announcements, final encouragement, and a blessing that returns attention to the body, the earth, and shared good intention.

title: The Four Noble Truths of Responding to Climate Change
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Continuing our exploration of ethical practice during earth care week. We examine, through the frame work of the Four Noble Truths, the question of the nature of climate change and how to respond as individuals, as communities, nationally and internationally.
podcast_description: What does it mean to face climate change as a practice of non-harming, rather than as a problem kept at a distance? The talk moves through the Four Noble Truths: first naming the scale of the suffering and the science of warming, then tracing its causes in fossil fuels, consumer culture, denial, and inequity. From there it turns toward possibility—shared distress, resilience, local and political action, and the idea that a committed minority can help shift what seems fixed.
short_summary: A Dharma talk that uses the Four Noble Truths to move from the realities of climate change to the inner and collective practices needed to respond.

chapters.title: Ethics and Integrity
chapters.description: Rothberg opens by situating climate work inside ethical practice, integrity, and the three trainings of Buddhism.

chapters.title: Non-harming and Earth Care
chapters.description: He widens the first precept through Thich Nhat Hanh and an Earth Care Week statement to frame ecological harm as a moral concern.

chapters.title: Four Noble Truths Framework
chapters.description: He lays out the Four Noble Truths as the structure for the talk and thanks several teachers who shaped the presentation.

chapters.title: The Bad News
chapters.description: He names climate change as a grave collective suffering, reviews basic climate science, and describes the scale of likely impacts and injustice.

chapters.title: Meeting Distress
chapters.description: After pausing with the weight of the topic, he turns to practices for facing fear, grief, numbness, and shock together.

chapters.title: Roots of the Crisis
chapters.description: He outlines outer and inner causes, including fossil fuels, consumer culture, privilege, fear, distraction, and denial.

chapters.title: Possibility of Transformation
chapters.description: He shifts to the third noble truth, arguing that a sustainable and more just world is possible and pointing to rapid changes already underway.

chapters.title: The Path of Response
chapters.description: He describes personal, community, and national responses, including meditation, ethical living, local organizing, divestment, and broad mobilization.

chapters.title: Questions and Closing
chapters.description: In discussion he returns to compassion, polarization, and staying connected, then closes with dedications and a reminder of shared interdependence.

title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if the images and fantasies that arise in meditation are not distractions to ignore, but living presences that shape the whole direction of practice? The talk moves from common Dharma habits of noticing and letting go, through psychological interpretations like childhood or compensation, toward a more radical possibility: to experiment with images as if they carry their own demand, their own beauty, and their own power. Along the way it draws on poems, myths, and Buddhist examples to ask whether the real issue is not the image itself, but the way we conceive it.
short_summary: A talk about rethinking images and fantasies in practice: not as distractions to dismiss, but as powerful, sometimes demanding presences that can open a deeper freedom.

chapters.title: Setting the frame
chapters.description: Rob introduces the theme of image and fantasy, and explains that the talk will extend the earlier teaching.

chapters.title: Common responses
chapters.description: He surveys the usual Dharma attitudes toward images: ignore them, notice and release them, or use them to soften judgment.

chapters.title: Psychological readings
chapters.description: He turns to psychotherapeutic and integrative ways of understanding images as past material, compensation, or parts of a self to be balanced.

chapters.title: Conceiving and experimenting
chapters.description: He shifts from true-versus-false to what different ways of looking lead to, emphasizing mindfulness, bodily resonance, and meaningfulness.

chapters.title: Image as living presence
chapters.description: He asks what changes when images are not judged by their content and when their relation to the body reveals whether they are fruitful.

chapters.title: From emptiness to freedom
chapters.description: The Buddha's teaching on emptiness, together with Eliot and Rilke, frames an experiment in letting images carry more power than the ego usually allows.

chapters.title: Daemons and angels
chapters.description: He develops the idea that images may not come from personal history at all, drawing on Corbin and Hillman to speak of service to psychic persons or daemons.

chapters.title: Risk, myth, and ethics
chapters.description: He addresses the danger of this approach, distinguishes mythic truth from literal belief, and asks how to tell ego-proliferation from something deeper.

chapters.title: What is not just ego
chapters.description: Through examples from Hendrix, Senna, Buddhist polemics, the Buddha's own words, and the myth of Horus, he questions how quickly we dismiss grandeur, desire, and force as mere ego.

title: Jack Kornfield, Ruth King, Tara Brach, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 1
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with Tara Brach, Ruth King and Jack Kornfield.
podcast_description: How do you stay open to the scale of climate grief without collapsing into fear, guilt, or numbness? This conversation begins with a short grounding practice and then moves into a clear-eyed look at the climate crisis as both ecological damage and a symptom of separation, with references to the People’s Climate March, Joanna Macy, Rumi, and a few stories that point back to presence. From there the talk widens into questions of systemic injustice, collective action, and how practice can support sustained response rather than righteous burnout.
short_summary: A grounded climate-action conversation that links mindfulness, systemic justice, and bodhisattva-style collective response.

chapters.title: Opening and grounding
chapters.description: Thanissara welcomes listeners, frames the conversation, and invites a brief settling into body and breath.

chapters.title: Climate as shared condition
chapters.description: Lou Leonard outlines the climate picture, the scale of the challenge, and the importance of sangha and shared attention.

chapters.title: Presence and belonging
chapters.description: Tara Brach speaks about separation, fear, the unreal other, and the need to meet the earth with love and attention.

chapters.title: Systems and justice
chapters.description: Ruth King links climate harm to greed, racism, and structural oppression, and names mutuality as the basis for action.

chapters.title: Dukkha and bodhisattva path
chapters.description: Jack Kornfield grounds the talk in the Buddha’s first noble truth and then shifts into bodhisattva vows and expansive responsibility.

chapters.title: Act from love
chapters.description: Jack draws on Merton, the Bhagavad Gita, Gary Snyder, and Maha Gosananda to stress non-attachment, love, and step-by-step compassion.

chapters.title: Questions on practice
chapters.description: The teachers respond to questions about anger, activism, and how inner practice and outer action can support each other.

chapters.title: Organizing together
chapters.description: Participants ask about systemic politics, good news, and practical networks, and the teachers name concrete movements and shared responsibility.

chapters.title: Closing blessings
chapters.description: The talk ends with a short blessing for Earth, a reminder to keep gathering, and an invitation to continue the series and the work.

title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What happens when an inner critic is met not as a solid enemy, but as one figure in a wider inner theater? The talk moves through retreat stories of comforting, devouring, erotic, and warrior-like images to show how engaging them with mindfulness and without literalizing can loosen the sense of a fixed self. It then widens further, suggesting that figures from art, myth, and public life can live in the psyche as real sources of resonance, challenge, and nourishment.
short_summary: A talk on meeting inner figures as images rather than literal truths, and using that relationship to soften self-clinging and open new energies.

chapters.title: Picking Up the Thread
chapters.description: He situates the talk as a continuation and says he will return to the material in stages through the day.

chapters.title: Inner Critic, Inner Persons
chapters.description: Through a retreat example, he shows how the inner critic can open into a dialogue with multiple figures rather than a single fixed voice.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and Image
chapters.description: He distinguishes mindfulness with images from literalizing them, and explains how this both protects and vivifies the practice.

chapters.title: Loosening a Solid Self
chapters.description: He places image-work alongside emptiness and mystical dissolution as another way of softening belief in a singular self.

chapters.title: Images as Resources
chapters.description: A series of retreat stories shows images as sources of care, nourishment, power, and soul-level opening, including the grandpa, goddess, dragon, soldier, and mountain lion.

chapters.title: Figures in Culture
chapters.description: He turns to public figures and traditions, including the Thai forest Ajahns, Mandela, Einstein, Bohr, Hendrix, and Coltrane, to show how people can live in the psyche as images.

chapters.title: Wildness and Shame
chapters.description: He considers images that do not fit Buddhist ideals, including Dionysian energy and a story of sexual shame reframed as sacred rather than merely fallen.

chapters.title: Working Deliberately
chapters.description: He offers practical ways to engage images in meditation and notes that upset and samadhi can both make image-work more available.

title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if the self is not something to erase, but something to relate to more wisely? The talk begins by questioning a familiar Buddhist simplification about self and aggregates, then argues for a more flexible approach that can make room for complexity, eros, art, and the patterns of modern personality. From there it turns to the inner critic as a concrete example, showing how loving-kindness, deconstruction, or even dialogue can change its force—and sometimes reveal a hidden protector or a grief-struck image of the mother beneath it.
short_summary: A talk on moving beyond rigid ideas of self to meet the inner critic, grief, and personality with more flexible and transformative ways of looking.

chapters.title: Questioning the usual view
chapters.description: Burbea begins by naming the common teaching that ego is the problem and self is illusory, then starts to test that framing.

chapters.title: Limits of aggregates
chapters.description: He argues that reducing self to a process of aggregates is not something the Buddha said and can flatten deeper understanding.

chapters.title: A skillful way of looking
chapters.description: The aggregates and emptiness are presented as perspectives that can be used meditatively, not final descriptions of reality.

chapters.title: Why simplicity fails
chapters.description: He turns to modern complexity, eros, art, and personality, suggesting that simplistic teachings can fail to nourish the richness of life.

chapters.title: Modern suffering and self
chapters.description: Burbea notes that much contemporary suffering sits at the level of personality, and asks for other ways of opening the notion of self.

chapters.title: Meeting the inner critic
chapters.description: He introduces the inner critic as a familiar structure and names loving-kindness and insight into emptiness as two ways of softening it.

chapters.title: From deconstructing to relating
chapters.description: The talk shifts to an imaginal move: treating the critic as a figure, entering dialogue, and allowing relationship to change the experience.

chapters.title: The clumsy protector
chapters.description: Burbea suggests the critic may be a protector in disguise, and tells a retreat story in which turning toward it reveals unexpected kindness.

chapters.title: Grief and the mother image
chapters.description: A second story shows how a dead mother can remain alive as an image in the psyche, and how responding to that image can open a freer and more beautiful relation.

title: Meditation: Opening to Aliveness
speaker: Tara Brach
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC)
podcast_description: Beginning with posture and breath, the meditation settles into a simple inquiry: what most matters right now? The talk then moves through body scanning, softening the face, shoulders, heart, belly, pelvis, legs, and feet, so attention can rest in aliveness rather than in thought. From there, sounds, sensations, and feelings are included as part of the same field, with repeated invitations to notice when the mind has wandered and start fresh. It ends by returning to the sense of being earth, awake and animated in the present moment.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from posture and intention into body awareness, open listening, and returning again to present-moment aliveness.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The meditation opens with posture, balance, and a few deliberate breaths to establish alertness and ease.

chapters.title: Intention and Aum
chapters.description: Attention moves to the heart, where she asks about sincere intention and introduces a brief chanting of Aum.

chapters.title: Softening the Face
chapters.description: The practice turns to the face, jaw, mouth, and shoulders, using relaxation to invite receptivity rather than force.

chapters.title: Heart and Belly
chapters.description: Awareness widens through the chest and belly, with the reminder that smiling into experience is a way of making space for it.

chapters.title: Pelvis and Earth Body
chapters.description: The scan continues through the pelvic region, legs, and feet, opening into the sense of the body as a living field and the earth beneath it.

chapters.title: Sound and Presence
chapters.description: Sound is folded into the practice, and the talk points to an alert stillness in the background of hearing and sensing.

chapters.title: Returning Fresh
chapters.description: She names the ordinary drift into story and offers a simple, nonjudgmental return to the senses and the breath.

chapters.title: Deepening Into Silence
chapters.description: The closing sections revisit relaxing more fully, sinking into the earth-body, and resting in the vast awake silence that remains aware.

venue: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC

title: Earth's Crisis - On the Edge of the Roof - (on climate change)
speaker: Tara Brach
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC) This talk views the ecological dis-ease of our planet through the lens of our evolutionary unfolding. We explore the egoic trance that has precipitated the destruction of our environment, and the inner practices of presence that enable us to respond from love and wisdom.
podcast_description: The talk begins with climate crisis as a waking-up call, then quickly asks why so much suffering can still feel distant. Through Rumi’s warning that we are “at the edge of the roof,” the speaker moves into stories of Parsifal, the “Sutra on the Son’s Flesh,” and contemporary ecology to show how denial, consumption, and spiritual trance keep us from responding. The talk then shifts toward practice: slowing down, feeling grief and fear honestly, remembering our belonging to the web of life, and acting without needing certainty about results.
short_summary: A grounded climate talk that moves from diagnosis of collective denial to the practice of feeling, belonging, and taking the next right step.

chapters.title: Opening the crisis
chapters.description: Brach frames the climate emergency as both a planetary event and a spiritual challenge, linking ecological harm to the habits of indifference and waking up.

chapters.title: Rumi and Parsifal
chapters.description: Rumi's warning about being drunk at the edge of the roof becomes a bridge into the Parsifal legend, where one honest question restores life to a wasteland.

chapters.title: Trance and consumption
chapters.description: She describes the egoic trance as grasping, numbness, and objectifying the world, drawing on the Sutra on the Son's Flesh and the logic of endless consumption.

chapters.title: Separation and entitlement
chapters.description: The talk expands into a developmental view of separation, fear, and entitlement, including cultural patterns of growth, extraction, and a sense that humans stand apart from the earth.

chapters.title: Digital distance
chapters.description: A contemporary texting story leads into a reflection on how cyber-life can flatten depth, embodiment, and compassion, even while increasing information.

chapters.title: Earth as belonging
chapters.description: Through Joanna Macy and Chief Seattle, Brach emphasizes that the earth is not a supply source but a shared body, and that shutting down in fear is a central danger.

chapters.title: Feeling and action
chapters.description: She shifts from diagnosis to practice: deepening attention, feeling what is here, not relying on hope of results, and asking what the earth requires of us now.

chapters.title: Collective awakening
chapters.description: Brach points toward systemic change, community, and teachings from Paul Hawken, Darwin, and Emerson that reframe life as interdependent, urgent, and worthy of wonder.

chapters.title: Listening and prayer
chapters.description: The closing turns into a brief contemplative appeal to hear the animals, plants, and planet itself, and to let prayer become response.

venue: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC

title: Buddhist Perspective On Climate Change
speaker: Sayadaw Vivekananda
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge) Observed changes in the climate system, attribution of climate change, Buddhist perspective, Buddhist response to climate change.
podcast_description: Climate change is first laid out as a concrete, evidence-based problem: warming atmosphere and oceans, shrinking ice, rising seas, and the role of greenhouse gases and human activity. From there, the talk moves into how a Buddhist lens changes the question from abstract blame to ethics, responsibility, and the way greed, consumption, and indifference shape collective harm. It brings in examples from the Northeast, Nepal, and a range of Buddhist texts and stories to widen the moral circle from human beings to animals, ecosystems, and future generations.
short_summary: An evidence-based talk on climate change that turns into a Buddhist inquiry into non-harming, greed, and universal responsibility.

chapters.title: Opening frame
chapters.description: The talk introduces the subject and outlines a path from climate science to Buddhist responsibility.

chapters.title: What climate science shows
chapters.description: He reviews key IPCC findings on warming, oceans, ice loss, sea level rise, and greenhouse gases.

chapters.title: Human influence and local impacts
chapters.description: The talk turns to attribution, the warming since the industrial era, and examples from the northeastern United States.

chapters.title: Nepal and the Himalayas
chapters.description: He describes Nepal’s vulnerability, glacier retreat, black carbon, and downstream risks to water and food security.

chapters.title: Causes of the crisis
chapters.description: Natural causes are mentioned, but the focus settles on industrialization and human-caused greenhouse gas emissions.

chapters.title: Harmony with nature
chapters.description: Buddhist teaching is presented as cooperation rather than domination, using Peter Harvey and D.T. Suzuki as supporting voices.

chapters.title: Non-harming and metta
chapters.description: He expands the first precept, ahimsa, and the brahmaviharas to include animals, ecosystems, and all sentient beings.

chapters.title: Greed and responsibility
chapters.description: The talk connects climate change to consumer greed, the economic system, and practical ways of reducing harm and emissions.

chapters.title: Trees and future generations
chapters.description: A tree-spirit story, texts on planting groves, and a Dalai Lama quotation are used to close on gratitude and universal responsibility.

title: Practice & Climate Change
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center) Knowing your refuge. Cultivating resilience. Alignment with authentic response.
podcast_description: How do we stay clear and responsive when climate change, fear, and instability press in? The talk begins with practice as refuge and preparation, then moves through resilience, ethical restraint, and the difference between awareness itself and the restless activity of mind. Along the way, it uses stories, sayings, and images like a guesthouse, a bridge, and a cobra to show how to meet difficult conditions without collapsing into reactivity. It ends by returning to simplicity, letting go, and the possibility of responding from a steadier heart.
short_summary: A talk on practice as refuge, resilience, and a steadier response to climate change and life’s instability.

chapters.title: Practice as preparation
chapters.description: The talk opens by framing retreat practice as training for the moments when strong experience hits the heart and we must choose between reactivity and wise pause.

chapters.title: Climate change and activation
chapters.description: Thanissara names climate change as a major pressure on the psyche and explains how it can trigger fear, overwhelm, and old patterns of distraction.

chapters.title: A shift in consciousness
chapters.description: She argues, with Einstein and Dharma language, that problems cannot be met from the same separative mindset that helps create them.

chapters.title: Refuge in Buddha Dharma Sangha
chapters.description: The talk unfolds the three refuges as living realities: inspiration, teachings, and the capacity to practice, rather than as mere formulas.

chapters.title: Ethics and conscience
chapters.description: Thanissara turns to the five precepts, conscience, and restraint as a grounded ethical base that protects both oneself and others.

chapters.title: Courage and resilience
chapters.description: Examples from Aung San Suu Kyi and the retreat context lead into a discussion of resilience as the ability to gather, steady, and return.

chapters.title: Knowing awareness directly
chapters.description: Ajahn Tate's distinction between awareness and mental activity becomes a way to practice with breath, recognize the listening heart, and hold grief without being ruled by it.

chapters.title: Simplify and let go
chapters.description: Using Ajahn Lee's bridge analogy and the principle of nekkhamma, she explains how ethics, samadhi, and wisdom support a life that can release grasping and consume less.

chapters.title: The peace that remains
chapters.description: The closing section returns to surrender, the naked moment of awareness, and a peace that can remain present even in unstable times, from which authentic response may emerge.

title: Wise Action for the Earth
speaker: David Loy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: The talk asks why the Buddha still matters in the face of ecological crisis, and answers by tracing the same basic pattern from personal suffering to collective climate breakdown: a mistaken sense of separation. From there it turns toward practice, arguing that inner work, wisdom, and compassion have to be joined to outer response if action is not to collapse into burnout or abstraction. It closes with images of awakening into intimacy with the whole earth, through Rumi, Shantideva, and a call for a new bodhisattva-like way of living.
short_summary: A reflection on ecological crisis as a spiritual crisis, linking Buddhist insight into dukkha and selfhood with collective action for the earth.

chapters.title: Buddhism and crisis
chapters.description: David opens by asking why the Buddha still matters for understanding the ecological crisis and proposes a parallel between personal dukkha and our collective situation.

chapters.title: Separate self, shared suffering
chapters.description: He develops the idea that the normal sense of a separate self is insecure, internally ungrounded, and tied to the feeling that something is missing.

chapters.title: Forgetting the self
chapters.description: Using Dogen and Nisargadatta, he describes awakening as moving beyond self-concern into intimacy, wisdom, and compassion.

chapters.title: From awakening to action
chapters.description: The talk turns toward bodhisattva responsibility, the earth as our larger body, and Rumi’s image of the worm waking into the vineyard.

chapters.title: A collective predicament
chapters.description: A Shantideva passage and Bill McKibben’s climate argument are used to show that the crisis is systemic and cannot be solved by individual effort alone.

chapters.title: Three kinds of transformation
chapters.description: Drawing on Joanna Macy, he outlines holding actions, building alternatives, and shifting consciousness, then closes with Howard Thurman on vocation and aliveness.

chapters.title: Questions and closing images
chapters.description: In the Q&A, he reflects on language, resilience, contagion, and a Spanish song about singing together so they cannot stop us.

title: Right Action on Climate
speaker: Ayya Anandabodhi
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: How do emptiness and impermanence become a reason to act, not a reason to look away? The talk begins with the image of the earth as sacred and moves through a sutta story of the Buddha bathing an abandoned sick monk, using it to argue that clear seeing should deepen care. From there it turns toward climate collapse, climate denial, and the emotional weight of knowing too much, then steadily into concrete responses: speaking truthfully, joining others, changing money and diet, and making commitments that can actually be kept.
short_summary: A climate talk that links Buddhist emptiness, grief, and ethical responsibility to practical action, from personal choices to collective organizing.

chapters.title: Earth as sacred
chapters.description: She opens with a felt sense of the living earth, the Welsh landscape, and the possibility of remembering sacred relationship with land.

chapters.title: Why Buddhists hesitate
chapters.description: She names the Buddhist tendency toward apathy or withdrawal around climate change and questions the idea that impermanence justifies inaction.

chapters.title: The sick monk
chapters.description: She recounts the Buddha bathing an abandoned sick monk as a model for insight joined with care and responsibility.

chapters.title: Climate as ethics
chapters.description: She parallels the monk’s abandonment with humanity’s treatment of the planet and asks whether practice can include turning toward harm rather than avoiding it.

chapters.title: Pressure and renewal
chapters.description: She places the crisis within a wider Buddhist cosmology of world cycles, then emphasizes that human choices can still speed up destruction or support renewal.

chapters.title: Resilience through practice
chapters.description: She speaks about congruence, self-respect, honoring pain for the world, and letting grief transform into strength rather than despair.

chapters.title: Concrete commitments
chapters.description: She introduces the shared action list and the Pali term adhiṭṭhāna as a firm resolve to choose a period of practice and action.

chapters.title: What to do now
chapters.description: She recommends resources, urges climate truth-telling, and gives practical examples of petitions, rallies, letters, government pressure, and organized collective action.

chapters.title: Questions and everyday choices
chapters.description: In the final exchange she discusses renewables, divestment, local banks, food choices, and the value of imperfect but sincere action guided by the larger systemic picture.

title: Today’s Climate Reality
speaker: Ayya Santussika
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: What happens when a climate talk begins by treating the facts as something to look at directly, even if they are hard to bear? The talk moves from the immediate evidence of warming, ice loss, drought, fires, and acidifying oceans into the human systems tied to them: violence, food insecurity, fossil-fuel economics, and the barriers that keep people from acting. It then shifts toward a practical response, arguing for emissions cuts, carbon pricing, keeping most fossil fuels in the ground, and broader changes in poverty, education, and restoration.
short_summary: A climate talk that starts with hard evidence and moves toward practical, collective action on emissions, justice, and resilience.

chapters.title: Look directly
chapters.description: The talk begins by placing climate reality in the frame of the First Noble Truth: face suffering clearly before trying to transform it.

chapters.title: What is happening now
chapters.description: She reviews present-day climate evidence from the AAAS report, including warming, ice loss, sea level rise, acidification, flooding, drought, storms, and fire.

chapters.title: Warming and wildfires
chapters.description: The talk continues with the causes of the crisis, the human role in rising CO2, and the growing scale of wildfires and megafires.

chapters.title: Impacts on life and conflict
chapters.description: Using the IPCC and a reference to Years of Living Dangerously, she describes how climate disruption affects ecosystems, health, food, water, and the risk of violence.

chapters.title: Maps of impact
chapters.description: She returns to the IPCC’s global picture and explains how water, marine systems, terrestrial systems, and food production are already being affected across continents.

chapters.title: California drought ahead
chapters.description: Michael Werner’s comments localize the talk to California, with Sierra snowpack, reservoirs, and a hotter, drier future.

chapters.title: The carbon budget
chapters.description: Drawing on Bill McKibben’s climate math, she explains the remaining carbon budget and the scale of continued fossil-fuel exploration.

chapters.title: Why action is blocked
chapters.description: She names the economic interests, misinformation, and subsidies that keep fossil fuels in place, and argues for a carbon tax and an end to new extraction.

chapters.title: What to do together
chapters.description: The closing turns to Lester Brown’s broader response—cut emissions, stabilize population growth, reduce poverty, restore ecosystems, and keep working together.

title: How To Be an Earthling
speaker: Wes Nisker
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) with Kevin Griffin on guitar
podcast_description: How do you respond when ecological crisis feels like a constant drumbeat of apocalypse? The talk moves through humor, song, and satire—beginning with Gary Snyder’s ecological Pledge and John Muir’s poetry, then widening into a critique of fear, consumerism, and the old stories that place humans outside the web of life. From there it turns to evolution, Earth Day, and a quieter instruction: simplify, protect the earth, and return again and again to here and now.
short_summary: A playful Earth Day talk that uses poems, songs, and evolutionary imagery to move from crisis anxiety toward interdependence, simplicity, and present-moment awareness.

chapters.title: Ecological pledge
chapters.description: Wes opens with Gary Snyder’s ecological Pledge of Allegiance to frame the talk around belonging to the earth and all beings.

chapters.title: John Muir and wonder
chapters.description: He honors John Muir and reads a short poem on the earth’s continuous cycles of sunrise, sunset, and turning.

chapters.title: Crisis and comic relief
chapters.description: A song about modern anxieties turns environmental dread and media saturation into a shared, playful release.

chapters.title: Liberation and Passover
chapters.description: Wes imagines Moses, Jesus, and Buddha in contemporary terms, linking spiritual renewal with liberation from greed and bondage.

chapters.title: Earth Day and Gaia
chapters.description: He praises Earth Day as a universal holy day and invokes Gaia, Pachamama, and the planet’s gravity as forms of care and support.

chapters.title: Evolution as creation myth
chapters.description: Wes contrasts the Holocene and Anthropocene, then asks listeners to take evolution seriously as a new story of who we are.

chapters.title: Made of earth
chapters.description: He emphasizes that our bodies are literally made from earth and shared life, and urges simpler living and practical protection of the planet.

chapters.title: Enough stuff
chapters.description: Kevin Griffin’s song satirizes consumer excess and names the feeling that we already have more than enough.

chapters.title: Here and now
chapters.description: The closing teaching becomes a call-and-response on presence, reducing the message to a simple instruction: be here now.

title: Compassion As We Take Action
speaker: Jack Kornfield
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Recorded from Spirit Rock's Earth Day Gathering
podcast_description: How do you act for the world without being driven by guilt, anger, or the need to control outcomes? The talk moves through stories, poems, and reflections on climate grief, kinship with animals, and long-horizon social change, all building toward the claim that effective action has to come from love, tenderness, and trust. It then turns into a guided meditation on breath, vastness, and a personally chosen bodhisattva vow, before ending with a blessing cord ceremony rooted in refuge, compassion, and non-harming.
short_summary: A talk on compassionate action that weaves together poems, activist stories, and a guided vow-and-blessing meditation.

chapters.title: Opening questions
chapters.description: He invites the room to name what would help as they return from the Earth Day gathering back into ordinary life.

chapters.title: Act from love
chapters.description: Using Gary Snyder's advice, he argues that effective action comes from love and tenderness rather than guilt, bitterness, or fighting.

chapters.title: Grief into song
chapters.description: A Gabriela Mistral poem and a line from Dina Metzger frame grief, beauty, and the possibility of transforming hurt into something luminous.

chapters.title: Finding our way
chapters.description: The Hawaiian voyaging tradition becomes an image for orientation, embodied wisdom, and what to do when we feel lost.

chapters.title: Interbeing and seeds
chapters.description: Stories of chimps, dolphins, and world-wide generosity lead into the image of carrying seeds of intention rather than trying to hold the whole world.

chapters.title: Long horizons
chapters.description: Ariyaratne's peace plan, the Dalai Lama's response, and Thomas Merton's counsel all point toward patient work without attachment to results.

chapters.title: A bodhisattva vow
chapters.description: He names the bodhisattva path and reads Diane Ackerman's vow as a public commitment to nature, healing, wonder, and peace.

chapters.title: Meditation on vastness
chapters.description: A long guided meditation expands the listener from breath and heart into a vast identity, then returns them to a personal vow and compassionate presence.

chapters.title: Blessing cords
chapters.description: He explains the symbolic cords, ties them to refuge, compassion, and intention, and has people share their vows with one another.

title: The Eco-Crisis as a Spiritual Crisis
speaker: David Loy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) There are precise and profound parallels between what Buddhism says about our usual individual predicament and our collective ecological predicament today.
podcast_description: What if the ecological crisis is the larger form of the same delusion that makes a person feel separate, anxious, and always lacking? The talk moves from the visible surface of climate breakdown, species loss, and poisoned oceans into a deeper argument about industrial growth, consumer capitalism, and the ways Buddhism can be reduced to coping. Along the way, it uses the image of two icebergs, then turns to Zen teachings, a poem about extinction, and lyrical readings that point toward non-separation rather than escape or mere adjustment.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk that links ecological collapse with Buddhism’s teaching on self and separateness, using the iceberg metaphor, poems, and Zen references to argue that both crises are spiritual as well as social.

chapters.title: Fragile Earth
chapters.description: Loy opens with Earth Day framing and simple images of thin water and air to show how fragile the planet is.

chapters.title: Seeing the Iceberg
chapters.description: He introduces the two-icebergs metaphor and broadens the ecological crisis beyond climate change to fisheries, plastic, and mass extinction.

chapters.title: Reading Extinction
chapters.description: A poem by Sam Taylor brings the loss of species into a more immediate, emotional register.

chapters.title: Beyond Climate Alone
chapters.description: Loy turns from visible symptoms to the deeper causes in industrial growth society and then uses the second iceberg to question Buddhism's public face.

chapters.title: What Awakening Means
chapters.description: He contrasts escape-oriented and fit-in versions of Buddhism with a third view in which awakening deconstructs the separate self and opens compassion.

chapters.title: Growth and Alienation
chapters.description: The talk draws precise parallels between the individual sense of lack and civilization's obsession with endless growth, technology, and economic expansion.

chapters.title: Intimacy With the World
chapters.description: Loy shifts to the felt realization of non-separation through examples from nature and two poems that point toward belonging rather than isolation.

chapters.title: A Spiritual Crisis
chapters.description: He returns to the ecological crisis as a collective spiritual problem and closes by insisting that inner transformation and social transformation cannot really be separated.

title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: Earth As Witness: International Dharma Teachers' Statement on Climate Change
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley)
podcast_description: How do we meet climate change without treating it as something outside our own lives? The talk begins with simple breath-based inquiry and expands into the carbon cycle, the greenhouse effect, and the urgency of rising emissions, then turns toward a Dharma teachers' statement that links suffering, craving, and interdependence to climate response. From there it becomes practical: household changes, mindful consumption, collective action, policy pressure, and a final move into loving-kindness for the Earth. The overall arc is sober but not despairing, holding the crisis as a call to wake up and act together.
short_summary: A Dharma-informed climate talk that moves from the breath and the carbon cycle to collective action, practical change, and closing metta for the Earth.

chapters.title: Opening the conversation
chapters.description: James Baraz introduces Bob Doppelt, his climate work, and the book that connects Dharma principles with climate response.

chapters.title: Breath and interdependence
chapters.description: Bob begins by asking how we are part of the Earth's climate, using breath, oxygen, carbon, and daily life to show direct participation in Earth systems.

chapters.title: The science of warming
chapters.description: He explains greenhouse gases, the long time lag of climate effects, and why the present crisis is both serious and still responsive to action.

chapters.title: Facing the suffering
chapters.description: Bob names the fear and sorrow in the room and prepares listeners to hear the teachers' statement as something to feel, not only analyze.

chapters.title: Earth as Witness
chapters.description: He reads the climate statement from Dharma teachers, linking climate change to suffering, craving, aversion, delusion, and the need for swift reductions in emissions.

chapters.title: Dharma principles for action
chapters.description: The statement unfolds into wisdom, ethical conduct, mindfulness, collective effort, and a call to reshape institutions and daily habits.

chapters.title: Hope through visible change
chapters.description: James and Bob discuss cautious optimism, the importance of talking about concrete changes, and how shared examples can shift consciousness.

chapters.title: Individual and systemic shifts
chapters.description: Bob gives examples from home energy use, solar power, policy, corporations, and regulation to show how personal and institutional change reinforce each other.

chapters.title: Tipping points and metta
chapters.description: The talk closes with the idea that a small committed shift can matter, a reminder that suffering can become initiation, and a loving-kindness practice for the Earth.

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Three)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if the images and stories that move through practice are not just private material to be managed, but forces that shape how the world appears? The talk pushes past the usual idea of mindfulness as simply staying with experience, and instead turns toward conceptual frameworks, emptiness, and different ways of looking that can either flatten life or open it. Drawing on examples from Buddhist texts, Jung, Hillman, Rilke, myth, and psychotherapy, the speaker keeps asking whether an image is something to integrate—or something larger that asks something of us.
short_summary: A talk on self, emptiness, and imaginal practice that argues our deepest assumptions and images shape reality far more than we usually notice.

chapters.title: Opening the series
chapters.description: He situates the talk as a continuation of a wider reframing of Dharma, noting that some listeners may want only small techniques while the larger movement is much farther-reaching.

chapters.title: Less fabrication
chapters.description: He distinguishes simple mindfulness and staying at contact from a deeper move toward less fabrication, while stressing that one cannot live permanently in an unfabricated state.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: The talk develops the idea that imagination, perception, and self are always shaped by assumptions, and that the Dharma can be understood as skillful ways of looking that reveal emptiness.

chapters.title: Concepts underneath experience
chapters.description: He shows how conceptual frameworks operate in ordinary perception, in emotional healing, and in religious traditions, using examples from the Pali Canon, Tantric Buddhism, and modern ideas of the unconscious.

chapters.title: Images are not parts
chapters.description: Burbea contrasts the common tendency to treat images as parts of the self with another way of conceiving them as presences with their own mode of being, drawing on Corbin, Robert Duncan, and gestalt therapy as points of comparison.

chapters.title: Archetypes and autonomy
chapters.description: He develops a more imaginal stance in which figures are not to be tamed or reduced, but enlivened and respected as living presences, with Rilke used to underline their autonomy and scale.

chapters.title: What the image wants
chapters.description: Using James Hillman, he argues that images make a claim on us, bear a kind of moral and aesthetic force, and may ask for service rather than integration, with examples from teachers, monks, angels, demons, and Dionysus.

chapters.title: Myth and soulfulness
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion to myth, Buddhist tradition, and even apparently realist views, suggesting that all are ways of looking that animate experience and can restore resonance, depth, and soulfulness.

chapters.title: Cautions and closing
chapters.description: He closes by naming the dangers on both sides: imaginal inflation or escapism, and on the other side flattening Dharma into realism; the talk ends as an invitation to notice the conceptual frame and try different ones with care.

title: Climate Change, Capitalism, and Buddhadharma
speaker: Joanna Macy
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) An impassioned talk that includes a little known story from the suttas about the dangers of and suffering caused by the notion of private property. During the talk, winds of 45 mph buffeted the monastery, emergency sirens sounded in the distance, and three power outages occurred. Ends with a lively discussion with the sangha.
podcast_description: How do private property, comparison, and climate collapse feed one another? The talk begins with the urgency of the 2013 Warsaw climate talks and then turns to an early Buddhist origin story, the Aggañña Sutta, to show how craving, hoarding, and fencing off land lead to hierarchy, theft, and violence. From there it moves into practical questions about what can be done now: media silence, divestment, grassroots organizing, and small groups working together rather than facing the crisis alone. It closes by returning to Sangha as a model for collective response and shared responsibility.
short_summary: An exploration of climate change through an early Buddhist critique of private property, ending in concrete calls for collective action and Sangha-based response.

chapters.title: Opening the crisis
chapters.description: She situates the talk in the climate emergency and the Warsaw COP meeting, with a focus on the urgency, injustice, and corporate power involved.

chapters.title: Buddhist lens
chapters.description: She grounds her reading in the early Pali Canon and turns toward Buddhist ideas of anatta, mine-ness, and the suffering that comes from possession.

chapters.title: Introducing the sutta
chapters.description: She introduces the Aggañña Sutta as a satirical origin story and sets up its challenge to caste and fixed social hierarchy.

chapters.title: Desire and comparison
chapters.description: She retells the story of luminous beings, tasting, differentiation, comparison, lust, and discrimination as conditions shift.

chapters.title: Property and violence
chapters.description: The story moves to rice hoarding, fences, theft, lying, and violence, which she reads as the arising of private property and social conflict.

chapters.title: Climate and capitalism
chapters.description: She returns from the sutta to the present, arguing that climate breakdown and fossil fuel power reveal the same patterns of extraction and denial.

chapters.title: How to speak and organize
chapters.description: In the discussion, she offers ways to talk about climate without forcing resistance, and points to groups like 350.org and One Earth Sangha.

chapters.title: Divestment and resistance
chapters.description: She speaks about socially responsible investing, divestment, and the limits of international agreements in the face of corporate power.

chapters.title: Local action together
chapters.description: The closing comments return to grassroots response, peer support, and small groups, ending with the Buddha’s teaching that we go forth together for the welfare of all beings.

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Two)
