speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Can imagination help reveal emptiness rather than merely decorate experience? The talk moves through that question by showing how images, archetypes, and resonances can loosen self-clinging, shift how practice feels in the body, and open perception beyond fixed biography. It ranges from devotion to Kuan Yin and the warrior tone of Thai forest teaching to visions, self-images, and the distinction between narrative images and more iconic, timeless ones. Again and again, the speaker asks not what an image represents, but what way of looking it opens.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on imagination as a meditative way of looking that can open self, practice, and world beyond reduction.

chapters.title: Practice and emptiness
chapters.description: He reviews the earlier talk and frames imagination within a larger Buddhist understanding of practice aimed at seeing emptiness, not merely coping better.

chapters.title: Images in practice
chapters.description: He surveys uses of imagination in metta, samadhi, and inner-critic work, then shows how different archetypal tones shape practice.

chapters.title: Archetype and presence
chapters.description: Through Kuan Yin, Ajahn Mun, Ajahn Maha Bua, Jimi Hendrix, and Bob Dylan, he shows how figures can embody and transmit imaginal force.

chapters.title: Love and shame reframed
chapters.description: A Mary Oliver passage and several retreat stories lead into the idea that love vivifies images and can recast shame in a more healing way.

chapters.title: Self as mythic field
chapters.description: He argues that self-images are not fixed realities, but part of a web of mythic relations, lineages, and possible identities.

chapters.title: Deliberate imaginal practice
chapters.description: He distinguishes his approach from simple identity affirmation and explains how to work with images through attention to body, mood, and dialogue.

chapters.title: Relationships with images
chapters.description: He explores many possible relationships to an image, including tenderness, eroticism, and guidance, then shares vivid retreat visions of healing.

chapters.title: Iconic, not reductive
chapters.description: He contrasts narrative images with timeless, iconic ones, warns against flattening them into psychology, and closes with a retreat image of the wind of enlightenment.

chapters.title: Concepts and closing
chapters.description: He ends by stressing that assumptions and concepts are always active in practice and says he will return next time to how they open or block the path.

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part One)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if the self is not a single solid thing, but something constructed in different ways of looking? The talk begins with emptiness and not-self, then moves through seven practice entry points for loosening identification, before turning to a more surprising claim: imagination is not a side issue, but one way the Dharma can reveal and soften selfhood. From there it opens into examples from metta, samadhi, inner critic work, and imaginal dialogue, where figures and voices are treated as active perspectives rather than literal truths.
short_summary: Starting from emptiness and not-self, the talk traces seven ways of loosening self-construction and argues that imagination and imaginal dialogue can open practice rather than merely decorate it.

chapters.title: Why Imagination Matters
chapters.description: Burbea frames the retreat as a long inquiry into the place of imagination in Dharma and in human life.

chapters.title: Emptiness and Not-Self
chapters.description: He sets emptiness and anatta as the core direction of practice, while distinguishing that from a narrow idea of mindfulness.

chapters.title: Seven Ways Through Self
chapters.description: He sketches seven practice approaches for seeing through self, from personality definitions to not-me contemplation.

chapters.title: Limits of Simplifying
chapters.description: He questions the view that the aggregates are the whole story, and shows why simplification can fail in romance, sexuality, and eros.

chapters.title: The Imaginal Opening
chapters.description: He names the imaginal as another way of seeing emptiness, one that does not flatten experience and can open the Dharma more widely.

chapters.title: Images in Practice
chapters.description: He gives familiar examples from metta, recollection, tantric visualization, and samadhi to show how images can support compassion, inspiration, and bodily ease.

chapters.title: Working With Inner Critics
chapters.description: He turns to the inner critic as an imaginal formation that can sometimes be deconstructed and sometimes engaged in dialogue.

chapters.title: Myths, Parents, and Voices
chapters.description: Through examples of duty, perfectionism, and heroic drive, he shows how images can carry depth beyond simple psychological labels.

chapters.title: Plurality and Dialogue
chapters.description: He closes by showing how imaginal persons can loosen a singular self and lead toward dialogue, transvaluation, and new ways of seeing.

title: Wise Responses to the Hindrances
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if the five hindrances are not proof that practice is going badly, but invitations to change the way the mind is relating? The talk moves through sense desire, ill will, dullness, restlessness, and doubt, showing how each can be met with a mix of antidotes, body awareness, and a less personal attitude. Again and again, the emphasis falls on loosening self-judgment, widening the frame, and noticing how perception itself is colored by the mind state. By the end, the hindrances are presented not just as obstacles, but as places where insight and freedom can actually begin.
short_summary: A practical talk on the five hindrances that focuses on attitude, perception, and skillful responses rather than self-blame.

chapters.title: Opening the Frame
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the five hindrances and sets up the talk as a look at practice attitude rather than just technique.

chapters.title: Not Personal Failure
chapters.description: He emphasizes that hindrances are part of being human and warns against taking them as evidence of inadequacy or lack of progress.

chapters.title: Samadhi Beyond Focus
chapters.description: The talk widens into a fuller account of samadhi as unification and harmonization, supported by kindness, openness, and letting be.

chapters.title: Working With Desire
chapters.description: Sense desire is met by returning gently, noticing pleasantness in the body, and learning to stay with subtler satisfactions.

chapters.title: Aversion and Ill Will
chapters.description: He offers ways to soften ill will by changing perception, feeling the pain inside aversion, and simplifying attention to what is actually felt.

chapters.title: Listening Underneath
chapters.description: Burbea shows how anger can carry useful energy and how boredom, resistance, and other states may be supported by aversion and deeper emotions.

chapters.title: Dullness and Energy
chapters.description: He turns to dullness and drowsiness, suggesting breath, posture, light, spaciousness, movement, and whole-body awareness as ways to brighten practice.

chapters.title: Restlessness and Subtle Effort
chapters.description: Restlessness and worry are met through the out-breath, space, and a careful tuning of effort, including the subtle forms of sinking and drifting.

chapters.title: Doubt and Perception
chapters.description: The talk closes with doubt, the value of questioning, and the larger insight that mind states color perception and can be known without believing them.

title: All In This Together
speaker: Catherine McGee
description: (Gaia House) The inter-relationship between inner and outer. Knowing that we belong as a basis for meeting and responding to the suffering of our world, both inner and outer.
podcast_description: What does it mean to be responsible for suffering without getting pulled into compulsion, guilt, or despair? The talk moves from Earth Care Week and the reality of climate-linked suffering into the practice question of how inner work and outer action are not separate, using examples from John Francis, a Zen teaching on response, and the image of the Buddha as awake and emptied out. It then turns inward again, showing how retreat can expose loneliness, anger, and the need to fix, and how those very moments can become a wider, more compassionate responsiveness.
short_summary: A talk on interdependence, where inner practice and outer care are shown to belong together, and where awakening is described as a clear, non-compulsive response to suffering.

chapters.title: Earth Care Frame
chapters.description: Catherine opens by situating the talk in Earth Care Week and naming the larger environmental context behind the retreat theme.

chapters.title: Inner and Outer
chapters.description: She asks how Dharma practice meets overwhelming global suffering without collapsing into guilt, urgency, or helplessness.

chapters.title: We Are the Environment
chapters.description: Using John Francis as an example, she shows how restraint, silence, and sustained attention can become a direct response to ecological harm.

chapters.title: No Separation
chapters.description: She deepens the teaching that inner and outer are not truly separate, and that action affects the whole even when it seems small.

chapters.title: Resonance and Loneliness
chapters.description: A story from retreat about a bucket and the need to be seen opens into the heart-mind's longing for resonance and its capacity to turn back toward itself.

chapters.title: Emptying Out
chapters.description: She turns to the Buddha as 'awake' and to emptiness as the clearing of greed, hate, and confusion so responsiveness can arise without strain.

chapters.title: Who Hears?
chapters.description: Kuan Yin and the question 'who hears?' guide the talk into compassionate listening that can hold both inner cries and the cries of the world.

chapters.title: Community Awakening
chapters.description: She weaves in Thich Nhat Hanh, Siddhartha, and Zen teaching to describe awakening as collective, intimate, and responsive to changing conditions.

chapters.title: Bodhisattva Vow
chapters.description: The talk closes with Shantideva's aspiration to be medicine, shelter, and sustenance for beings until freedom, ending in shared sitting.

title: The Anatomy of Ignorance 2 - The Social Roots of Ignorance and climate Disruption
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) For Earth Care Week we review the three aspects of ignorance examined last week - personal (or psychological), social, and universal. We focus on the social roots of ignorance with attention mostly to understanding and responding to the climate crisis.
podcast_description: Climate disruption is taken up first as a problem of ignorance: personal, social, and universal. The talk moves from a sobering account of the science and the scale of the carbon budget to a closer look at denial, emotional overwhelm, and the ways individualism can keep larger systems out of view. It then turns practical, asking what can be done at the levels of personal conduct, community, and collective action, with brief attention to divestment, institutional change, and the role of practice in staying open to fear and grief without being paralyzed by them.
short_summary: A sober, practical talk on climate disruption that connects Buddhist practice with denial, collective grief, and the kinds of action that can still matter.

chapters.title: Reviewing the Three Ignorances
chapters.description: Rothberg recalls the previous week’s framework of personal, social, and universal ignorance and explains why naming ignorance can be sobering and hopeful at once.

chapters.title: Social Conditioning
chapters.description: He develops the social dimension of ignorance through race, gender, individualism, and the ways culture keeps larger systems out of view.

chapters.title: Climate Disruption
chapters.description: The talk turns to the climate crisis, using the IPCC report and Bill McKibben’s carbon-budget framing to show the scale of the mismatch between science and current plans.

chapters.title: How We Don’t Take It In
chapters.description: Rothberg explores how people know the facts but do not fully receive them, describing climate denial as intellectual, emotional, and tokenistic.

chapters.title: Grief and Processing
chapters.description: Drawing on Joanna Macy, he describes the need for shared settings where fear, sadness, shock, and overwhelm can be felt and transformed into energy for action.

chapters.title: Personal and Local Action
chapters.description: He moves through practical responses at the individual and institutional level, including simplicity, transportation choices, local carbon footprints, and examples such as Berkeley’s climate planning.

chapters.title: Collective Mobilization
chapters.description: Rothberg argues that technical solutions already exist, but they require a committed minority, and he presents Joanna Macy’s three-part model of preventing damage, shifting institutions, and shifting consciousness.

chapters.title: Practice and Motivation
chapters.description: He emphasizes that climate action needs Dharma resources such as compassion, equanimity, and a different motivation rooted in care for future generations.

chapters.title: Questions and Final Counsel
chapters.description: In discussion, he addresses the realities of fossil-fuel dependence, the limits of individual virtue, and the difficulty of speaking about crisis in a Dharma setting, then closes by returning to the ethical contradiction of harming one’s own children and grandchildren.

title: The Broader Context of Mindfulness
speaker: Mark Coleman
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Mindfulness is so much more that attention. Supporting wholesome qualities like patience and equanimity, Right Mindfulness specifically develops wise action in our lives and in the world.
podcast_description: What does mindfulness become when it is more than bare attention? The talk moves from a critique of “McMindfulness” and the easy commercialization of attention practices into stories from hiking, panic, email, military training, and public action, showing mindfulness as something that can pause reactivity, gather wisdom, and lead to wiser conduct. Rather than treating it as a passive witnessing practice, the speaker keeps returning to how clarity, equanimity, and restraint support speech, ethics, and compassionate intervention in the world.
short_summary: A grounded talk arguing that mindfulness is not just attention, but a practice that supports restraint, wisdom, and wise action in daily life and larger moral situations.

chapters.title: Mindfulness Beyond Attention
chapters.description: Mark introduces the talk by responding to the popularized, decontextualized view of mindfulness as bare attention.

chapters.title: Rooted in Tradition
chapters.description: He situates mindfulness inside the larger Buddhist training and introduces his mountain stories as a way to explore it.

chapters.title: Clarity in Action
chapters.description: A night hike and a panic attack on the mountain become examples of how presence, breath, and groundedness can help in crisis.

chapters.title: Path, Ethics, Wisdom
chapters.description: He places mindfulness within ethics, meditation, and wisdom, and shows how it reveals karma, self-talk, and the causes of suffering.

chapters.title: Unwise Attention
chapters.description: Mark explores how attention can be used manipulatively, including in advertising, status-seeking, and military contexts.

chapters.title: Qualities of Presence
chapters.description: The group reflects on what arises with mindfulness: safety, joy, cohesion, intuition, less delusion, and a lighter heart.

chapters.title: From Awareness to Conduct
chapters.description: He argues that mindfulness gathers wholesome qualities and then moves outward as speech, conduct, and non-harming action.

chapters.title: Stories of Intervention
chapters.description: A second camping story, the Buddha stopping a war, and Christopher Titmuss confronting a political leader all illustrate mindful action in the world.

chapters.title: Practice and Climate
chapters.description: Mark ends with reflections on how mindfulness informs action, including the climate crisis and Earth Care Week.

title: Practicing in Times of Big Changes
speaker: Ayya Santacitta
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Hartford Street Zen Center))
podcast_description: When a worldview starts to break down, what lets practice stay steady instead of reaching for escape? The talk moves from a long opening chant into a clear argument that change is not forced but seen through, using the butterfly story, the Buddha’s stillness under Mara, and the teaching on paññā as the capacity to be with things as they are. It then turns practical: feeling is named as the crucial point where grasping can be interrupted, before reaction hardens into anger or more becoming.
short_summary: A talk on meeting social and inner upheaval by staying with experience, seeing clearly, and meeting feeling before it turns into grasping.

chapters.title: Opening chant
chapters.description: The talk begins with an extended chant and settles the room before the teaching starts.

chapters.title: Facing unavoidable change
chapters.description: She turns to the reality that things will not return to an old pattern and frames practice as a support for meeting what is here.

chapters.title: Butterfly transformation
chapters.description: A caterpillar-to-butterfly story becomes an image for cultural breakdown, latent change, and the conditions for a new worldview.

chapters.title: Seeing without forcing
chapters.description: She emphasizes that change is born from looking closely and staying with what is happening rather than trying to manufacture an outcome.

chapters.title: Mara becomes flowers
chapters.description: The Buddha’s night under the Bodhi tree is used to show how not moving away from difficulty can let wisdom and compassion grow.

chapters.title: Inner and outer climate
chapters.description: She links personal practice to collective conditions, including the climate crisis, while allowing for the need to rest and turn away sometimes.

chapters.title: Paññā and insight
chapters.description: Paññā is described as the capacity to be with things as they are, and repeated clear seeing is said to mature into insight.

chapters.title: Where practice interrupts
chapters.description: The closing question turns to feeling, grasping, and the quick moment where mindfulness can stop the cycle before it becomes anger or clinging.

venue: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Hartford Street Zen Center

title: Follow Your Heartbreak
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) Inspired by Andrew Harvey's book A Guide to Sacred Activism. The journey of transformation, both personal and global, includes our hearts opening to all the suffering we encounter and letting our heart be shattered and break open to new possibilities. This is a natural and essential component of true awakening. This process is explored on different levels: our own dharma practice, the classical Progress of Insight and what Harvey calls "The Dark Night of the Species."
podcast_description: What if the heartbreak of seeing the world clearly is not a detour from practice, but part of the path itself? Drawing on Andrew Harvey’s sacred activism, the talk moves from a sobering view of global crisis into the suggestion that collective upheaval and retreat life follow the same pattern: dissolution, fear, letting go, and eventually a more spacious balance. Along the way, the speaker brings in the Kali Yuga, the progress of insight, the hero’s journey, and images from Buddha, Jesus, and Star Wars to show how breaking down can become a passage to wakefulness and action.
short_summary: A talk on heartbreak, practice, and sacred activism that traces how crisis and inner difficulty can open into awakening and engaged response.

chapters.title: Opening and retreat context
chapters.description: James begins with a brief check-in about his self-retreat and the value of both solitude and practice with others.

chapters.title: Andrew Harvey's framing
chapters.description: He introduces Andrew Harvey’s book on sacred activism and explains why it has been shaping his own thinking and reading during retreat.

chapters.title: Dark night of the species
chapters.description: James uses Kali Yuga and Harvey’s language of collective decline to describe a troubled age that may also be ripe for transformation.

chapters.title: Practice through hardship
chapters.description: He turns inward, describing the ups and downs of retreat and arguing that difficulty is part of how courage, compassion, and verified faith are formed.

chapters.title: Progress of insight
chapters.description: James sketches the classical Theravada map from dissolution through fear, misery, and disgust toward equanimity and release.

chapters.title: The hero's journey
chapters.description: He connects the progress of insight with Joseph Campbell’s hero myth and examples from the Buddha, Jesus, Star Wars, and The Hunger Games.

chapters.title: Heartbreak and sacred activism
chapters.description: Returning to Andrew Harvey, James says that both personal and global transformation require a willingness to let the heart break open.

chapters.title: Signs of awakening
chapters.description: He ends with encouraging examples of global movements for justice and the possibility that consciousness can outpace fear.

title: The Dharma Of Global Climate Change
speaker: Chas DiCapua
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center) How our dharma practice can turn this difficult situation onto an opportunity for practice and awakening.
podcast_description: Global climate change is approached here as a question of how we meet what is already happening: with denial and business as usual, or by turning toward reality. The talk moves from the smoke-in-a-room experiment to Buddhist teachings on craving, contentment, interdependence, and wrong view, showing how a sense of separate self feeds both overconsumption and ecological harm. Along the way, it brings in the Buddha, Atisha, and later voices like David Korten and Joanna Macy to ask what kind of response can come from clearer seeing rather than fear. It ends by framing climate change not only as a crisis, but as a demanding opportunity for awakening and a change in how we live with one another and the earth.
short_summary: A Dharma talk that treats climate change as a test of perception, asking how clearer seeing, contentment, and interdependence can shape a more fitting response.

chapters.title: Opening the Topic
chapters.description: The talk begins by framing climate change as something to turn toward carefully, rather than avoid, and by setting up the Dharma questions that will guide the rest of the evening.

chapters.title: Smoke and Social Freeze
chapters.description: A room-smoke experiment is used to show how people can ignore clear danger when others act as if nothing is wrong, much like the cultural freeze around climate change.

chapters.title: Why It Matters
chapters.description: He explains why climate change is not just a political matter, but a moral and practical one that affects everyone, though not equally, and invites a conscious response.

chapters.title: Craving and Contentment
chapters.description: The talk turns to the Second Noble Truth, linking craving for sense pleasure to both suffering and ecological strain, then introducing Atisha's teaching on contentment.

chapters.title: Possession and Stewardship
chapters.description: He questions the assumption that land and resources can really be owned, and suggests that a stewardship view would naturally lead to less harm and more responsibility.

chapters.title: Interdependence and Story
chapters.description: Dependent arising, social narratives, and David Korten's warning about stories out of tune with reality are used to show how collective habits shape collective outcomes.

chapters.title: Cause, Effect, and Avoidance
chapters.description: Everyday examples of consumption, travel, and waste illustrate cause and effect, followed by Bob Doppelt's account of blame and avoidance and a return to the inner forces Dharma can address.

chapters.title: Mind and Separate Self
chapters.description: The Dhammapada and Ajahn Chah are used to point back to mind as the place where suffering and clarity are made, and Dilgo Khyentse Rinpoche frames ignorance and separation as root causes.

chapters.title: From Fear to Awakening
chapters.description: The ending turns toward Joanna Macy's 'great turning,' Nyoshul Khenpo's nondual language, and Jennifer Welwood's poem, presenting climate change as a demanding but potent field for awakening and compassion.

title: Planet Earth as Divine Messenger
speaker: Ayya Santacitta
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Marin Sangha))
podcast_description: How do we respond when the same truth that shows up in aging, sickness, and death also appears on a planetary scale? The talk moves from the Buddha’s teaching on dukkha as unsatisfactoriness, through the story of the four sights and daily contemplation of impermanence, into a direct meditation on climate change, ecological responsibility, and the gap between knowing and acting. It closes by asking listeners to stay with discomfort, keep the heart open, and take the next workable step rather than turning away.
short_summary: A Dharma talk that connects the Buddha’s teaching on unsatisfactoriness and impermanence with climate change, ecological identity, and the practice of turning toward difficulty.

chapters.title: Arriving in the Present
chapters.description: She opens by inviting listeners to settle the body and mind, and frames practice as coming into clear contact with the present moment.

chapters.title: Dukkha and Impermanence
chapters.description: She explains the Buddha’s teaching on suffering as unsatisfactoriness, using the image of a wheel that does not quite fit and a life that is not meant to run smoothly.

chapters.title: The Four Sights
chapters.description: She retells the Buddha’s encounter with sickness, old age, death, and a truth seeker, showing how the story turned him toward renunciation.

chapters.title: Extending the Contemplation
chapters.description: She describes the monastic practice of reflecting daily on aging, illness, and death, then extends that contemplation to the planet and future generations.

chapters.title: Climate as Context
chapters.description: She argues that climate change makes the Buddha’s teaching newly urgent, because the same honesty about impermanence now applies at the scale of the Earth.

chapters.title: Home, Ecology, and Ego
chapters.description: Using the roots of ecology and economy, she suggests that we have forgotten how to care for our shared home and mistaken the larger context for something separate from ourselves.

chapters.title: Turning Toward Dissonance
chapters.description: She uses the Four Noble Truths and a reference to Philip Moffat to emphasize that healing comes from turning toward difficulty rather than escaping it.

chapters.title: Earth as Sacred Text
chapters.description: With Thomas Berry’s line about the universe as sacred scripture, she reflects on cosmic evolution, interdependence, and the need to respect what we do not know.

chapters.title: Ecological Self
chapters.description: She quotes Arne Naess on the ecological self, then connects non-self, meditation, and the social difficulty of acting on what we already know.

title: Learning from Climate Change
speaker: Ayya Santacitta
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery)
podcast_description: How do we face climate change without shutting down, and how do we speak about it without avoiding the discomfort it brings? The talk moves from that practical conundrum into a larger argument: climate grief, confusion, and urgency are not signs of failure but part of the process of waking up to a worldview that no longer fits. Along the way, it brings in Thomas Berry, Arne Næss, Joanna Macy, and the Easter Island collapse to show why the response has to be both inward and collective. A final image from Rumi leaves the listener with the sense of standing at the edge of the roof and needing to be still enough to see clearly.
short_summary: A climate talk that treats discomfort, grief, and urgency as part of a necessary shift from a mechanistic worldview to deeper ecological belonging.

chapters.title: Naming the dilemma
chapters.description: She opens with the challenge of teaching climate change honestly without pushing people away.

chapters.title: Shock and denial
chapters.description: Climate disruption is compared to traumatic news that first meets disbelief, anger, and shutdown.

chapters.title: Discomfort as guidance
chapters.description: She argues that discomfort carries information and can help communities adjust course.

chapters.title: Universe as scripture
chapters.description: Thomas Berry’s line becomes a way to trust the larger intelligence of evolution beyond the thinking mind.

chapters.title: Outgrown worldviews
chapters.description: She traces the Enlightenment and mechanistic thinking, suggesting they no longer fit current conditions.

chapters.title: Grief and ecological self
chapters.description: Using Arne Næss and Joanna Macy, she describes how sitting with climate grief can open a wider identity and a more natural response.

chapters.title: Attention and resilience
chapters.description: She returns to the need for samatha, clear attention, and collective resilience in the face of overwhelming information.

chapters.title: Learning from collapse
chapters.description: Easter Island and Rumi frame the danger of overshoot, while also pointing to humility, practice, and the possibility of waking up.

chapters.title: Closing appeal
chapters.description: She ends by saying the crisis can catapult us into a new worldview if we are willing to meet it and keep listening.

title: The Brahma Viharas and the Climate Challenge
speaker: Ayya Anandabodhi
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley)
podcast_description: How do you meet climate fear without collapsing into denial, panic, or resignation? The talk begins from that tension and moves through the brahma-viharas as a lived response: calming the heart with loving-kindness, opening to suffering with compassion, finding uplift in the beauty and life of the world, and widening into equanimity. Along the way, the speaker pairs Buddhist teaching with concrete images—a pelican’s broad view, a sick monk cared for by the Buddha, the reality of children and grandchildren living in a changing world—to show how wisdom and care have to stay together.
short_summary: A talk on meeting the climate crisis through the brahma-viharas, from metta and compassion to equanimity and responsible action.

chapters.title: Opening Homage
chapters.description: The talk begins with traditional homage to the Buddha and a quiet opening gesture of respect.

chapters.title: Practice in Turbulent Times
chapters.description: She introduces the setting, names the uncertainty of the age, and describes meditation as learning how the mind is pulled by desire, aversion, and confusion.

chapters.title: Awakening Is Human
chapters.description: She reflects on the Buddha as a human being and on awakening as a real possibility rather than something remote or exceptional.

chapters.title: Training Awareness
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts to recognizing agitation, seeing states clearly, and returning again and again to presence and choice.

chapters.title: Facing Climate Anxiety
chapters.description: She turns directly to climate change, naming denial, overwhelm, and the need to let the reality in without collapsing into fear.

chapters.title: Metta and Compassion
chapters.description: A personal meditation shows how loving-kindness can steady the heart enough to open compassionately to suffering.

chapters.title: Mudita and Equanimity
chapters.description: She moves through appreciative joy and equanimity, using the beauty of Earth and the image of pelicans to evoke spacious, enduring love.

chapters.title: Ultimate and Conventional
chapters.description: She contrasts the broad, changing view with the close, frightened view, then tells the story of the Buddha caring for a sick monk to show that wisdom must include care.

chapters.title: Ethics and Renunciation
chapters.description: The talk becomes more explicit about pollution, consumption, and responsibility, and she frames the Brahma-viharas and right intention as tools for skillful action.

title: 400 and Rising
speaker: Ayya Anandabodhi
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery)
podcast_description: What do we do when the climate crisis feels too big to hold, and how do we stay open without becoming numb? The talk begins with a recent trip through monastery communities and the sight of eagles, then turns toward the sharper question of rising carbon, collective denial, and the feeling of living inside a damaged world. From there it moves through the four Brahma Viharas—loving-kindness, compassion, appreciative joy, and equanimity—as ways to meet grief with clarity and action, without losing sight of the vulnerability of sentient life. A Buddha story about caring for a sick monk finally grounds the whole talk in ordinary responsibility: ultimate truth does not cancel the need to help.
short_summary: A reflection on climate grief, the four Brahma Viharas, and the Buddha’s teaching that insight must still become care.

chapters.title: Arriving in community
chapters.description: She opens with recent travels to monasteries and reflects on long practice, community life, and the softening that comes from sustained mutual adjustment.

chapters.title: Hermitage and the eagles
chapters.description: A visit to the Pacific Hermitage leads into images of kayaking, birds, and the felt sense of being quietly part of nature rather than separate from it.

chapters.title: The illusion of control
chapters.description: She widens into reflection on human vulnerability, modern control, and the ways city life and technological mastery can obscure our dependence on the natural world.

chapters.title: 400 parts per million
chapters.description: Using Bill McKibben and 350.org, she frames climate change in measurable terms and names 400 ppm as a serious threshold the world has already crossed.

chapters.title: Listening for the feminine
chapters.description: A brief account of hearing the Dalai Lama at an environmental summit emphasizes compassion, nurturing, listening, dialogue, and education as urgently needed qualities.

chapters.title: Seeing the larger truth
chapters.description: She brings in impermanence, no-self, and collective responsibility, asking how Buddhist practice meets the reality of climate grief and shared harm.

chapters.title: Four Brahma Viharas
chapters.description: Loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity are presented as inner supports that steady the heart without denying the crisis.

chapters.title: Equanimity and karma
chapters.description: She explains equanimity through the Buddha’s reflection on karma, emphasizing intention, responsibility, and the freedom to shape how we meet the present.

chapters.title: Care for what is sick
chapters.description: The talk ends by holding ultimate truth together with relative care, using the sick monk story to argue that impermanence never excuses neglect.

title: Reflections on the Day
speaker: Nina Wise
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: It begins with a body: a short, playful sequence of stretches, shakes, and sounds that opens into the claim that changing the world may require more of that kind of embodied attention. From there, the talk moves from climate grief and the film Chasing Ice into stories of people meeting loss directly, then turning toward action through love, weeping, and responsibility. A long closing story about a prison garden and a boy with a gun gathers these threads into one question: how seeing our interdependence can soften fear and make room for care.
short_summary: A movement-filled talk that moves from body practice to climate grief and a prison garden story, returning again and again to love, loss, and interdependence.

chapters.title: Opening into the body
chapters.description: Nina begins with a brief, humorous introduction and a playful standing sequence that wakes up the body and the room.

chapters.title: Motion theater and release
chapters.description: She introduces motion theater, explains the form, and continues a lively series of movements, sounds, and loosening exercises.

chapters.title: From me to we
chapters.description: The talk turns from embodied play to the idea that change requires moving beyond the isolated self toward a larger communal identity.

chapters.title: Glaciers and grief
chapters.description: A viewing of Chasing Ice becomes a direct encounter with climate loss, and she follows that clear seeing into grief and love.

chapters.title: A prison garden begins
chapters.description: She tells Catherine Sneed’s story of turning a jail yard into a garden through cleanup, bureaucracy, and patient work with the men.

chapters.title: Planting and recognition
chapters.description: The story continues as a boy with a gun appears, the tree is planted, and the scene shifts from threat to a moment of shared human recognition.

chapters.title: Stardust and non-self
chapters.description: The final story links water, clouds, wind, sun, stars, and the hand, using that web of causes to point toward interdependence and the easing of 'me'.

chapters.title: Closing in the hills
chapters.description: Nina closes by imagining the drive home, the brown hills as gold, and a final attention to lupine and stars.

title: Active Hope
speaker: Joanna Macy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: How do you stay present when the world seems to be moving toward collapse? The talk begins with a rethinking of hope as something you do, not something you possess, and then moves into a wider bodhisattva view: caring enough to step forward, even without a fail-proof plan. From there it opens into ecological grief, the Cousteau plankton warning, stories of nuclear power and tar sands, and a practical insistence on collective action, honest witnessing, and not turning away. In the final stretch, the talk turns more explicitly to the Avatamsaka and Flower Garland vision of interdependence, using it to hold climate crisis, future generations, and present responsibility in one frame.
short_summary: A clear, grounded talk on active hope, bodhisattva practice, and staying engaged with ecological संकट without panic or paralysis.

chapters.title: Opening and the book
chapters.description: The talk opens with a song reference and introduces Active Hope as the frame for the evening.

chapters.title: Hope as a verb
chapters.description: Macy explains why she no longer treats hope as wishful thinking, but as an active way of stepping forward.

chapters.title: Bodhisattva and bodhicitta
chapters.description: She defines Bodhisattva and bodhicitta as caring, interdependent intention rather than private salvation.

chapters.title: The plankton shock
chapters.description: A memory of Cousteau’s warning about plankton becomes the turning point that changes her sense of ground and purpose.

chapters.title: Panic and paralysis
chapters.description: She describes the two ditches of responding to crisis and the need to stay present without checking out.

chapters.title: Working together
chapters.description: The story of Ralph Nader’s movement and the tar sands flyover shows how action, witness, and companionship change despair.

chapters.title: Grief into action
chapters.description: A song about the tar sands leads into reflections on mourning, renewable rebuilding, and being moved by a larger purpose.

chapters.title: Service across time
chapters.description: Macy quotes a climate scientist on what service requires now and expands responsibility across generations and lifetimes.

chapters.title: One hair, all worlds
chapters.description: She ends with the Avatamsaka Sutra’s vision of interpenetration, where one body, one moment, and one place contain the whole web of life.

title: Connecting Inner and Outer Responses to Climate Change
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) At this time of climate disruption, we need powerful responses--integrating more "inner" spiritual practices and principles, on the one hand, with skill in "outer" responses, on the other. This integration or marriage can happen in many ways as we participate in the "great turning"--whether our primary emphasis, to use Joanna Macy's analysis, is stopping further damage from occurring, transforming our institutions, or helping to shift consciousness. Without this integration, however, spiritual practice runs the risk of becoming a kind of middle-class escapism and activism runs the risk of being caught in self-righteousness, attachment to views, demonization of the "enemy," and burnout. We need a new integration! We look at several dharma principles that can be the basis for such an integration, consider briefly how Spirit Rock is responding (and might respond further) to climate issues, and especially look at the figure of the bodhisattva.
podcast_description: How do inner practice and outer response meet when climate disruption asks for more than private steadiness or public outrage? The talk moves from a short Tibetan invocation on awareness, through the risks of Buddhist escapism and activist burnout, into a wide-ranging case for a bodhisattva-like response that can hold suffering without losing clarity. It then becomes practical, naming personal habits, community action, institutional change, and the larger shift in consciousness needed to face climate change together.
short_summary: A talk on climate change that argues for joining meditation, ethics, and collective action through the bodhisattva ideal.

chapters.title: Opening Invocation
chapters.description: Rothberg begins with a short meditation on spacious, steady awareness and uses it to dissolve the split between inner practice and the earth.

chapters.title: Why Integration Matters
chapters.description: He lays out the central theme: without connecting practice and action, Dharma can become escapism and activism can become reactive or self-righteous.

chapters.title: The Bodhisattva Ideal
chapters.description: Drawing on bodhisattva vows across traditions, he presents the bodhisattva as the figure who can hold awakening and service together.

chapters.title: Finding Your Calling
chapters.description: Rothberg uses Howard Thurman and Joanna Macy to suggest that people have different roles in the great turning and should find the work that makes them come alive.

chapters.title: Mindfulness Must Act
chapters.description: He quotes Thich Nhat Hanh to show engaged Buddhism as a decision to practice and help at the same time, especially in the face of suffering.

chapters.title: Dharma and Climate Practice
chapters.description: The talk turns to ethics, wisdom, and the need to widen practice beyond the individual into relational and collective life, including concrete examples from Spirit Rock and Berkeley.

chapters.title: Personal to Systemic Response
chapters.description: He moves through household changes, transportation, and larger climate strategies, arguing that personal discipline matters most when it supports systemic change.

chapters.title: Reflective Exercise
chapters.description: Rothberg invites listeners to name what calls them now, then asks them to share with a neighbor where they feel drawn to respond.

chapters.title: Closing Readings
chapters.description: He ends with short readings from Rabbi Tarfon, a Zen teacher, and Deena Metzger, emphasizing steady work, ordinary practice, and love in urgent times.

title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: From Me to We
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: Climate change is framed here as a test of whether awareness can move faster than fear. The talk begins with a call for sangha and teacher leadership, including a letter asking Buddhist teachers to offer guidance on the crisis, then shifts into a practical framework drawn from Bob Doppelt’s From Me to We. From there it becomes a series of clear Dharma-to-action movements: interdependence, cause and effect, moral conduct, trusteeship, and free will, each tied back to how we live, choose, and take responsibility in a closed planetary system.
short_summary: A climate-focused Dharma talk that moves from sangha organizing into a five-part framework for shifting from individualism to interdependence, ethical action, and ecological responsibility.

chapters.title: Opening the gathering
chapters.description: The talk begins with the Earth Day setting, the climate focus, and the introduction of James Baraz and Bob Doppelt.

chapters.title: Sangha and teacher request
chapters.description: James Baraz speaks about collective refuge, the need for Dharma leadership on climate change, and the letter inviting teachers to respond.

chapters.title: Bob's framework
chapters.description: Bob Doppelt is introduced through his climate work and his book From Me to We, which becomes the bridge into his teaching framework.

chapters.title: Climate as closed system
chapters.description: Bob explains climate change as a global systems problem, introduces the blue marble image, and names the five commitments as a Dharma-informed sustainability model.

chapters.title: Breath and interdependence
chapters.description: A guided reflection uses the breath to point directly to oxygen, photosynthesis, and the web of life, leading into emptiness and dependent origination.

chapters.title: Awareness and systems
chapters.description: The teaching turns to increased awareness, systems mapping, and the first two commitments: seeing the systems you are part of and understanding cause and effect.

chapters.title: Moral choice
chapters.description: Bob develops the decision-making exercise and the third commitment, moral justice, emphasizing do no harm and the need for clearer ethical intention.

chapters.title: Trusteeship and restoration
chapters.description: The fourth commitment frames us as temporary trustees who must not only reduce harm but actively restore the earth.

chapters.title: Free will and closing
chapters.description: The final commitment is free will shaped by wisdom, followed by audience reflection, a brief discussion, and a closing John Seed quote about waking up and choosing change.

title: Dharma practice and the climate Crisis
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) At the time of Earth Day, we pose a a dilemma and challenge. Dharma practice is so compelling. The climate crisis is so compelling. Which do we choose? Both, in our own ways. We look at the ways that our practice (and principles of generosity, compassion, ethics, interdependence, and wisdom) call us to regard and that those responding deeply need spiritual practices and principles to meet the great need.
podcast_description: The talk begins with a plain dilemma: meditation practice is compelling, and the climate crisis is compelling, so how do we not choose one against the other? From there, the speaker moves through examples, from Thich Nhat Hanh’s engaged Buddhism and Merton’s monastic activism to Joanna Macy’s “Great Turning,” to show that inner practice and outer response can belong together in different ways. The second half turns practical and personal, asking where one’s gifts lie, how to avoid burnout and self-righteousness, and why climate action without Dharma can distort, while Dharma without the world can also narrow. It ends with a brief poem about the world being on fire and “no time not to love,” holding urgency and steadiness in the same breath.
short_summary: A talk on climate crisis and Dharma that argues for a both-and path: practice deeply, respond wisely, and let the two strengthen each other.

chapters.title: Retreat and urgency
chapters.description: Rothberg opens by naming the contrast between the beauty of retreat and the urgency of the climate crisis.

chapters.title: The climate dilemma
chapters.description: He lays out the apparent choice between deep practice and worldly action, then shows why climate change makes that tension impossible to ignore.

chapters.title: Engaged Buddhism examples
chapters.description: Thich Nhat Hanh, Thomas Merton, Buddhadasa, Dorothy Day, Aung San Suu Kyi, and Gandhi are offered as models of different ways to combine inner and outer life.

chapters.title: Joanna Macy's framework
chapters.description: Rothberg turns to the Great Turning and its three modes of response: holding actions, shifting institutions, and shifting consciousness.

chapters.title: Vocation and critique
chapters.description: He asks listeners to find where their gifts meet the world's need, while also warning that meditation can become a refuge from responsibility.

chapters.title: Practice needs ethics
chapters.description: Using Gary Snyder's remarks, he argues that wisdom, meditation, and ethics belong together and that Dharma must include social awareness and non-harming.

chapters.title: Why activism needs practice
chapters.description: He names problems in activism such as anger, self-righteousness, burnout, and demonization, and says these are exactly the places where practice matters.

chapters.title: Concrete climate responses
chapters.description: The talk moves into practical examples, including household reductions, city plans, organizations, and Buddhist ecology groups.

chapters.title: Questions and closing
chapters.description: In dialogue with the audience, Rothberg discusses differences within climate work and ends by returning to urgency, equanimity, and the poem that says there is no time not to love.

title: The Necessity of Fantasy
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What happens when the image of practice narrows to calmness, non-involvement, and personal freedom, while the wider world is asking for responsibility? The talk moves from ethics as a base of the path, through climate change, species loss, and the silence of Dharma communities, into a larger claim that our lives are shaped by the stories and images we live by. By the end, the speaker is asking whether practice needs a wider range of fantasies, myths, and forms of awakening that can honor grief, outrage, engagement, and the scale of our shared life.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on how ethics, climate crisis, and the stories we live by may require Dharma to widen its image of awakening beyond calm and non-involvement.

chapters.title: Setting the frame
chapters.description: He explains that this talk will move differently from ordinary teaching, taking up the bigger picture and the role of view and imagination in practice.

chapters.title: Ethics as foundation
chapters.description: Burbea redefines ethics as how we live in relation to others, contrasts it with inner critic culture, and places it at the base of the Buddhist path.

chapters.title: Climate and responsibility
chapters.description: He widens ethics beyond the personal to species loss, climate change, and collective responsibility, using contemporary crisis as the pressure behind the talk.

chapters.title: Silence and Buddhist culture
chapters.description: He asks why Dharma communities stay quiet, brings in the language of commodification and capitalism, and questions the cultural image of spiritual practice.

chapters.title: Fantasy and image
chapters.description: Burbea argues that Buddhist practice is shaped by fantasy and image whether or not we notice it, and that one cannot simply leave fantasy behind.

chapters.title: Narrowed images
chapters.description: He critiques a constricted Dharma imagination, asking what has been excluded from the image of awakening, teacherhood, and a meaningful path.

chapters.title: Dukkha beyond the self
chapters.description: He examines precepts, omission, social structures, and the Four Noble Truths, suggesting that suffering and its causes may be far wider than individual patterns.

chapters.title: Stories that shape culture
chapters.description: Burbea turns to Norman Brown, Ben Okri, and Carl Sagan to show how stories and images alter collective feeling, values, and responsibility.

chapters.title: Widening the range
chapters.description: He closes by calling for a larger plurality of fantasies and images in Dharma, including more room for wildness, outrage, Bodhisattva aspiration, and a more engaged modern lion’s roar.

title: Approaching the Dharma: Part Two - Liberating Ways of Looking
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if insight is not a rare experience to wait for, but a way of looking that can be trained, repeated, and used? The talk starts from that question and moves through not-self, impermanence, unsatisfactoriness, metta, and mindfulness as practical lenses that can loosen clinging and reduce dukkha in the moment. It then pushes further, asking how even the sense of self, object, and awareness are fabricated together, and how deeper insight can make those fabrications begin to dissolve. The talk ends by widening the frame again: practice is less about holding to one correct view than about experimenting with skillful ways of seeing that unbind suffering.
short_summary: A careful, practice-centered talk on insight as a trainable way of looking that loosens clinging, reveals fabrication, and opens toward emptiness.

chapters.title: Setting the frame
chapters.description: Rob explains the long arc of the two talks and why this material may only make sense when held alongside the earlier discussion of emptiness.

chapters.title: Insight, loosely defined
chapters.description: He gives a broad definition of insight as any realization that decreases dukkha, and distinguishes it from mere mindfulness or knowing.

chapters.title: Insight from experience
chapters.description: Personal and universal examples show how insight can arise from one’s own experience and bring relief in the present or later on.

chapters.title: A way of looking
chapters.description: The talk turns to insight as a starting point and then to the Buddha’s teaching on not-self as a perception to train deliberately.

chapters.title: Impermanence and letting go
chapters.description: Rob develops impermanence and unsatisfactoriness as practical lenses, showing how sustained attention to change and dissatisfaction loosens clinging.

chapters.title: Metta and the Four Noble Truths
chapters.description: He widens the field to metta, mindfulness, and awareness, then frames these as present-time enactments of the Four Noble Truths through the way of looking.

chapters.title: Repetition and deeper insight
chapters.description: Repeatedly sustaining a perception consolidates insight, and then opens further shifts, including the fading of self and object through the same practice.

chapters.title: Fabrication and emptiness
chapters.description: The discussion deepens into fabrication, dependent origination, and the idea that ordinary ways of looking reinforce dukkha, while skillful ones unbind it.

chapters.title: Practice as experimentation
chapters.description: He closes by stressing that practice is the experimental cultivation of different ways of looking, each one empty, provisional, and capable of bringing freedom.

title: Approaching the Dharma: Part One - Unbinding the World
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: The talk starts with a question about the “end of the world” and slowly turns that into a question about how perception itself is built, sustained, and sometimes undone. Moving through the Bahiya sutta, the speaker argues that “in the seen, just the seen” is not mainly a technique of bare attention, but a clue to how reactivity, selfing, measurement, and story keep experience in place. From there the talk widens into a careful inquiry into fabrication, emptiness, and the limits of any simple claim that either this world or a transcendent beyond is the final answer.
short_summary: A close reading of Bahiya leads into a wider investigation of perception, selfing, fabrication, and the middle way beyond simple oppositions.

chapters.title: Opening the arc
chapters.description: Rob sets up a two-part exploration and asks listeners to stay with a longer line of thought rather than trying to pin the teaching down quickly.

chapters.title: The world of perception
chapters.description: He begins with the Buddha's saying about the end of the world and uses it to point toward the world as it is actually perceived through the senses and mind.

chapters.title: Beyond elements and extremes
chapters.description: He reads passages about a realm where the elements have no footing and brings in Sariputta to show the teaching moving beyond binary positions.

chapters.title: Bahiya's urgency
chapters.description: The story of Bahiya searching for the Buddha leads into the famous instruction and the reminder that the teaching is not just a philosophy but something to be lived.

chapters.title: Just the seen
chapters.description: Rob argues that the Bahiya instruction is not simply bare attention, but a way of dropping labels, story, and reactivity so perception is less burdened by papanca.

chapters.title: Desire and fabrication
chapters.description: He gathers more Buddha passages on abandoning desire, ending ignorance, and ceasing fabrication to show how consciousness and perception quiet when grasping stops.

chapters.title: A spectrum of construction
chapters.description: Perception, self-sense, and dukkha are presented as mutually dependent and variable, not as all-or-nothing states.

chapters.title: Mirage and illusion
chapters.description: Rob turns to the Buddha's descriptions of phenomena as fabricated, mirage-like, and illusory, then adds the middle-way warning against reifying either existence or nonexistence.

chapters.title: What practice means
chapters.description: He returns to Bahiya and asks what it means to train this way in meditation and daily life, ending with a careful question about tendencies toward the 'transcendent' or the world of experience.

title: Thoughts and Images in Meditation
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Thoughts are first treated as a problem to be softened rather than fought: when the mind is buzzing or scattered, the talk keeps returning to the body, to patience, and to a gentler level of attention. From there it moves into more active methods—labeling, questioning beliefs, and noticing how emotion and body sensation feed obsessive loops—before opening into a wider field where thoughts, sounds, and sensations appear and pass like fireworks in space. The later part shifts again, arguing that thought itself is not the issue so much as the views we believe, and then broadens into the skillful use of images, including metta, light imagery, deity practice, and the subtle work of loosening self-view.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of working with thought, emotion, and image in meditation, from calming mental turbulence to questioning the views that sustain suffering.

chapters.title: Opening the topic
chapters.description: Burbea frames the talk as a practical look at thought and image in meditation, while noting that the subject is not simple.

chapters.title: Scattered thought and effort
chapters.description: He describes buzzing, drifting, and restlessness, emphasizing softness, body awareness, patience, and experimentation over forcing the mind.

chapters.title: Labeling and relieving
chapters.description: The talk turns to subtle trains of thought, labeling, and the relief that can come from cutting a thought short and feeling that release in the body.

chapters.title: Obsession and belief
chapters.description: Burbea examines obsessive thinking as something that rests on beliefs, assumptions, and often unspoken views that can be questioned.

chapters.title: Emotion in the body
chapters.description: He offers a close method for working with obsession by finding the emotion in the body, tuning in to vedana, and sometimes meeting anger, hurt, or fear more directly.

chapters.title: Open awareness
chapters.description: The practice widens into an open field where body sensations, sounds, and thoughts arise and disappear like fireworks, loosening identification.

chapters.title: Skillful use of image
chapters.description: Thought and image are then presented as deliberate practices in metta, breath, jhana, cemetery contemplation, light imagery, and deity imagery.

chapters.title: Views and conceiving
chapters.description: In the final movement, Burbea argues that suffering rests not on thought alone but on believed views and conceivings, which shape perception and must eventually be seen through.

title: The Place of Calm
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What is calm for: relief from stress, clearer seeing, or something more unsettling? The talk starts with the familiar case for calming the mind, then steadily widens into samadhi as a whole field of well-being, energy, sensitivity, and unification—not just one-pointed concentration. From there it moves into the body’s contractions, clinging, nourishment, and the possibility that practice may be less about forcing stillness than about loosening entanglement and allowing perception to open in unexpected ways.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on calm and samadhi that moves from concentration and well-being to clinging, bodily tension, and the deeper question of what practice is really for.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: He begins by noting the range of people and practices in the room and frames calm as a topic that may be useful in different ways for different listeners.

chapters.title: Why calm matters
chapters.description: Using the Buddha’s pond image, he describes calm as both relief from stress and a condition that allows clearer seeing.

chapters.title: What samadhi includes
chapters.description: He broadens samadhi beyond one-pointed focus into well-being, harmonization, energization, sensitivity, and subtle refinement of attention.

chapters.title: Nourishing practice
chapters.description: He turns to retreat life as part of practice, emphasizing kindness, gratitude, appreciation, and human connection as supports for samadhi.

chapters.title: Less entanglement
chapters.description: He explains that letting go of entanglement with phenomena allows the mind to gather and settle, and he warns against over-effort that turns practice tight and joyless.

chapters.title: Clinging in the body
chapters.description: He names clinging as bodily contraction and offers the body, especially tension and tightness, as a practical indicator for easing effort and releasing grasping.

chapters.title: Whole-body practice
chapters.description: He describes samadhi as including the whole body and suggests practices that begin from the subtle or energy body, or even make clinging itself the theme.

chapters.title: Reservoir and perception
chapters.description: He presents samadhi as a reservoir of well-being that empowers letting go, then moves into a deeper view in which practice fabricates and gradually unfabricates perception.

chapters.title: Questioning the image
chapters.description: He questions the modern ideal of calm as a final spiritual image, asks what gets lost in that framing, and ends by returning to retreat as a time for deepening rather than settling for a narrow ideal.

title: Stewardship and Intention as Gaia Practices
speaker: James Baraz
description:
(Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) This is part 4 of the series 'Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point' that began 6/14. In this talk, James discusses the last two of the five transformational commitments in Bob Doppelt's book From Me to We:

4. Acknowledge your trustee obligations and take responsibility for the continuation of all life
5. Choose your own destiny
podcast_description: What does it mean to live as a trustee of the earth rather than an owner? The talk moves through an ecological reading of Dharma principles, first building the case for interdependence, karma, and moral responsibility, then applying the idea of trusteeship to land, resources, and social inequality. From there it turns to the question of agency: if what we do matters, how do we move out of denial, discouragement, and rationalization into clear intention and action?
short_summary: An ecological Dharma talk that links interdependence, trusteeship, and free will to the practical choice to take responsibility for life on the planet.

chapters.title: From Me to We
chapters.description: Baraz introduces Bob Doppelt’s framing book and reviews the first three principles that lead toward a shift from individualism to interdependence.

chapters.title: The Law of Trusteeship
chapters.description: He develops the fourth principle, arguing that seeing oneself as part of larger systems naturally gives rise to benevolent responsibility.

chapters.title: Benevolence and No Ownership
chapters.description: Using the Buddha’s post-awakening compassion and Ram Dass’s line about being accountants in the firm, he emphasizes that ownership is ultimately provisional.

chapters.title: Power as Responsibility
chapters.description: A remembered Andreas Vollenweider concert becomes a vivid example of being called to use power wisely and in service of others.

chapters.title: Duties of a Trustee
chapters.description: Baraz lays out the duties of loyalty and prudence, connecting them to fairness, public goods, sustainability, and climate preparation.

chapters.title: Taking the Commitment
chapters.description: He invites listeners to take in the commitment to acknowledge trustee obligations and help sustain life on earth.

chapters.title: Choosing Destiny
chapters.description: The fifth commitment shifts to free will and intention, with the reminder that what we choose in each moment shapes our world.

chapters.title: Stages of Change
chapters.description: Baraz outlines a practical process for moving from disinterest through deliberation into action, maintenance, and a stable trustee identity.

title: Karma and Sila as Gaia Practices
speaker: James Baraz
description:
(Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) This is part 3 of the series 'Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point' that began 6/14. In this talk, James discusses the first three of the five transformational commitments in Bob Doppelt's book From Me to We:

1. See the systems you are part of
2. Be accountable for all the consequences of your actions
3. Abide by society’s most deeply held universal principles of morality and justice
podcast_description: How do karma and morality become practical responses to climate crisis rather than abstractions? The talk moves from the shift from “me” to “we,” through interdependence and cause-and-effect, into a sobering look at ecological damage, then returns to mindfulness as a way to stay awake without being crushed by guilt. It closes by reading three commitments from Bob Doppelt’s From Me to We as a simple framework for seeing systems, taking responsibility, and living by shared principles of justice.
short_summary: A climate-focused Dharma talk that links interdependence, karma, and mindfulness to Bob Doppelt’s three commitments in From Me to We.

chapters.title: Framing the talk
chapters.description: Baraz introduces the series and sets up the question of how Dharma practice meets planetary crisis.

chapters.title: From me to we
chapters.description: He presents Doppelt’s framework and connects the shift from separateness to interdependence with Buddhist not-self.

chapters.title: Seeing the systems
chapters.description: Baraz develops the first commitment, showing how each person is nested in many overlapping systems.

chapters.title: Karma and accountability
chapters.description: He turns to cause and effect, arguing that actions matter even when consequences are delayed or hard to see.

chapters.title: The Buddha’s example
chapters.description: Using the story of the Buddha’s hesitation to teach, he shows how compassion responds to people’s conditioning.

chapters.title: Climate as consequence
chapters.description: He brings in John Seed and a series of climate examples to show how collective habits shape the Earth over time.

chapters.title: Hidden impacts
chapters.description: A Buddha discourse on impurities becomes a way to question older assumptions about how harm disappears into large systems.

chapters.title: Mindfulness over guilt
chapters.description: Baraz asks how to stay awake to ecological responsibility without becoming immobilized or self-blaming.

chapters.title: Three commitments
chapters.description: He closes by reading the first three commitments aloud and reframing them as a joyful practice of interdependence, accountability, and justice.

title: Seeing the System You Are Part Of
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) Sharing a positive vision for the future from Ernest Callenbach’s "Epistle to Ecotopians". We then begin to explore the first of five Dharma principles using systems and sustainability expert Bob Doppelt’s book From Me to We. The ignorance of these principles is what perpetuates the problem and the understanding of them is the key to changing our consciousness and providing a path toward healing the planet. This is part 2 of the series 'Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point' that began June 14.
podcast_description: How do you stay clear and useful when the world feels like it is tipping toward collapse? The talk begins with a turn toward hope, drawing on Ernest Callenbach’s final ecological essay, then moves into a practical inquiry into five commitments from Bob Doppelt’s From Me to We. From there it narrows to the first commitment—seeing the systems you are part of—through images of interdependence, non-self, and the way a person is always already woven into larger natural and social processes.
short_summary: A talk on ecological crisis, hope, and the Dharma move from individualism toward seeing oneself as part of larger living systems.

chapters.title: Framing the Tipping Point
chapters.description: James opens by naming the cultural moment as a race between fear and consciousness and sets the talk within a larger tipping point.

chapters.title: Callenbach’s Final Vision
chapters.description: He reads from Ernest Callenbach’s Epistle to the Ecotopians as a hopeful model for meeting collapse with courage, mutual support, and practical wisdom.

chapters.title: Hope and Mutual Support
chapters.description: The reading highlights hope, cooperation, and shared effort as survival qualities, while James reflects on holding a positive vision without denial.

chapters.title: Hard Times, Regeneration
chapters.description: He continues through the essay’s emphasis on practical skills, organizing together, and learning to live with contradiction, decay, and renewal.

chapters.title: From Me to We
chapters.description: James introduces Bob Doppelt’s book and explains how its five commitments translate ecological concern into a Dharma-based shift in consciousness.

chapters.title: Five Commitments
chapters.description: He outlines the five commitments—seeing systems, accountability, morality, trusteeship, and intention—then focuses on the first as a doorway into practice.

chapters.title: Interbeing in the Body
chapters.description: Using Thich Nhat Hanh and Lewis Thomas, he closes by showing how a person is already an ecosystem, inseparable from the wider web of life.

title: Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point: Concern for the Planet as Dharma Practice
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) This is the first of a four-part series inspired by the Spring 2012 issue of Inquiring Mind, entitled 'Earth Now.' In addition to reading this issue, especially the interview with Joanna Macy: Woman on the Edge of Time, James also suggested these readings: Ernest Callenbach’s essay "Epistle to Ecotopians" and Bob Doppelt's book From Me to We: The Five Transformational Commitments Required to Rescue the Planet, Your Organization, and Your Life. The series continues on 6/28, 7/5, and 7/19.
podcast_description: Climate collapse, in this talk, is not treated as an abstract issue but as a moment that tests practice: do we fall into apathy, panic, or a steadier kind of response? The speaker begins with a front-page warning about a tipping point in global warming, then moves through his own swings between urgency and overwhelm before turning to concrete examples from the Dharma world, including questions about travel, sustainability, and institutional responsibility. He draws on Bhikkhu Bodhi’s warning about greed, arrogance, and ignorance, and on Joanna Macy’s sense of deep time, before leading a short guided exercise that asks listeners to imagine speaking from 75 years in the future.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of climate grief and responsibility that moves from urgency and overwhelm into deep-time reflection and a future-self practice.

chapters.title: The climate warning
chapters.description: James opens with a front-page climate article and the recurring feeling that the planet is nearing a tipping point.

chapters.title: From outrage to overwhelm
chapters.description: He speaks candidly about his own shift from urgency and crusade to discouragement, and the mixed responses climate news can trigger.

chapters.title: Systems change and Dharma
chapters.description: A book by Bob Doppelt helps him frame ecological response as personal, organizational, and social change rooted in Dharma principles.

chapters.title: Institutions and responsibility
chapters.description: He tells a story about Dharma teachers weighing the carbon cost of travel and asking what sustainability requires of their communities.

chapters.title: Bhikkhu Bodhi at the cliff edge
chapters.description: James summarizes Bhikkhu Bodhi’s analysis of greed, arrogance, and ignorance, along with the need for immediate, long-term, and systemic action.

chapters.title: Joanna Macy and deep time
chapters.description: He turns to Joanna Macy’s reflections on hurry, consumption, and the industrial growth society, emphasizing a longer view of time.

chapters.title: Seventh-generation ethics
chapters.description: The talk widens into intergenerational responsibility, with the Iroquois seventh-generation ethic and Macy’s language of future beings.

chapters.title: Future self practice
chapters.description: James leads a guided contemplation from the standpoint of a being 75 years in the future, then invites short triad sharing and a quiet close.

title: This Matter of Emptiness
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if the main problem is not the experience itself, but the way mind keeps making it seem solid? The talk begins by contrasting the immediate work of meeting experience in meditation with a larger question: whether practice stops at being with what is, or goes on to see how self and world are constructed. From there it moves through Nagarjuna, the Buddha’s language of emptiness, and a vivid shadow play of a wolf turning into a bunny rabbit to show how fear, relief, and even a sense of basic reality can all be fabricated. The closing movement returns to practice as training in ways of looking that loosen realism and open the possibility of groundlessness without collapsing into nihilism.
short_summary: A talk on emptiness that moves from mindfulness as simple presence to the deeper insight that self and world are constructed, illusory, and dependently arisen.

chapters.title: Footstep and map
chapters.description: Burbea begins by distinguishing the immediate work of practice from the larger map, and explains why the talk will focus on the deeper dimensions of the path.

chapters.title: Ultimate truth
chapters.description: He introduces Nagarjuna and frames awakening as something that depends on realizing ultimate truth, not only settling into present-moment experience.

chapters.title: Self and all things
chapters.description: The talk widens from selflessness to the emptiness of all phenomena, with care around levels of teaching and the limits of a purely realist reading.

chapters.title: How experience is built
chapters.description: Burbea develops the practical insight that self and object are fabricated by reactivity, perspective, and the mind’s way of looking.

chapters.title: Wolf and rabbit
chapters.description: A visual demonstration with a wolf shadow and a bunny rabbit shows how fear, relief, and even mindfulness can miss the construction process itself.

chapters.title: Foam, mirage, magic
chapters.description: He turns to a sutta of similes—foam, bubble, mirage, plantain tree, magic show—to show that the aggregates and consciousness are empty of essence.

chapters.title: Realism and liberation
chapters.description: The talk names realism as the root delusion and reinterprets liberation as seeing the emptiness and dependent arising of things as they are.

chapters.title: Practice as seeing
chapters.description: In the closing movement, Burbea describes meditation as training ways of looking, so that fabrication loosens and a freer, less fixed relationship to experience becomes possible.

title: The Beauty of Desire (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: What if desire is not mainly a problem to suppress, but a current to understand? The talk moves from a brief return to letting go and the suffering of clinging, into a series of concrete case studies where asking “what am I really wanting?” shifts anger, craving, worry, and resistance into energy, tenderness, joy, or a wider sense of self. Along the way, the speaker questions simple spiritual assumptions about desire, draws on Buddhist and a few mystical references, and keeps returning to the possibility that the treasure is in the movement of desire itself rather than its object.
short_summary: A close inquiry into desire that starts with letting go and then opens into case studies where wanting becomes a source of energy, insight, and spaciousness.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea sets up the talk as a continuation of last week’s exploration and says he wants to open questions rather than land on conclusions.

chapters.title: Letting go of desire
chapters.description: He outlines a classical Buddhist practice of noticing desire and clinging, then repeatedly letting it go to see suffering, selfing, and solidity diminish together.

chapters.title: A different approach
chapters.description: He introduces a second method, framed as a research inquiry into consciousness, that will use case studies to explore desire more experimentally.

chapters.title: The bank story
